Remaining handwritten config surfaces are explicitly justified as prose/presentation rather than semantic source of truth. 4. Follow-up work becomes narrower because the contract now owns more of the maintained semantics.
close_reason: Completed: Home Manager defaults and generated config surfaces now derive from the canonical config contract, the pack catalog default table is deleted from the module, and the shipped main template remains mechanically defended by the config-surface contract validator.

title: Desktop relaunch rebuild must stay visible instead of happening invisibly
description: After the runtime-identity hash churn fix, desktop relaunch no longer always forces stale rebuilds, but when a rebuild is genuinely needed it can still happen before the terminal window becomes visible again. Track the remaining desktop-entry UX regression so rebuild/setup work happens in the visible Yazelix window rather than invisibly behind the launcher.
close_reason: Completed

title: Stop exporting source-checkout runtime identity through ambient YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR
description: Delete the source-shell export of YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR from devenv enterShell so repo-loaded code and generated wrappers stop inheriting the installed runtime by accident. Keep explicit runtime env only where cross-process handoff genuinely needs it, and defend the source-checkout launch path against a conflicting installed-runtime env. Split shellHook runtime identity so repo-local shells keep DEVENV_ROOT as the live source surface while internal setup and config-state bookkeeping can still use an explicit runtime root when needed.
close_reason: Completed: repo-local devenv enterShell no longer exports ambient YAZELIX_RUNTIME_DIR or YAZELIX_DIR; repo shells now keep DEVENV_ROOT as the live checkout identity while internal shellHook setup still passes an explicit runtime root only where needed. Managed Nushell config is defended through IN_YAZELIX_SHELL without requiring a fake runtime-root export.

title: Audit and classify yzx command families by backend coupling
description: Audit the exported `yzx` command surface from `core/yazelix.nu` and `yzx/*.nu` and classify each command family by backend coupling. The goal is not a flat "lite or not" list, but an honest map of which surfaces are backend-required control-plane commands, which are backend-agnostic workspace/config UX, which are runtime-owned distribution/install surfaces, and which are genuinely mixed seams that need refactoring before `Yazelix Core` decisions are honest. Cover canonical families such as env/run/refresh, launch/restart, doctor/status, desktop/update, menu/edit/config/import/open, popup/cwd/reveal, packs/gc, and the purely informational commands.
design:
Treat this as a command-family audit grounded in the actual exported defs, not in docs/help text. Use `core/yazelix.nu` plus `yzx/*.nu` as the source of truth, and fold aliases into canonical families instead of listing every leaf command separately. For each family, record: the canonical commands it covers, its primary owner bucket, why it belongs there, the current code evidence for that coupling, and whether a future `Yazelix Core` edition would plausibly keep it unchanged, narrow it, or drop it.

Do not force every command into a fake binary choice. The audit should allow a fourth subtype for backend-agnostic but runtime-owned/distribution surfaces so commands like `yzx desktop launch` or `yzx update runtime` do not get mislabeled as full environment-backend requirements. Be explicit about mixed seams instead of pretending the current architecture is already clean.

The code pass already suggests these likely families to verify:
- Backend-required control plane: `yzx env`, `yzx run`, `yzx refresh`, probably `yzx packs`, and probably `yzx gc`
- Backend-agnostic workspace/config UX: `yzx cwd`, `yzx reveal`, `yzx popup`, `yzx config`, `yzx edit`, `yzx import`, `yzx open`, `yzx keys`, `yzx tutor`, `yzx screen`, `yzx why`, `yzx sponsor`, `yzx whats_new`
- Runtime-owned distribution/install surfaces: `yzx desktop *`, `yzx update runtime`, possibly parts of `yzx update nix`
- Mixed/refactor-needed seams: `yzx launch`, `yzx restart`, `yzx status`, `yzx doctor`, and `yzx menu`

The output should leave behind a small ranked shortlist of the genuinely mixed command families that deserve refactoring next, rather than just a spreadsheet of labels.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete command-family matrix covering the exported `yzx` surface from `core/yazelix.nu` and `yzx/*.nu`, with aliases folded into canonical families. Each family is classified as one of: backend-required, backend-agnostic, runtime-owned/distribution, or mixed/refactor-needed, with a short reason grounded in the current code. The audit explicitly identifies the most important mixed seams to refactor next instead of pretending every command already fits cleanly. The result is precise enough to guide `yazelix-qv8c`, `yazelix-qow`, and later backend-adapter/Rust work without having to re-argue the command surface family by family.
notes:
Initial code-evidence pass:
- The exported surface is defined in `nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu` plus `nushell/scripts/yzx/*.nu`.
- The main mixed seam is not just `yzx launch`; it is the cluster of `launch`, `restart`, `status`, `doctor`, and `menu`, where backend state, runtime integrity, install/distribution surfaces, and workspace UX still overlap.
- `yzx desktop *` and `yzx update runtime` look different from `yzx env`/`run`/`refresh`: they are runtime/distribution ownership surfaces, not generic workspace UX, but they also do not need the full environment-control semantics of the current backend.
close_reason: Completed: added the command-family backend-coupling audit spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Extract a backend adapter boundary around env, run, refresh, and launch bootstrap
description: Refactor the command/bootstrap layer so the current devenv integration sits behind a clearer backend adapter boundary instead of bleeding directly into user-facing command semantics. Focus on the shared paths now rooted in environment_bootstrap.nu and the yzx env/run/refresh/launch surface. The immediate goal is not to support a second backend in main; it is to make current devenv-specific assumptions explicit, shrink accidental coupling, and leave behind a clearer seam for later experiments or a Core edition.
design: Treat this as main-branch architecture cleanup, not speculative abstraction. The important move is to identify what the command layer expects from the environment backend and to keep the rest of the command semantics outside that adapter. Likely hot spots include get_devenv_base_command, run_in_devenv_shell_command, resolve_preferred_devenv_path, and the launch/bootstrap state that currently assumes devenv-shaped profiles. Delete direct ad hoc devenv assumptions where possible, but do not build a pretend generic backend framework unless the resulting seam is materially clearer. This should complement ongoing env-var cleanup in yazelix-2ex.2.1 rather than duplicating it.
acceptance_criteria: The env/run/refresh/launch bootstrap path has a clearer backend boundary, with fewer direct devenv-specific assumptions bleeding into user-facing command semantics. The repo shape is simpler to reason about than before, not more abstract. Future backend experiments or a Core edition would have a concrete seam to target instead of rediscovering environment_bootstrap.nu as a monolith.
notes:
2026-04-08 streamlining alignment:
- This remains a high-value architecture seam, but it should not absorb simpler delete-first cuts that can land independently inside `common.nu`, `doctor.nu`, or `yzx/dev.nu`.
- Use this bead for the shared backend boundary only: env/run/refresh/launch bootstrap, backend command resolution, and profile-shaped state assumptions.
- As the refactor lane sharpens, treat `common.nu`, `environment_bootstrap.nu`, `launch_state.nu`, and the relevant command entrypoints as the core input set.

2026-04-09 backend adapter extraction progress:
- Moved the shared devenv-backed command/build/reuse functions into `utils/devenv_backend.nu` and repointed the production env/run/refresh/launch entrypoints at that narrower owner.
- Deleted the temporary `environment_bootstrap.nu` re-export bridge so environment preparation now owns only prep/state checks while backend execution helpers live only under the backend adapter.
- Tightened the refresh regressions to model the real split: `environment_bootstrap.nu` now stubs only `prepare_environment`, while backend-specific tests source `devenv_backend.nu` directly.
- Deleted the duplicate `devenv build shell` runner from `yzx/refresh.nu`; refresh and rebuild now share `run_devenv_build_shell` in the backend adapter instead of maintaining separate execution logic, unfree handling, and profile-resolution plumbing.
- Centralized the standard reuse/skip-refresh/requested-rebuild classification and guidance in `devenv_backend.nu`, so env/startup/launch/restart stop hand-rolling the same stale-environment branch logic and user messaging.
- Tightened the workspace/startup fixtures to mirror the real split as well: test stubs now separate `environment_bootstrap.nu` prep ownership from `devenv_backend.nu` execution ownership instead of saving the same fake module to both paths.

2026-04-09 completion:
- Finished the backend adapter boundary by moving the remaining shared runtime-entry context and recorded-profile selection rules behind `devenv_backend.nu` and `launch_state.nu`.
- `env`, `run`, `launch`, and `start_yazelix` now consume the shared adapter context instead of rebuilding the same backend-state logic locally.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement a shared runtime contract checker and reuse it across launch and doctor
description: Implement a small shared runtime contract checker for the overlap between launch-preflight and `yzx doctor`, instead of scattering the same dependency reasoning across launch, startup, and doctor-specific helpers. The checker should stay lightweight enough for normal entrypoint use, return structured results, and give both launch and doctor one authoritative story for the checks they truly share. This is not a mega-doctor rewrite: the first slice should focus only on the runtime blockers and dependency conditions already called out by `yazelix-j4qv`.
design: Treat this as a narrow shared-evaluation layer plus separate presentation layers. The checker should probably live in a small runtime-validation module and return structured records with fields such as check id, status, severity, owner surface, and recovery hint. Ground the first slice directly in the current overlap seams instead of inventing a giant abstraction: `start_yazelix.nu` currently owns startup working-directory and generated-layout/runtime-script checks, `yzx/launch.nu` owns runtime launch-script checks before re-entry, `core/launch_yazelix.nu` owns new-window working-directory and terminal-availability checks, and `doctor.nu` owns richer rendering/reporting. The first implementation slice should cover only the checks that both launch and doctor can honestly share today: working-directory validity, required runtime entrypoint scripts, required generated layout presence, and configured terminal availability for new-window launch. Launch should consume only hard blockers from that result set and fail fast. `yzx doctor` should be able to render the same result set more fully, but doctor-only and install-only surfaces should remain separate for now. Do not try to absorb config-migration preflight, shell-hook freshness, desktop-entry freshness, version drift, Helix runtime conflicts, plugin/session-local health, or heavy install-smoke invariants into the first shared checker just because they are all “health” concerns. This bead should also standardize the result vocabulary enough that later Rust work (`yazelix-kt5.3`) has a clean target without forcing a daemon or giant abstraction now.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has one shared runtime contract checker used by both launch-facing code and `yzx doctor` for the overlapping runtime dependency checks covered by `yazelix-j4qv`. The checker returns structured results rather than only ad hoc error strings. The first shared slice covers at least: missing or invalid working directory, missing runtime entrypoint scripts, missing generated layout required for startup, and configured terminal availability for new-window launch. Startup and launch entrypoints consume the shared checker instead of keeping those checks split across `start_yazelix.nu`, `yzx/launch.nu`, and `core/launch_yazelix.nu`, while `yzx doctor` can surface the same shared results without reimplementing their core reasoning separately. Doctor-only diagnostics such as shell-hook freshness, desktop-entry freshness, version drift, Helix runtime conflicts, plugin health, and heavy install/package checks remain outside the first shared checker unless they clearly fit the same runtime contract result model. The implementation stays lightweight enough for normal launch use and does not regress startup into a slow audit path.
close_reason: Implemented shared runtime contract checker and reused it across launch and doctor

title: Define the runtime dependency contract and launch preflight checker scope
description:
Specify the runtime dependency contract that normal user-facing entrypoints actually rely on, and define the scope of a preflight checker that should validate that contract before launch. Cover required versus optional dependencies, config-conditioned requirements, runtime assets versus host tools, and the boundary between lightweight launch-preflight checks and heavier doctor or install-smoke flows.

The contract should explain at least:
- always-required launch/startup prerequisites
- config-conditioned requirements such as terminal availability
- the difference between runtime-owned assets and host/external tools
- what launch should check quickly
- what doctor may check more deeply
- what only install/package validation should defend
design:
Treat this as a contract-first task, but not as the generic backend contract. Start from the current launch, startup, doctor, and install-smoke behavior and write down what the runtime layer is actually promising when a user runs Yazelix.

Ground it in the current code seams:
- `nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/terminal_launcher.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/doctor.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/dev/validate_installed_runtime_contract.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/dev/validate_flake_install.nu`

Important boundary: config migration preflight remains adjacent but separate. Launch should still run it, but stale-config/migration semantics are not the same thing as runtime dependency checking.

This bead should complement the backend contract from `yazelix-6lkw`, not reopen it. The output should also stay honest for current full Yazelix while being reusable for a future Core discussion.
acceptance_criteria:
There is an explicit runtime dependency contract for user-facing entrypoints, with clear required, optional, and config-conditioned classifications.

The contract clearly separates:
- lightweight launch-preflight checks
- richer `yzx doctor` diagnostics
- heavier install/package validation

It is concrete enough to explain at least these current cases:
- missing or invalid working directory as an immediate launch blocker
- missing runtime script or generated layout as an immediate startup/runtime blocker
- unavailable configured terminal as a config-conditioned launch blocker for new-window launches
- stale shell hooks, desktop entries, install artifacts, version drift, and deeper runtime health as doctor-first or install-validation concerns rather than mandatory launch-preflight checks

A later bead should be able to implement one shared runtime checker from this contract without rediscovering the scope ad hoc.
close_reason: Runtime dependency/preflight contract written, issue #532 synced, launch preflight tightened for runtime scripts/layouts, and doctor-side desktop-entry freshness diagnostic defended.

title: Define the runtime dependency contract and launch preflight checker scope
description: Specify the runtime dependency contract that normal user-facing entrypoints such as yzx launch actually rely on, and define the scope of a preflight checker that should validate that contract before launch. Cover required versus optional dependencies, config-conditioned requirements, runtime assets versus host tools, and the boundary between lightweight preflight checks and heavier doctor flows. The result should be explicit enough that launch, doctor, install smoke, and any future Core edition can share one dependency story instead of ad hoc checks.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-j4qv created during parallel bead creation.

title: Separate environment-backend concerns from workspace UX and prepare a future Yazelix Core edition
description: Drive the next modularity pass around a clearer separation between Yazelix's environment backend concerns and its workspace/runtime UX layer. The immediate goal is to improve main while keeping devenv: define backend-facing contracts, add a shared runtime preflight checker, reduce hardcoded backend assumptions in the command surface, and classify what would remain viable in a future no-package-management edition. The longer-term motivation is to make a future Yazelix Core edition possible without turning main into a confused multi-backend product.
design: Build on the already-closed modularity framing work in docs/architecture_map.md and docs/workspace_session_contract.md instead of reopening subsystem-boundary debate from scratch. This epic is about making main cleaner while still using devenv: clarify backend-facing contracts, reduce ad hoc backend coupling in the command/bootstrap layer, and make a future Core edition a consequence of better boundaries rather than a speculative fork. Adjacent existing beads such as yazelix-2ex.2.1, yazelix-b16x.1, and yazelix-uwz5 should be treated as parallel cleanup inputs, not displaced by this epic.
acceptance_criteria: There is a coherent bead sequence that sharpens the environment-backend boundary, introduces a shared runtime/preflight dependency story, classifies the current CLI surface by backend reliance, and leaves behind an evidence-based recommendation for or against a future Yazelix Core edition. Main gets cleaner without promising multi-backend support in place.
close_reason: Completed as epic housekeeping: the concrete backend-boundary, runtime-contract, command-coupling, cross-language ownership, and Core-edition decision beads are all closed, and the remaining conclusions now live in durable specs/docs rather than open epic planning state.

title: Audit the yzx command surface into backend-required versus backend-agnostic commands
description: Inventory the yzx command surface and classify which commands fundamentally require a managed environment backend and which commands belong to the backend-agnostic UX/runtime layer. This should cover launch, edit, doctor, desktop install, config migration, update flows, env/run/refresh, and any commands that only read or generate Yazelix-owned state. The result should make it much easier to reason about current product boundaries and a future Core edition without guessing command by command.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-d4pw created during parallel bead creation.

title: Implement a shared runtime contract checker and reuse it across launch and doctor
description: Implement a shared checker for the runtime dependency contract instead of scattering availability probes and stale assumptions across launch, doctor, and one-off helpers. The checker should be lightweight enough for normal preflight use, classify failures clearly, and expose the same underlying reasoning to yzx doctor. Avoid turning launch into a giant slow audit; the value is one authoritative dependency story with clear required/optional classifications and actionable errors.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-hv2m created during parallel bead creation.

title: Extract a backend adapter boundary around env, run, refresh, and launch bootstrap
description: Refactor the command/bootstrap layer so the current devenv integration sits behind a clearer backend adapter boundary instead of bleeding directly into user-facing command semantics. Focus on the shared paths now rooted in environment_bootstrap.nu and the yzx env/run/refresh/launch surface. The immediate goal is not to support a second backend in main; it is to make current devenv-specific assumptions explicit, shrink accidental coupling, and leave behind a clearer seam for later experiments or a Core edition.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-m5rk created during parallel bead creation.

title: Define the Yazelix backend contract before evaluating alternative runtime backends
description:
Define the Yazelix backend capability contract before doing deeper backend experiments. The point is to stop using “backend” as a vague synonym for “env manager” and instead write down the concrete capabilities Yazelix currently needs from its runtime/environment layer.

This contract should classify at least these capability families:
- runtime materialization of the shipped/default Yazelix tool stack
- stable install/update ownership and user-facing entrypoint identity
- env/run/shell entrypoints for commands like `yzx env`, `yzx run`, and launch re-entry
- cached profile or reusable activation semantics, including refresh/invalidation rules
- runtime-owned binary and asset resolution, not just generic PATH lookup
- generated runtime/state ownership and how it relates to the backend
- platform/package availability expectations and which current conveniences are actually optional

The goal is not to bless a new backend yet. The goal is to produce a contract concrete enough to evaluate current full Yazelix and a future Nix-only `Yazelix Core` flake candidate without redefining the criteria midstream. Later optional backend experiments should be able to reuse the same contract instead of shaping it.
design:
Treat this as contract/spec-first work, not implementation work. Build directly on `docs/architecture_map.md` plus the existing runtime/install/config contracts: `runtime_root_contract.md`, `flake_interface_contract.md`, `one_command_install_ux.md`, and `config_surface_and_launch_profile_contract.md`.

Ground the contract in the real code seams that currently depend on the backend layer:
- `nushell/scripts/utils/devenv_cli.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/launch_state.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/utils/config_state.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/run.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu`
- `nushell/scripts/yzx/refresh.nu`

The written contract should produce:
1. a capability matrix with at least these columns:
   - full Yazelix today
   - possible Nix-only Core backend
2. a required vs optional vs explicitly-out-of-scope classification for each capability bucket
3. a clear boundary between backend capability requirements and launch-time dependency/preflight checks
4. a clear boundary between backend capability requirements and the eventual product/support boundary for `Yazelix Core`

Optional later backend experiments, such as the Pixi lane, should be evaluated against this contract after it exists instead of influencing the contract definition itself.

Important constraint: this bead is not where launch-time “is Helix/Zellij/Ghostty installed?” checks are defined. That belongs to `yazelix-j4qv`. This bead is about what a backend must make possible for Yazelix as a product at all.
acceptance_criteria:
There is a written backend contract for Yazelix that groups the real backend surface into explicit capability buckets instead of an abstract “backend” label. Each bucket is marked as required, optional, or out of scope for backend evaluation.

The contract is concrete enough to evaluate at least:
- current `devenv`-backed full Yazelix
- a future Nix-only, no-`devenv`, opinionated flake candidate for `Yazelix Core`

The contract explicitly covers the current hard seams visible in code today:
- runtime-owned CLI/tool resolution
- install/update ownership and stable entrypoint identity
- env/run/rebuild/re-entry entrypoints
- cached profile reuse and invalidation semantics
- relationship between backend materialization and generated runtime/state artifacts

The contract also explicitly states what it does *not* own:
- user-facing launch preflight / dependency checker behavior (`yazelix-j4qv`)
- the final `Yazelix Core` product boundary (`yazelix-qv8c`)
- implementation of a new backend

A later bead should be able to use this contract to evaluate candidate backends without moving the goalposts or rediscovering requirements from scattered code.
notes:
Evidence already points to a broader backend surface than “can spawn a shell”:
- `devenv_cli.nu` and `environment_bootstrap.nu` show that Yazelix currently expects preferred runtime-owned CLI resolution plus rebuild/re-entry control.
- `config_state.nu` and `launch_state.nu` show that cached profile reuse and invalidation are part of the backend story, not just a UX optimization.
- `yzx env`, `yzx run`, `yzx launch`, and `yzx refresh` show that the backend currently owns both activation and rebuild semantics.
- `flake_interface_contract.md`, `one_command_install_ux.md`, and `flake.nix` show that install/update identity and stable runtime pointers are part of the backend boundary too.

The likely delete-first outcome is that some things people casually bundle into “backend” will turn out to be:
- launch preflight concerns
- product-boundary concerns
- or optional conveniences rather than hard backend requirements.
close_reason: Backend capability contract written, linked into architecture docs, and aligned with issue #538.

title: Evaluate reusable fuzzy target selection across the yzx CLI
description: Analyze the new picker/query pattern introduced for yzx edit and decide where else it should be reused across the CLI. Scope: inventory candidate commands, compare command-surface clarity vs subcommand sprawl, identify shared implementation opportunities, and recommend where fuzzy disambiguation improves UX without making scripting or discoverability worse.
design: Treat this as a command-surface audit, not as a blanket mandate to add fuzzy selection everywhere. The point is to identify where a small target catalog meaningfully simplifies the CLI and where it would instead add ambiguity or make automation worse. The output should include candidate commands, a recommendation on whether interactive selection, fuzzy query matching, exact aliases, or plain subcommands fit best, and whether the current yzx edit implementation should be extracted into a reusable helper at all.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for which yzx commands should reuse the new target-selection pattern, which should not, and why. Any proposed reuse points are grounded in command-surface clarity and scripting impact rather than novelty. If a shared implementation seam exists, it is identified explicitly; if not, the bead says so instead of forcing abstraction.
close_reason: Completed: keep fuzzy target selection local to yzx edit and the command palette. Do not generalize it across the CLI today. yzx import, yzx keys, and yzx tutor read more clearly as explicit subcommands, and workspace commands like yzx cwd should keep plain path/query arguments rather than fuzzy target catalogs. There is no strong reusable implementation seam yet beyond the existing local edit target catalog.

title: Extend yzx edit to Yazi keymap and init.lua surfaces
description: Follow-up to the picker-based yzx edit surface. Add explicit support for the managed Yazi sub-surfaces beyond yazi.toml: keymap.toml and init.lua under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/. Keep the same canonical managed-surface policy and avoid exposing host-owned files.
design: Treat this as a narrow continuation of the current yzx edit surface, not as a generic file browser. Keep the supported targets limited to Yazelix-owned Yazi surfaces that are already part of the managed config contract. Reuse the existing target-selection machinery instead of adding one-off path logic, and keep the user-facing names explicit enough that scripting and printed-path flows remain predictable.
acceptance_criteria: yzx edit can target the managed Yazi keymap and init.lua surfaces in the same way it already targets config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml. Printed-path and interactive selection flows remain coherent, and the command still avoids exposing host-owned files outside Yazelix-managed paths.
notes:
2026-04-09 completion:
- Extended `yzx edit` to cover the managed Yazi `keymap.toml` and `init.lua` surfaces under `user_configs/yazi/`.
- Updated the printed-path regression and CLI docs so the new surfaces are part of the supported managed edit surface.
close_reason: Completed

title: Expand yzx edit to additional managed surfaces
description: Replace the current narrow yzx edit surface with a picker or fuzzy-query flow for canonical Yazelix-managed config surfaces. Scope for this bead: support config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml; allow yzx edit with no args to present choices, and yzx edit QUERY to filter or disambiguate managed targets. Keep host shell rc files out of scope. Follow-up bead will cover extra Yazi sub-surfaces like keymap and init.lua.
close_reason: Completed: yzx edit now supports picker or fuzzy-query selection for config, packs, helix, zellij, and yazi.toml managed surfaces.

title: Restore Starship Yazi plugin prompt integration
description: GitHub issue #529 reports that the Starship Yazi plugin has regressed and Yazi is showing the normal prompt instead of the plugin-driven one. Investigate the current Yazi shell/prompt wiring, identify where the plugin path stopped taking effect, and restore the intended prompt integration without depending on host-local prompt setup.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Continue centralizing Yazelix declarative config semantics onto the canonical contract
description: Yazelix already has a canonical machine-readable config contract in config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml, but some real consumers still duplicate semantics across the default template, Home Manager, parser-adjacent logic, schema/diagnostics-adjacent logic, and rebuild-sensitive ownership sites. Treat this as a delete-first centralization bead: continue moving real consumers onto the canonical contract, delete duplicated semantic ownership that is no longer justified, and make the remaining duplication explicit where it must stay. Build on the already-completed 0oj1 centralization work instead of reopening the problem from scratch.
design: Treat this as a delete-first follow-up to the already completed 0oj1 centralization work, not as a fresh architecture spike. The canonical in-repo TOML contract already exists and several consumers already moved onto it; the remaining job is to identify which real semantic ownership sites still duplicate that contract, migrate at least one meaningful remaining consumer, and delete semantic duplication that is no longer justified. Keep the protocol question pragmatic: only adopt an external protocol if it clearly beats the current in-repo TOML contract for Yazelix's actual consumers and maintenance burden. Related open beads in this lane include b16x.1 for doctor freshness reporting and 53zx.* for staged migration semantics across managed surfaces; this bead should complement those, not reopen their contracts.
acceptance_criteria: The bead records a clear statement of which config-semantic ownership sites are still duplicated after the completed 0oj1 work. At least one meaningful remaining consumer is migrated further onto the canonical contract, reducing real semantic duplication instead of adding abstraction. The design makes an explicit keep-vs-adopt decision on the in-repo TOML contract versus any external protocol, with concrete reasoning tied to Yazelix's real consumers. The resulting repo shape is simpler than before: fewer duplicated semantic ownership sites, not more generation or indirection.
notes: Imported from GitHub issue #516. This should be treated as an umbrella cleanup/centralization bead for the remaining live work after the closed 0oj1.* sequence, not as evidence that the earlier centralization work failed. Likely remaining hotspots to inspect first: home_manager/module.nix, yazelix_default.toml, and any residual hand-maintained semantic lists that still sit outside config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml.
close_reason: Completed: main-config semantics are now materially more centralized on config_metadata/main_config_contract.toml. home_manager/module.nix already derives defaults/types from the contract, and config_parser.nu now iterates the contract instead of hand-upserting every field. Remaining duplication is intentionally limited to human-facing prose and a few local schema allowances rather than another maintained semantic field table.

title: Split CI into fast and integration lanes with path-gated heavy install validation
description: Restructure GitHub Actions so strong always-on branch protection remains in place while the current single serial CI job stops paying for heavy install/runtime validation on every push; the main target is to stop validate_flake_install.nu from dominating Beads/docs and other unrelated changes without losing real install/runtime/packaging/Home Manager coverage.
design: Current state: .github/workflows/ci.yml is one serial validate job; cheap validators finish quickly, validate_default_test_budget is about 60s, Check Home Manager flake is about 15-20s, and validate_flake_install.nu dominates runtime with roughly 4m cold-success paths and >10m hangs/cancels. Recommended architecture: keep an always-on fast required lane for cheap branch-protection checks; move heavy install/runtime coverage into a separate integration lane gated internally by changed-file detection rather than workflow-level paths filters; keep nix flake check ./home_manager --no-build in fast unless later timing proves otherwise; preserve visibility so skipped integration work concludes cleanly as not-needed instead of disappearing into pending required-check state. Likely heavy-lane file patterns: flake.nix, shells/posix/install_yazelix.sh.in, yazelix_runtime_package.nix, mk_runtime_tree.nix, locked_devenv_package.nix, home_manager/**, nushell/scripts/setup/**, nushell/scripts/core/**, nushell/scripts/utils/**, and configs/** when they affect installed/generated runtime content.
acceptance_criteria: 1. CI no longer consists of one fully serial validate job that always runs the heavy install smoke on every push. 2. There is an always-on fast lane preserving current cheap branch-protection checks. 3. Heavy flake-install/runtime integration validation runs only when relevant install/runtime/packaging/Home Manager files change, or under another explicitly documented narrow trigger. 4. The design avoids the GitHub Actions trap where skipped required workflows remain pending and block merges. 5. Heavy-lane skip decisions are explicit and observable in CI output. 6. Workflow comments or nearby docs explain which checks belong to fast vs integration and why. 7. Routine CI runtime and Actions spend are materially reduced for non-runtime changes.
notes: Relevant repo surfaces: .github/workflows/ci.yml, nushell/scripts/dev/validate_flake_install.nu, nushell/scripts/dev/validate_default_test_budget.nu, home_manager/flake.nix. External references informing the design: GitHub Docs on skipped-but-required checks staying pending and on passing job outputs between jobs, which supports a detector-job to gated-integration-job design. Observed timings from recent runs: default-suite runtime budget about 60s, Home Manager flake about 15-20s, flake install smoke 4m timeout-triggered failure on cold runner after the fail-fast patch and >10m when previously hanging/cancelled.
close_reason: Implemented: split CI into fast and integration lanes with explicit internal change gating for flake install smoke

title: Manually validate Yazelix installation and startup through Home Manager for the first time
description: Run a real first-time Home Manager install of Yazelix on a clean user setup, verify runtime/current ownership, desktop entry, yzx shim, launch flow, and managed config generation, then record any gaps between the documented Home Manager path and actual user experience.
design: Treat this as a real user-path validation bead, not as another flake-check proxy. The important thing is to exercise the documented Home Manager install story from a clean user perspective and record where setup, runtime ownership, generated configs, desktop entry integration, or launch behavior diverge from the intended contract. Keep the validation focused on the canonical Home Manager path rather than maintainer-only local shortcuts.
acceptance_criteria: There is a manual validation record for the Home Manager install/startup path covering first-time installation, runtime/current ownership, yzx wrapper behavior, generated config presence, desktop entry path, and successful launch. Any gaps found are either fixed or split into explicit follow-up beads, leaving the Home Manager story better defined than before.
notes:
Validation on 2026-04-08:
- Clean-room Home Manager switch against the root flake now succeeds without pre-existing ~/.bashrc or ~/.config/nushell/config.nu.
- Home Manager activation now materializes generated runtime-owned Yazi and Zellij config surfaces during switch.
- Verified runtime/current ownership, managed TOML surfaces, generated Zellij layouts, generated Yazi config, Home Manager-owned yzx profile path, successful `yzx doctor`, successful `yzx run which uv`, successful `yzx run which tokei`, and successful `yzx enter --skip-refresh` preflight in an isolated HOME.
close_reason: Completed

title: Popup Alt+t flashes and closes immediately
description: Imported GitHub issue #527. Pressing Alt+t shows the yzx_popup_toggle pane briefly and the floating popup pane flashes closed immediately.
close_reason: Fixed on main in 347dc07 (popup env serialization) and verified live after runtime reinstall

title: Define the staged migration transaction contract for managed config surfaces
description: Specify the staged transaction model for Yazelix-managed config migrations: which managed surfaces participate, where staged output lives, what gets validated before commit, what backup or rollback artifacts are created, and which failures must leave the previous valid state untouched.
design: Keep this contract-focused. Define the exact managed surfaces, staging boundary, validation boundary, backup semantics, and generated-state handling that together make a migration coherent instead of partially applied. Preserve the boundary against touching external non-Yazelix config.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has an explicit staged migration transaction contract for managed config surfaces. 2. The contract names the participating surfaces, staging location or mechanism, validation-before-commit rules, and backup or rollback behavior. 3. The contract is specific enough that both yzx config migrate and startup preflight can follow the same model later without reinterpreting it differently.
close_reason: Completed: added the staged managed-config migration transaction contract and aligned the migration engine spec with it.

title: Implement staged apply semantics in yzx config migrate
description: Replace the direct-write backup/copy-back path in `apply_config_migration_plan` with a narrow managed-config transaction helper that stages rewritten TOML payloads and a JSON manifest under `user_configs`, validates the staged main/pack set, commits via same-directory rename, and rolls back cleanly on failure or stale journal recovery. Even though this bead is framed around `yzx config migrate --apply`, the helper is already shared by doctor and entrypoint call sites, so the implementation must preserve those callers while keeping the explicit migrate path as the main proving UX.
design:
Delete-first:
- do not invent a generic file-transaction framework
- do not broaden the surface beyond the canonical managed config pair

Implementation seam:
- extract a small helper, likely under `nushell/scripts/utils/`, that owns `prepare -> validate -> commit -> rollback` for the canonical managed surfaces
- have `apply_config_migration_plan` delegate to it instead of writing final targets directly

First implementation should:
- stage rewritten TOML payloads in the same `user_configs` filesystem as their final targets
- persist a hidden JSON manifest with `transaction_id`, `schema_version`, `caller`, `phase`, target, staged, backup, and `existed_before` metadata
- validate the staged final pair with the same config-surface loading rules before any canonical target swap
- preserve explicit backup files as the rollback source of truth
- clean up staged files and manifest on success
- on a subsequent explicit migrate apply, detect any stale manifest and roll back conservatively before starting a new transaction

Keep out of scope for this bead:
- startup/entrypoint recovery wiring beyond what already shares `apply_config_migration_plan`
- generic transaction support for runtime state, desktop entries, shell hooks, or external configs
- forward-completion heuristics for interrupted transactions
acceptance_criteria:
1. `apply_config_migration_plan` no longer writes canonical targets directly with `save --force` followed by copy-back rollback.
2. Main-only and main-plus-pack migrations are staged, validated, and committed coherently through the managed-config transaction helper.
3. Safe migrations that create a new `yazelix_packs.toml` sidecar record that the file did not exist before and remove it again on rollback.
4. `yzx config migrate --apply` detects and rolls back an unfinished managed-config transaction before starting a new apply attempt.
5. Successful apply leaves backups in place but removes staged files and manifest artifacts.
6. High-signal regression coverage exists for at least one interrupted or failing apply path, and the existing migrate/doctor fix behaviors continue to pass.
close_reason: Completed: staged yzx config migrate apply through a managed-config transaction helper with stale-journal recovery and regression coverage.

title: Finish reusing the staged migration transaction model in entrypoint preflight and legacy-root relocation
description: 53zx.2 moved deterministic rewrites onto the shared staged transaction engine, but the startup and entrypoint family still has one important ad hoc path left: legacy root-level config relocation is still performed before the transaction boundary. This bead should finish that integration so entrypoint migration preflight uses one coherent prepare, recover, and apply model for both deterministic rewrites and any managed-surface relocation it owns, instead of mixing staged writes with separate pre-transaction moves.
design:
Keep this bead narrow and entrypoint-focused. Start from nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu, nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/core/launch_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/env.nu.

Delete-first boundary:
- do not build a second transaction engine for startup
- do not reopen yzx config migrate apply or doctor semantics beyond shared helper reuse
- do not broaden into generic config-surface ownership work already covered elsewhere

The real remaining seam is that build_preflight_context currently performs legacy-root relocation through reconcile_primary_config_surfaces before the staged transaction lifecycle begins. That means entrypoint preflight already reuses staged apply for deterministic TOML rewrites, but not yet for the full managed-surface transition it is responsible for.

The implementation should either:
1. move legacy-root relocation under the same staged prepare, recover, and apply boundary as entrypoint migration preflight, or
2. introduce one small shared helper that truthfully wraps relocation plus staged apply as one managed-surface transition contract for entrypoints.

Keep the caller distinction explicit:
- entrypoint_preflight may commit safe deterministic changes and relocation
- it may still block launch afterward if manual-only items remain
- interrupted transaction recovery should remain conservative and rollback-first

High-signal code seams to review:
- nushell/scripts/utils/entrypoint_config_migrations.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migration_transactions.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_migrations.nu
- nushell/scripts/utils/config_surfaces.nu
- existing entrypoint regressions in nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_core_commands.nu
acceptance_criteria:
1. Entry-point migration preflight no longer relies on an ad hoc legacy-root relocation step outside the shared managed-config transition model.
2. Safe deterministic rewrites and managed legacy-root relocation for the entrypoint family use one coherent prepare, recover, and apply lifecycle, or one clearly shared wrapper around that lifecycle.
3. Startup and entrypoint code does not maintain a second incompatible migration-commit model beside yzx config migrate apply.
4. Entry-point behavior remains explicit: safe deterministic changes may commit, but remaining manual-only items still block continuation afterward.
5. There is at least one regression that defends the remaining seam this bead closes, not just the already-covered happy path.
notes: Current state after 53zx.2: entrypoint preflight already reuses staged apply for deterministic rewrites and already recovers interrupted staged transactions. The main remaining mismatch with the transaction spec is legacy-root relocation happening early in build_preflight_context via reconcile_primary_config_surfaces, before the staged transaction boundary. Existing tests already cover auto-apply, manual-follow-up blocking, and basic relocation; this bead should focus on the still-unified transition boundary rather than re-testing generic migration behavior.
close_reason: Implemented entrypoint relocation through the shared managed-config transaction lifecycle and added regressions for stale recovery ordering and pack-only relocation reporting.

title: Make Yazelix config migrations land atomically across managed surfaces
description: Imported GitHub issue #520.
design: Treat this as config-transition atomicity, not merely filesystem atomicity. The goal is to make migrations across Yazelix-managed config surfaces land as one coherent state transition: stage the migrated result, validate it before commit, update related managed config files coherently, and preserve explicit backup/recovery paths. Scope this to Yazelix-owned managed paths such as user_configs and generated state that depends on migrated config, while preserving the existing boundary against taking ownership of external user config.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Yazelix has a clearer staged migration model across managed config surfaces. 2. A migration either preserves the previous valid managed state or lands the new valid managed state coherently, rather than leaving partial cross-surface updates behind. 3. yzx config migrate and startup migration preflight follow the same staged transition model. 4. Backups and failure recovery remain explicit and understandable.
notes: Planning refinement: this work is broad enough to deserve explicit child beads for (1) the staged migration contract, (2) staged apply semantics in yzx config migrate, and (3) reuse of that staged transition model in startup or entrypoint preflight. The goal is to keep the architecture coherent without collapsing design and implementation into one vague task.
close_reason: Completed: child beads 53zx.1, 53zx.2, and 53zx.3 landed the staged transaction contract, staged apply semantics, and entrypoint preflight reuse.

title: Require per-dimension test strength scoring to reduce score inflation
close_reason: Completed

title: Enforce per-test strength scoring across all governed test lanes
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit nondefault test lanes file-by-file and remove dead weight
close_reason: Completed

title: Apply test-prune deletions and simplify runner surfaces
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit and prune the default-lane test suite
close_reason: Completed

title: Inventory every test file with lane, contract, score, and verdict
close_reason: Completed

title: Audit and prune the full Yazelix test surface for signal
design: Treat this as an aggressive pruning program, not a test-preservation exercise. First inventory every test file by lane, contract, score, and likely verdict. Then audit the default lane test-by-test using the new strength rubric and remove or demote weak/default-inappropriate cases. After that, audit nondefault lanes file-by-file to decide whether each file still deserves to exist. Finish by applying deletions, demotions, and runner simplifications so the repo is smaller and the suite stays easier to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Every test file under nushell/scripts/dev/test_*.nu is inventoried with lane, rough contract, and a keep/demote/remove/rewrite verdict. 2. The default lane is reviewed test-by-test and at least one weak, redundant, or mis-laned test is removed, demoted, or rewritten if such a case exists. 3. Nondefault lanes are reviewed file-by-file and dead-weight files or tests are removed or narrowed where justified. 4. The resulting runner/validator surfaces remain coherent and all affected validation lanes pass. 5. The audit leaves behind a concise written inventory or report so future pruning starts from reality instead of memory.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add a scored strength rubric for default-lane tests
design: Add a per-test strength rubric for the default suite so test quality is judged explicitly instead of only through prose. Use a small 0-10 score made from five 0-2 dimensions: failure significance, behavior proximity, refactor resilience, cost efficiency, and uniqueness. Require a nearby '# Strength: N/10' marker for every canonical default-lane test entry and enforce a minimum 7/10 bar in the existing default-suite validator. This should remain a decision aid with mechanical enforcement, not fake precision for every test in the repo: default-lane tests carry the score because that lane is the one whose quality bar matters most in CI.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The test governance spec documents the 0-10 default-test strength rubric and the meaning of the score bands. 2. AGENTS.md tells agents to add a nearby '# Strength: N/10' marker for new canonical default-lane tests and respect the minimum 7/10 bar. 3. validate_default_test_traceability.nu rejects canonical default-lane tests that are missing a score or fall below 7/10. 4. The current default canonical suite is scored under the new rubric and passes the validator. 5. The runtime budget remains a separate guard from the quality score.
close_reason: Completed: added a 0-10 strength rubric for canonical default-lane tests, enforced nearby # Strength: N/10 markers with a minimum 7/10 bar in CI, and scored the current default suite under the new model.

title: Strengthen test governance against weak AI-generated tests
design: Replace the default-suite canonical test-count budget with structural governance that better resists low-value AI-generated tests. Enforce small explicit lane declarations on all test_*.nu files, require nearby justification markers for every canonical default-lane test entry, and forbid new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. Keep the runtime budget as the real speed guard. Update AGENTS and the test governance spec so the repo, not prompt discipline, carries the enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The canonical test-count budget is removed from the default-suite validator and from the governance spec. 2. CI rejects test files without an allowed # Test lane declaration. 3. CI rejects default-lane canonical tests that lack a nearby # Defends:, # Regression:, or # Invariant: marker. 4. CI rejects new generic _extended overflow files unless they are explicitly grandfathered. 5. The runtime budget validator remains the speed guard for the default suite.
close_reason: Completed: removed the canonical test-count budget, added enforced test-lane declarations and canonical-test justification markers, and blocked new generic _extended overflow files while keeping the runtime budget as the real CI speed guard.

title: Make the Yazelix desktop entry stable across runtime updates
description: The desktop entry should not keep going stale whenever the installed runtime store path changes. Replace the current runtime-targeted desktop-entry contract with a stable launcher path so normal installs and updates do not require rerunning yzx desktop install just to keep the desktop entry working.
design: Treat the current stale-desktop-entry detection as a guardrail, not the final UX. The real contract problem is that the desktop file points at a specific runtime store path, so normal runtime updates can invalidate it. Replace that with a stable launcher surface such as ~/.local/bin/yzx or ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current so desktop launches remain valid across installs and updates. Preserve clear diagnostics, but the goal is to stop needing repeated yzx desktop install repairs in normal use.
acceptance_criteria: A previously installed Yazelix desktop entry continues to work after normal runtime updates without needing yzx desktop install again. The desktop entry uses a stable launcher path instead of a specific runtime store path. Doctor and launch-time checks may still detect genuinely broken installs, but normal runtime churn should not make the desktop entry stale.
close_reason: Completed

title: Consolidate Home Manager flake surface into the top-level flake
description: Reduce packaging-surface duplication by moving the Home Manager flake entrypoint to the top-level Yazelix flake. The goal is one canonical flake surface that exposes both the install/runtime package and `homeManagerModules.default`, with a deliberate migration path for the current `?dir=home_manager` input users.
design: Treat this as flake-surface consolidation, not a packaging side quest. The top-level flake should become the canonical entrypoint for both Yazelix install/runtime outputs and the Home Manager module, with a deliberate compatibility story for current users of the separate ?dir=home_manager path. Prefer deleting duplicate flake surface area over adding another forwarding layer unless a short migration bridge is genuinely necessary.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The top-level flake exposes the canonical Home Manager module surface alongside the existing install/runtime package surface. 2. The repo no longer relies on the separate home_manager flake entrypoint as the primary story. 3. Existing users of the current ?dir=home_manager path have a clear migration note or temporary compatibility path. 4. Docs and examples point to one canonical flake surface after the change.
close_reason: Completed: the top-level flake now exports the canonical Home Manager module surface, the duplicate home_manager subflake and lockfile are deleted, CI validates the root flake interface directly, and docs now migrate users from ?dir=home_manager to the repo root flake.

title: Add launch-profile freshness diagnostics to yzx doctor
description: Teach `yzx doctor` to report whether the current cached Yazelix launch profile is valid, stale, or missing for the active rebuild-relevant config and tracked devenv inputs so users get actionable guidance before launch/reuse confusion sets in.
design: Keep this user-facing and runtime-local. Reuse compute_config_state and the existing refresh-reason logic instead of reimplementing launch-profile validity in doctor. The result should distinguish at least: healthy profile, stale profile because rebuild-relevant config changed, stale profile because tracked devenv inputs changed, and missing verified launch profile. It should suggest the right recovery path such as `yzx refresh` or rerunning without `--reuse`. Do not turn `yzx doctor` into a repo-maintainer parity validator; that stays in the cheap validator lane.
acceptance_criteria: Running `yzx doctor` includes a clear launch-profile health result. When the cached profile matches the current rebuild-relevant config and tracked inputs, doctor reports it as healthy. When the profile is stale or missing, doctor reports the reason using the existing config-state logic and points the user at the right next command. The check relies on the canonical launch-profile contract rather than duplicating its rules ad hoc.
notes:
2026-04-09 implementation:
- Added launch-profile freshness classification to `yzx doctor` by extending the canonical config-state/launch-state contract instead of reimplementing freshness heuristics inside doctor.
- `doctor` now distinguishes healthy cached launch profile, stale due to rebuild-relevant config changes, stale due to tracked runtime/devenv input changes, and missing verified launch-profile evidence.
- Added a strong default-lane regression covering healthy, stale-config, stale-input, and missing-profile states through the real `yzx doctor --verbose` command.
- Hardened the new check so it skips cleanly when the active config cannot yet be parsed into canonical config state; doctor still reports stale/migration-aware config instead of crashing.
close_reason: Completed: yzx doctor now reports launch-profile freshness states via the canonical config-state and launch-state contract.

title: Canonicalize devenv CLI resolution and PATH ordering
description: Yazelix currently conflates the standalone devenv CLI, the project devenv input, and whatever devenv happens to be first on PATH. Fix the contract by introducing one shared host-tool resolver for devenv, using it for internal invocations and version reporting, and repairing Nushell PATH restoration so stale initial PATH entries do not outrank the current shell PATH.
close_reason: Canonicalized remaining maintainer/internal devenv invocations onto the shared resolver and made Nushell PATH restoration explicitly preserve current-shell precedence over stale saved entries.

title: Fix config validation for optional TOML fields
description: Yazelix config validation currently infers the allowed schema from the parsed default TOML surface, which means optional fields that only exist as commented examples in yazelix_default.toml are treated as unknown. Comments should not affect validation in any way. Fix the validator so supported optional fields like helix.runtime_path are accepted based on a real schema/source-of-truth rather than whether the default TOML happens to materialize that key after parsing. The doctor and startup diagnostics should then report real config problems instead of false unknown-field errors for documented optional settings.
close_reason: GitHub issue #499 is closed; tracker synced.

title: Audit rm usage for safety and destructive-command hygiene
description: Audit the codebase for every rm invocation and decide where deletion behavior needs stronger safety checks, clearer scoping, or better diagnostics.
design: Treat this as destructive-command hygiene, not as a mechanical search-and-replace pass. For each rm usage, verify what is being deleted, how paths are resolved, whether the target can escape the intended runtime boundary, and whether errors are visible. The goal is to catch genuinely risky deletion paths without adding unnecessary friction to safe, bounded cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: All rm usage in the repo has been reviewed and any risky or unclear deletion paths have a concrete follow-up fix or direct cleanup. The audit records which rm invocations are already acceptably bounded and which ones need stronger safeguards or diagnostics.
notes: Promote this safety audit. Current repo direction is pre-packaging hardening: reduce LOC, remove dead/redundant code, and audit destructive paths. rm-call hygiene is a high-priority cleanup pass because it can hide unsafe path handling behind routine maintenance code.
close_reason: Completed: bounded managed cleanup now uses a shared root-checked delete helper, preserves lexical symlinked managed paths, and refuses root/outside targets.

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij/config.kdl
description: Make Yazelix-managed Zellij config self-contained under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl. In managed mode, stop implicitly reading ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default input surface. Instead, treat native Zellij config as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the generated merged runtime config in ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/config.kdl. Update config loading, migration/doctor flows, docs, and verification so the user mental model matches Yazi and the broader user_configs architecture. Include strong behavior tests for managed-path loading, native-config migration/import, and generated runtime output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Move managed Zellij config into user_configs/zellij
description: Make Zellij follow the same ownership model as the rest of managed Yazelix config. In managed mode, the canonical user-editable Zellij input should live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/ (starting with config.kdl), and Yazelix should generate the merged runtime config into ~/.local/share/yazelix/configs/zellij/. Do not keep ~/.config/zellij/config.kdl as the default managed input path; at most, treat it as an import/migration source or explicit escape hatch. This should optimize for system coherence, least surprise, self-contained flake story, and a single mental model: user edits user_configs/*, Yazelix renders runtime state into ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Include deterministic migration/import behavior, updated docs, and focused regression coverage for path ownership and merge precedence.
close_reason: Completed: managed Zellij config now lives under user_configs/zellij with deterministic legacy relocation, fail-fast duplicate detection, docs updates, and regression coverage.

title: Move user-owned config under user_configs with terminal subdir
description: Move user-owned Yazelix config into a dedicated user_configs boundary. End-state: all Yazelix-managed user inputs live under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ (including yazelix.toml, yazelix_packs.toml, terminal/, yazi/, and zellij/), while generated runtime state lives under ~/.local/share/yazelix/. Keep tracked defaults at the repo/config root. Managed mode should not implicitly read external native tool config from unrelated XDG paths; native tool config should only participate as an explicit import/migration source or escape hatch. Keep the system self-contained, coherent for users, and friendly to a future first-party Yazelix flake. Do not auto-scaffold optional user-owned files by default. Add only one minimal guide file at user_configs/README.md rather than a README in every subfolder.
close_reason: Completed

title: Tighten the test contract further and cut LOC, especially Nushell and tests
description: Investigate how to make the test contract stricter while reducing overall LOC. Focus especially on Nushell code size and the test suite footprint, since a significant chunk of the LOC growth is test code.
notes:
Progress on 2026-03-29:
- Extracted shared managed-config fixture and e2e log helpers into nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_helpers.nu.
- Reduced duplicated plumbing across test_yzx_core_commands.nu, test_config_migrate_e2e.nu, test_stale_config_diagnostics_e2e.nu, and test_upgrade_summary_e2e.nu.
- Reduced duplicated doctor/config fixture setup across test_yzx_doctor_commands.nu and test_yzx_generated_configs.nu.
- Fixed stale test setup assumptions that still wrote managed config to legacy root paths instead of user_configs/.
- Landed in commits c62577c and 8f1383e.
Remaining work:
- Continue scanning for honest shared-fixture reductions in remaining high-duplication Nushell tests.
- Keep removing only setup/plumbing duplication; preserve strong behavior coverage and avoid weak existence tests.
close_reason: Tightened the default-suite contract to spec-backed entrypoints only, removed regression-only sidecars, and pruned low-signal tests to cut default-suite time and LOC.

title: Make stale config diagnostics migration-aware across startup and doctor
description: When Yazelix hits a known stale config value or field, surface a precise migration-aware diagnostic at the exact failure boundary instead of collapsing into a generic startup or generation failure. The same classification should also appear in yzx doctor so users can understand what changed and what the safest next action is.
design: Consume the shared migration registry from the migrate work instead of inventing a separate error taxonomy here. Startup must stay read only: no silent rewrites, no hidden fallbacks, and no generic wrap up that hides the real stale config problem. Diagnostics should name the exact config path and stale value, state what changed and in which real Yazelix version or date when known, distinguish safe auto-fixable cases from manual-only cases, and point to the right next step such as yzx config migrate, yzx doctor --fix when explicitly invoked, a one line manual edit, or the changelog surface. Cover startup, refresh, and doctor paths with the same underlying classification and copy contracts, even if the surrounding wrapper command differs.
acceptance_criteria: A stale config such as zellij.widget_tray containing layout fails with a targeted actionable message instead of a generic Failed to generate Zellij configuration wrapper. yzx doctor reports the same migration-aware finding with the same path, rationale, and next steps, and safe cases clearly advertise whether yzx config migrate or yzx doctor --fix can resolve them while startup remains read only. Unknown or unsupported schema problems still fail clearly without pretending to have a migration. Add comprehensive unit tests for finding classification, message formatting, multi-finding aggregation, and safe versus manual guidance. Add end to end scripts with detailed logging that launch or refresh Yazelix from temp homes with stale configs, capture the full failure output, and verify the doctor output for both a known migration and an unknown invalid config case.
notes:
Planning refinements:
- Create a focused spec such as docs/specs/stale_config_diagnostics.md.
- Keep startup read only; explicit repair happens only via dedicated commands.
- Prefer one message-rendering layer so start_yazelix, refresh, and doctor do not drift in terminology or suggested actions.
- Preserve a clear distinction between known migrations, unknown invalid values, missing fields, and non-config runtime failures.
- Verification plan: unit coverage for classified findings and message rendering, plus end to end Nushell scripts that log full launch and refresh stderr or stdout, doctor output, and the exact message differences between known migration cases and unsupported schema errors.
close_reason: Completed: added shared stale-config diagnostics, migration-aware startup and refresh failures, doctor reporting and doctor --fix integration, plus spec-backed unit and e2e coverage for known migrations versus unsupported config problems.

title: Fix missing Yazelix plugin permission popup on fresh Zellij startup
description: On fresh Yazelix launches under Zellij 0.44, the pane orchestrator/popup runner permission popup may never appear even after clearing Zellij runtime/cache/share state. Only zjstatus gets a persisted permission block, while Yazelix plugins remain ungranted until permissions are seeded manually in permissions.kdl.
notes: Added repo-level recovery path on 2026-03-27: `yzx repair zellij-permissions` now seeds the tracked/runtime permission blocks for the pane orchestrator and popup runner into ~/.cache/zellij/permissions.kdl and tells the user to restart Yazelix. This is an explicit recovery/workaround path, not the root-cause fix for the missing popup.
close_reason: Superseded by the startup-handoff hardening and permission recovery work on 2026-03-27. The missing Yazelix plugin permission popup was a downstream symptom of the broken Zellij startup path; commit 5043aeb removed the boot-time plugin IPC and duplicate launch-tree hazard, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit recovery path for stale permission state.

title: Fix Yazelix launch hanging after Zellij config generation
description: Yazelix sometimes hangs during startup after the welcome prompt and after successfully regenerating the Zellij config. The visible stall point is after the final 'Config will auto-regenerate when source files change' line, before the Zellij UI takes over. This should be tracked separately from the stale zjstatus layout-label bug.
design: Treat this as a startup handoff bug, not a status-bar naming bug. The key boundary is the transition from Yazelix's launch/setup flow into the actual Zellij UI. Investigate whether the hang is in the included terminal path, start_yazelix_inner handoff, wrapper interaction, plugin permission state, or Zellij startup state after config generation. Avoid bundling unrelated fixes. Keep the bug scoped to restoring reliable startup first; layout-label correctness is a separate issue.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix no longer hangs after printing that Zellij configuration was generated successfully. From a normal launch, the UI reliably transitions into the Zellij session after that point. Add regression coverage or a defended diagnostic contract around the launch handoff, and document the exact failure boundary that was fixed.
notes: March 27 fresh-state evidence: after clearing /tmp/zellij-$UID, /run/user/$UID/zellij, ~/.cache/zellij, ~/.local/share/zellij, and the permission cache, a new launch still recreated the bad state. Zellij restored only the cached zjstatus permission block; neither yazelix_pane_orchestrator.wasm nor yazelix_popup_runner.wasm reached the permission-prompt stage. The launch also spawned two separate Zellij 0.44.0 servers from a clean slate, each with its own launch_sidebar_yazi/set_workspace_root path. This means the missing Yazelix plugin permission popup is a downstream symptom, not the root cause. Current strongest boundary: duplicate server/session creation or earlier Zellij 0.44 startup corruption before Yazelix plugins can finish loading.
close_reason: Completed by the March 27 startup-handoff fixes. Commit 5043aeb (Harden Zellij startup handoff) removed the startup-time sidebar launch path that was implicated in the duplicate-server / pre-UI stall boundary, and commit 142e4c8 added an explicit permission recovery path for already-corrupted Zellij plugin state. The observed hang after successful Zellij config generation is no longer the active failure mode; any future recurrence should come back as a fresh regression with new evidence.

title: Verify whether the LiteLLM PyPI compromise affected Yazelix
description: Audit whether the malicious LiteLLM PyPI releases affected Yazelix directly or through any shipped dependency or package surface.
notes: Assessment: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or package declarations. Yazelix does not appear to ship or pin LiteLLM directly in its maintained runtime surface, so this issue is treated as not affected.
close_reason: Not affected: no litellm or LiteLLM references were found in the repo, shipped config, or maintained package declarations, so Yazelix does not appear to ship the compromised PyPI package in its supported runtime surface.

title: Audit large files and identify worthwhile refactors
description: Inspect Yazelix's largest or most complex files and decide which ones deserve real refactoring versus which ones are large for legitimate reasons. The goal is to create a justified shortlist, not to split files reflexively.
design: Use delete-first reasoning rather than assuming every large file is bad. Consider file size, complexity, churn, ownership boundaries, and whether the file is hard to understand or hard to test. Prefer identifying a small number of high-value refactors with concrete boundaries over creating a blanket mandate to break up large files everywhere.
acceptance_criteria: There is a ranked shortlist of large files that are worth refactoring, with concrete reasons and suggested seams. Files that are large but acceptable are explicitly called out as such. Follow-up implementation beads are narrower than this audit umbrella.
notes:
2026-04-09 continued public-surface cleanup:
- Internalized file-local `ascii_art.nu` helpers that only supported the local welcome/screen rendering pipeline.
- Internalized `is_palette_eligible_command` in `yzx/command_palette_catalog.nu`; the real exported surface is `get_palette_menu_items`.
- Verification: screen canonical tests, workspace palette-eligibility regression, syntax validation, and traceability validation pass.

2026-04-09 completion:
- The large-file/refactor audit is now recorded in `docs/streamlining_audit_2026_04.md`.
- The doc names the acceptable large files, the remaining worthwhile seams, and the specific delete-first rationale for stopping the broad umbrella here.
close_reason: Completed

title: Test suite governance: keep every test relevant
description: Define how Yazelix decides whether a test belongs in the suite, which suite it belongs in, and when an existing test should be removed. The goal is to prevent the test surface from growing forever without clear relevance.
design: Start from first principles: every test should defend a real user-visible behavior, a regression that already happened, an integration boundary, a source-of-truth invariant, or an explicitly documented contract. This policy must apply to both new and existing tests. Define the actual Yazelix suite surfaces explicitly: the default yzx dev test path, optional heavier lanes such as sweep or visual integration checks, CI-only checks, and cheap local maintainer hooks like prek. Require a lightweight inventory of the current suites by test file or suite entrypoint, classify each bucket by the kind of contract it protects, and define what happens when a test cannot be tied to a living contract, regression, invariant, or accepted user-visible behavior: remove it, demote it out of the default suite, or explicitly grandfather and justify it. Prefer fewer higher-signal tests over broad shallow coverage.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a written test-policy document or issue design note that defines: 1. the real suite categories and command entrypoints (yzx dev test, optional heavier lanes, CI-only checks, and prek); 2. admission criteria for new tests; 3. retention and pruning rules for existing tests; and 4. when a test belongs in the default suite versus an optional lane. Existing tests are inventoried at least at the suite or file level against that policy, and at least one low-value or redundant test is removed, demoted, or explicitly grandfathered with justification. Future testing work can point to this policy when deciding whether a test is worth adding and where it belongs.
notes: Final shape: public test model simplified again after the aggressive prune. yzx dev test now runs the normal non-sweep automated regression suite with explicit membership: test_yzx_commands.nu, test_yzx_extra_regressions.nu, and test_reuse_mode.nu. The public --extended flag was removed because only a tiny number of extra checks remained and the split no longer earned its UX cost. Internal organization remains: core yzx contracts plus a small extra-regression bundle. The default suite currently covers 20 core checks + 6 extra regressions + 1 reuse-mode regression. yzx dev test --all now means default suite plus sweep + visual. Verified: validate_syntax, yzx dev test, direct core/extra/reuse entrypoints, validate_specs, and validate_readme_version all pass.
close_reason: Completed: added test-suite governance spec, switched to explicit default-suite membership, aggressively pruned low-value tests, and simplified the public yzx dev test model.

title: Repo cleanup pass for legacy and unused artifacts
description: Do a deliberate cleanup pass across the repository to remove legacy, dead, or clearly unused files, commands, docs references, and compatibility paths that no longer serve the current Yazelix runtime model. Keep the pass conservative: prefer deleting things that are demonstrably stale over broad aesthetic churn.
design: Treat this as a conservative pruning pass, not an aesthetic refactor. Target legacy compatibility paths, stale docs references, dead commands, unused helper files, superseded repo-era assumptions, and external-tool artifacts that are no longer part of the intended Yazelix workflow. Every deletion should be justified by one of: no remaining callers, superseded by a newer path, no longer part of the supported public surface, or contradicted by the current architecture/runtime model. Prefer removal over replacement when behavior is no longer needed, and avoid broad style churn or speculative rewrites just to make the repo look cleaner.
acceptance_criteria: The repo leaves the pass with less unjustified surface area: at least one real stale, duplicated, superseded, or unused artifact is removed from the categories touched, any removed public surface is scrubbed from docs/help text, and the default test suite still passes. Leave behind a short inventory of what was removed versus intentionally kept. Net repo LOC should decrease unless a small amount of new code is explicitly justified as necessary to enable a larger cleanup or add a validator that supports future pruning.
notes:
Partial progress already landed in 5253b00, 591edee, 7aef715, and 4857fcb, but the cleanup lane is not exhausted. Keep this bead open as an ongoing high-priority repo-hardening pass for deleting legacy, dead, redundant, or stale artifacts that are still discoverable.

2026-04-09 conservative delete-first cleanup:
- Removed definition-only dead surfaces with no repo/docs/config callers instead of inventing more wrappers or keeping compatibility shims alive.
- Deleted the unused terminal-config compatibility shim `resolve_terminal_config_from_env` from `utils/terminal_launcher.nu`.
- Deleted the unused Helix probe `is_helix_running_test` from `integrations/helix.nu`.
- Deleted the unused sidebar cwd export `get_active_sidebar_cwd` from `integrations/yazi_sidebar_state.nu`.
- Deleted stale pane-orchestrator and popup-runner sync aliases from `setup/zellij_plugin_paths.nu`; the surviving tracked/runtime getters and explicit sync entrypoints remain the supported surface.

2026-04-09 second dead-helper prune:

- Deleted the unused `build_active_config_diagnostic_report` export from `utils/config_diagnostics.nu`; the surviving startup/doctor entrypoints already call the explicit report builders they need.
- Deleted the unused `build_current_upgrade_summary_report` export from `utils/upgrade_summary.nu`; the supported surface remains `build_upgrade_summary_report`, `maybe_show_first_run_upgrade_summary`, and `show_current_upgrade_summary`.
