title: Fix Ghostty dead keys regression
description: Ghostty dead keys stopped working again in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path. Restore dead-key composition without changing the default terminal contract or relying on host-specific local workarounds.
design: Treat this as a real input regression in the default Ghostty path, not a cosmetic character-rendering bug. Reproduce the dead-key failure in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty integration, identify whether the breakage comes from wrapper/env handling, config generation, input-method propagation, or another launch-layer change, and restore dead-key composition without falling back to a different terminal or shipping a local-machine workaround.
acceptance_criteria: Dead-key composition works again in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path. The fix lives in Yazelix itself, preserves Ghostty as the intended default experience, and is verified through the real Ghostty launch path rather than a substitute terminal. The bead records the actual cause so the regression is less likely to recur.
close_reason: Fixed by restoring the managed Ghostty path and moving launch to the current runtime-local Ghostty script. Dead keys now work again in the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path.

title: Prepare the nixpkgs PR patch and submission notes for Yazelix
description: Once the local package shape is stable, prepare the actual nixpkgs-facing patch, metadata, and submission notes for the upstream PR.
design: This is the public-facing packaging bead: adapt the proven local package work into the patch shape expected by nixpkgs, capture reviewer-relevant notes, and make the upstream submission path straightforward. Keep policy and implementation disputes out of this stage as much as possible by resolving them earlier.
acceptance_criteria: The upstream-facing patch and submission notes are prepared from the proven local package work. The remaining work to open the nixpkgs PR should be mechanical or review-driven rather than unresolved product design.
close_reason: Prepared local nixpkgs draft package, validation harness, and submission notes

title: Implement and smoke-test a local nixpkgs-ready Yazelix package expression
description: Translate the nixpkgs package contract into a local package expression and smoke-test the wrapped Yazelix entrypoints before any upstream PR is prepared.
design: Do the real packaging work locally first: package files, runtime assets, wrapper behavior, desktop integration expectations, and smoke paths that match how nixpkgs users would actually invoke Yazelix. Keep this bead repo-local; do not conflate it with opening the upstream PR.
acceptance_criteria: A local nixpkgs-style package expression builds successfully and the wrapped Yazelix entrypoints are smoke-tested. The package shape should match the contract from yazelix-2ex.1.4.3.1 closely enough that the remaining upstream step is mainly adaptation and review.
close_reason: Completed

title: Define the nixpkgs package contract for Yazelix
description: Define the package shape expected for a nixpkgs submission now that Yazelix has a package-ready runtime and flake install surface.
design: Treat this as a packaging contract bead, not implementation churn. Be explicit about package outputs, wrapper behavior, runtime asset layout, required dependencies, desktop integration expectations, and what stays out of scope for a first nixpkgs submission. The result should make the later implementation bead mostly translational work.
acceptance_criteria: Record the nixpkgs-facing package contract in a spec or equivalent packaging note. Be explicit about Linux and macOS scope, wrapper/runtime behavior, installed files, update story, and what a first submission will intentionally not provide.
close_reason: Defined the nixpkgs package contract in docs/specs/nixpkgs_package_contract.md and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Define the v14 release gate around boundary hardening and honest Core-readiness
description: Capture when Yazelix should actually jump to v14. The major bump should mean the workspace/backend boundary is real enough that a narrower Core-style omission mode would be honest, even if Yazelix still ships as one product. Treat v14 as a boundary-hardening release gate, not as a packaging milestone.
design: Treat v14 as a product-era decision about architectural honesty rather than distribution prestige. The gate should answer: which workspace flows are now proven to survive without backend ownership, whether install/update/doctor can degrade honestly in a narrowed mode, and which remaining cross-language hot paths still prevent a clean workspace-vs-backend split. Do not make nixpkgs packaging a required part of this gate while packaging remains explicitly low priority.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit durable definition of what must be true before v14: the required boundary-hardening milestones, the honest Core-readiness expectations, and the concrete remaining beads/issues that satisfy that gate. The decision is specific enough that future release planning can say whether v14 is actually earned without leaning on packaging progress.
notes:
2026-04-09 rescope:
- Packaging and nixpkgs submission are no longer part of the v14 gate.
- v14 now means workspace/backend boundary hardening and honest Core-readiness, not packaged Yazelix.
- The active gate is the remaining proof work around backend-free workspace UX, honest capability tiers, pane-orchestrator-owned workspace truth, sidebar-cache removal, and explicit runtime-identity seams.

Internal pipeline to follow:
1. Root and runtime-identity hygiene: `yazelix-le0s.6`.
2. Workspace truth migration branch: `yazelix-0hm3` -> `yazelix-3a0u`.
3. Honest capability-tier branch: `yazelix-zjyw`.
4. Backend-free workspace proof: `yazelix-5wao` after the two branch outputs and the root-identity cleanup.
5. v14 decision gate: close `yazelix-wg3z` only when the proof and boundary-hardening slices are complete.
6. Only after the v14 gate: Rust sequencing and slice ordering in `yazelix-kt5.1`, then later Rust implementation beads.
7. Only after the v14 gate and Core boundary proof: evaluate speculative Core backend candidates like `yazelix-qgj7.1`.
close_reason: Defined the v14 boundary-hardening gate as a durable spec tied to the completed runtime-identity, workspace-truth, capability-tier, and backend-free workspace proof slices.

title: Show a focused stale-desktop-entry repair error at launch time
description: Teach the desktop launcher to validate the desktop-entry freshness metadata at startup and fail with a short, actionable repair message when the installed desktop entry is stale.
design: At launch time, validate the desktop-entry freshness metadata against the current installed runtime and fail fast with a short repair message when they differ. Keep the error focused: say the desktop entry is stale and recommend yzx desktop install. Avoid generic stack traces or broader reinstall guidance unless the runtime is genuinely missing.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Desktop launches can detect a stale Yazelix desktop entry before continuing into broken runtime paths. 2. The failure message names the problem as a stale desktop entry. 3. The repair path is the minimal one: yzx desktop install.

title: Add a self-validating desktop-entry freshness contract
description: Define a Yazelix desktop-entry contract that carries enough metadata to detect when the installed desktop file is stale relative to the current installed runtime, instead of relying only on indirect path drift checks.
design: Prefer explicit desktop-entry metadata over heuristic-only staleness checks. Store a runtime-target identifier in the desktop file, such as the runtime/current target path or a derived install-generation token. Keep the contract readable and stable enough for both yzx doctor and the desktop launcher to consume.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The desktop entry format includes a Yazelix-owned freshness field. 2. The chosen field is strong enough to distinguish a current runtime from a stale one without relying only on version strings. 3. The contract is documented clearly enough for both doctor and launcher-side validation.

title: Add broader install-artifact staleness detection to yzx doctor
description: Teach yzx doctor to detect stale or broken repairable Yazelix artifacts beyond the desktop entry, such as shell hooks, ~/.local/bin/yzx, runtime/current, and generated config references. Each diagnosis should point to the smallest repair command that fixes the specific artifact.

title: Add desktop-entry staleness detection to yzx doctor
description: Teach yzx doctor to detect when an installed Yazelix desktop entry exists but points at a missing runtime or a runtime different from ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current, and recommend the exact repair command.

title: Add repairable install-artifact staleness checks to yzx doctor
description: Teach yzx doctor to detect stale or broken installed Yazelix artifacts that are repairable from userland, such as desktop entries, shell hooks, CLI symlinks, runtime symlinks, and generated config references. Focus on actionable diagnosis with exact repair commands rather than maintainer-only invariants.
design: Keep yzx doctor focused on user-facing installed-state health, not repo parity. Start with artifact families that have already broken in practice during no-clone/flake installs. Checks should distinguish missing, broken, and stale-but-repairable states and print the minimal repair command for each case.
acceptance_criteria: 1. There is a bead-level plan for repairable install-state staleness checks in yzx doctor. 2. The plan explicitly includes desktop-entry staleness and distinguishes it from broader install-state drift. 3. The plan keeps maintainer-only validators out of yzx doctor.

title: Implement Ghostty source policy for non-NixOS Linux
description: Apply the Ghostty package-source policy by removing the assumption that nixpkgs Ghostty is the safe default on non-NixOS Linux.
design: Turn the package-source policy into concrete behavior. Keep Homebrew/system Ghostty on macOS, keep nixpkgs Ghostty acceptable on NixOS, and change the non-NixOS Linux story so Yazelix no longer funnels users into nixpkgs Ghostty as the unqualified default. That may mean preferring another default terminal such as WezTerm on non-NixOS Linux, preferring a system Ghostty path when present, or explicitly demoting the Nix-managed Ghostty path behind an opt-in/caveat.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix no longer treats nixpkgs Ghostty as an unqualified default on non-NixOS Linux. The resulting default and override behavior is explicit in code and docs, and users on non-NixOS Linux are not steered into a Ghostty path that predictably fails without extra OpenGL setup.

title: Fix Yazelix-provided Ghostty runtime integration
description: The Yazelix-managed Ghostty path is still less reliable than it should be. Recent install and launch regressions showed that the packaged/default Ghostty flow can fail because of runtime-root propagation, wrapper assumptions, desktop-launch differences, or other Yazelix-managed integration bugs.
design: Treat this as the end-to-end Ghostty integration bug, not a single wrapper tweak. Audit the Yazelix-provided Ghostty path across install, yzx launch, restart, and desktop entry launch. Separate Ghostty-host problems from Yazelix-owned runtime/env/config bugs, then fix the Yazelix-owned path so the default managed Ghostty experience is reliable without local handholding.
acceptance_criteria: A normal installed Yazelix setup can launch through the managed Ghostty path from yzx launch and the desktop entry without clone-path assumptions or stale-wrapper failures. The remaining Ghostty-specific host limitations, if any, are explicit and documented rather than surfacing as silent Yazelix breakage.
notes: New evidence on 2026-04-01: plain nix-provided Ghostty also fails on the maintainer's hybrid Intel+NVIDIA Linux host outside the Yazelix wrapper path. Direct probe: devenv shell -- bash -lc 'timeout 8 ghostty --gtk-single-instance=false -e true' reproduced the OpenGL/EGL failure. Treat remaining Yazelix-owned wrapper/runtime issues separately from the possibility that nixpkgs Ghostty itself is not a safe default on some Linux hosts.
close_reason: Fixed by stabilizing the managed Ghostty launch path across install, yzx launch, and desktop launch, and removing stale-wrapper dependence from the installed runtime path.

title: Fix Ghostty shader generation and packaging
description: Ghostty shader assets and generated shader config are still fragile in packaged/runtime installs. Recent failures showed that shader generation, copying, or cleanup can leave Ghostty without the expected shader files even when Yazelix otherwise launches.
design: Treat this as a real generated-asset contract bug. Identify which shader artifacts Ghostty actually needs at runtime, where Yazelix generates or copies them, and why installed-runtime launches can end up with missing, unreadable, or stale shader trees. Fix the asset pipeline at the source instead of relying on local cleanup or manual re-generation.
acceptance_criteria: Installed and clone-based Yazelix launches both materialize the expected Ghostty shader tree. Repeated config generation does not leave broken or empty shader directories. The maintained Ghostty config points only at shader assets that actually exist after generation/install.
notes: Completed on v15. The shader pipeline already generated referenced shader files for clone-based runs, but the packaged-runtime half of the contract was under-defended. Added installed-runtime validation to validate_flake_install.nu: it now checks the packaged runtime contains the Ghostty shader builder, trail variants, and vendored cursor-effect templates; then it materializes Ghostty terminal config through the installed yzx runtime and verifies every generated custom-shader reference exists, including generated_effects output. Verification: validate_syntax, git diff --check, targeted validate_flake_install verify against an installed temp home, and full validate_flake_install all after fixing the probe to use double-quoted Nushell module paths.
close_reason: Completed packaged-runtime Ghostty shader validation and verified installed-runtime materialization.

title: Make flake install/bootstrap independent of host Nushell version
description:
Problem:
The flake installer and bootstrap path still expose users to host Nushell compatibility issues. Recent install failures on Nushell 0.108 proved that Yazelix bootstrap is not yet sufficiently isolated from the user's ambient Nu version.

Design:
Audit and reduce every bootstrap-sensitive path that still depends on host Nushell parsing or runtime behavior, and ensure the installer uses the packaged Nushell/tooling path consistently enough that host Nu version drift no longer breaks first-run onboarding.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The flake install/bootstrap path no longer fails because of host Nushell version drift in the supported onboarding flow.
- Bootstrap-sensitive scripts either avoid host Nu entirely or are explicitly compatible with the supported host floor.
- The maintained smoke/install lane covers the intended bootstrap contract.
close_reason: Completed

title: Harden flake onboarding for host tool bootstrap
description:
Problem:
The flake install surface still assumes too much about host bootstrap tools. Recent failures showed that host Nushell version drift can break install/setup even when the packaged runtime is correct.

Design:
Track the remaining onboarding hardening work as a small subtree focused on bootstrap-tool assumptions, especially host Nushell dependence and explicit pinned-tool onboarding behavior.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The remaining host-tool assumptions in the flake onboarding path are explicit and tracked.
- Host Nushell dependence is addressed separately from broader pinned-tool update workflow.
- The onboarding story for pinned bootstrap tools is coherent.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add a maintained flake install smoke check
description:
Problem:
The repo can ship flake install regressions without detection. Recent failures affected `nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install` directly.

Design:
Add a focused smoke check that validates the flake install surface end-to-end enough to catch installer/runtime regressions without turning it into a heavy full-session integration suite.

Acceptance Criteria:
- There is a maintained smoke path for the flake install surface.
- The smoke check exercises the installer and verifies the installed `yzx` entrypoint at minimum.
- The check is wired into the appropriate maintainer/CI lane.
close_reason: Implemented flake install smoke validator and wired it into CI

title: Harden the flake install surface
description:
Problem:
The top-level flake install surface exists, but it is still easy to regress because the repo does not yet defend the full install path, some docs remain contradictory, and desktop-entry behavior is not yet an explicit flake-install responsibility.

Design:
Track the remaining flake-specific hardening work as a small focused subtree: smoke validation, docs reconciliation, and explicit desktop-entry installation policy.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The flake install surface has maintained regression coverage.
- User-facing docs describe the flake install path consistently.
- Desktop-entry installation/responsibility is explicitly decided and tracked.
close_reason: Completed initial flake hardening follow-up subtree

title: Include Nushell version in the pin/update workflow
description:
Problem:
The current pin/update flow tracks Yazelix and devenv version movement, but it does not treat the host/bootstrap Nushell version as an explicit pinned surface. Recent installer breakage on Nushell 0.108 showed that Nushell version drift matters during bootstrap and should be part of the maintained update story.

Design:
Define Nushell version as an explicit pinned tool surface in the same workflow that updates maintained pins, and extend the maintainer-facing update automation so `yzx dev update` (or the relevant subcommand in that surface) can refresh the Nushell pin and any corresponding lock/config references intentionally. The workflow should make it obvious when Yazelix runtime behavior depends on a host/bootstrap Nu floor versus the managed environment Nu version.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The maintained pin/update workflow explicitly includes Nushell version handling.
- `yzx dev update` (or the relevant maintainer update path) can update the Nushell pin without manual scattered edits.
- The implementation keeps Home Manager/default config/runtime pin metadata coherent where applicable.
- The command/docs make clear which Nushell version is bootstrap-sensitive versus runtime-managed.
- The workflow fails clearly if the Nushell pin cannot be updated consistently.

title: Packaging: define the top-level flake output contract and installed runtime layout
description: Define the exact flake outputs Yazelix should expose and the installed runtime layout those outputs target, so the flake stays thin and the install command does not accidentally treat the flake source path as the runtime root.
design: This is the contract bead that keeps the flake honest. Specify which outputs exist first, which ones are optional later, where the persistent runtime tree lives, how yzx points at it, and how desktop integration relates to that runtime. Keep devenv.nix and the runtime-root contract as the source of truth; the flake should wrap them, not fork them.
acceptance_criteria: A concrete output contract and installed-runtime layout are documented well enough that the implementation beads can add a thin flake.nix and an install app without reopening basic path-ownership questions.
notes:
Recorded in docs/specs/flake_interface_contract.md.

Phase 1 required outputs:
- apps.<system>.install
- packages.<system>.runtime
- packages.<system>.default = runtime

Phase 1 installed-runtime model:
- stable runtime pointer at ~/.local/share/yazelix/runtime/current
- stable yzx entrypoint at ~/.local/bin/yzx
- user config remains under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/
- bootstrap flake source path is not the installed runtime
close_reason: Flake output and installed-runtime contract recorded

title: Packaging: add the top-level flake skeleton and expose the install app
description: Add a thin top-level flake.nix that exposes the initial Yazelix outputs, starting with the canonical installer app selected by the install-UX decision.
design: Keep the flake minimal. It should expose only the outputs needed for the chosen front door and future packaging discoverability, while delegating the real runtime behavior to the existing runtime assets and the devenv.nix-backed model.
acceptance_criteria: The repo has a working top-level flake.nix whose initial outputs match the documented contract, including an install app entrypoint.
close_reason: Top-level flake skeleton and install app exposed

title: Packaging: implement the install app and persistent runtime materialization
description: Implement the nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install flow so it materializes a persistent runtime, initializes user config if missing, and installs the stable yzx entrypoint without requiring a live clone.
design: Bootstrap only. The install app should not duplicate the runtime definition or point stable entrypoints at the bootstrap flake source path. It should install or materialize a persistent runtime and wire yzx and optional desktop assets to that runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Running the install app from the flake creates a persistent Yazelix runtime, leaves user config under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/, and makes yzx available as a stable command without relying on a clone path.
close_reason: Install app now materializes the runtime and stable yzx entrypoint

title: Packaging: rewrite install docs and smoke-check the flake front door
description: Update the installation story to lead with the flake install command and verify the basic install surface works end-to-end.
design: Do not over-document transitional clone flows once the flake front door exists. Make the guide short, copy-paste friendly, and honest about Nix prerequisites and runtime ownership. Keep smoke checks focused on the real front door, not broad test churn.
acceptance_criteria: The install guide leads with nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install, and the flake front door has at least one maintained smoke path that proves the installed yzx command and runtime layout are coherent.
close_reason: Install docs now lead with the flake front door and the smoke path is verified

title: Install UX: choose the canonical one-command Yazelix install story
description:
Choose the single install story Yazelix should optimize for after comparing the leading options. This is where the project stops debating surfaces and commits to one first-class command users can copy and paste.

Design:
Use the comparison work to choose one canonical path and demote the others to secondary or maintainer-oriented options. Record the user-facing command, supported platforms, runtime/config/state ownership, how yzx becomes available, and what documentation or packaging changes are required.

Acceptance Criteria:
One install path is clearly recommended as the front-door UX. The bead records the chosen command, why it beat the alternatives, and the concrete follow-on implementation work needed to ship it.
notes:
Decision: optimize for a first-party Nix installer app as the canonical install front door.

Chosen command shape:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Why it beat the alternatives:
- honest about Yazelix being a Nix-first product
- short enough to feel like a real copy-paste install command
- aligns with runtime-root and package-ready work already underway
- avoids creating a separate hosted-installer product before the packaged path is stable

Minimum follow-on work:
- add a thin top-level flake interface
- expose an `install` app
- install or materialize a persistent runtime tree
- install the stable `yzx` executable and optional desktop assets
- make update behavior work without a live Git clone
- rewrite installation docs around this front door
close_reason: Canonical one-command install story chosen

title: Install UX: evaluate a hosted curl-pipe bootstrap installer
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should ship a hosted bootstrap command in the style of modern tools: a short curl-pipe installer that fetches or installs the real runtime cleanly without teaching users the repo layout first.

Design:
Compare trust, auditability, update behavior, rollback story, macOS/Linux portability, and how the installer would hand off to Nix, devenv, runtime assets, yzx wrapper creation, and desktop integration. Be explicit about what must stay transparent to avoid a sketchy installer story.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether a hosted installer is a viable first-class install surface, the exact command shape if yes, and the minimum infrastructure or release discipline Yazelix would need before shipping it.
notes:
Outcome: not the preferred first move.

A hosted `curl | sh` installer could eventually wrap a mature packaged install path, but it should not be Yazelix's first-class front door yet.

Why it loses now:
- adds a second trust surface immediately
- adds release-hosting and cross-platform bootstrap burden
- still has to hand off to Nix and the pinned `devenv` story anyway
- risks looking simpler than it really is while hiding important runtime constraints

Reconsider only after the packaged runtime and first-party installer app are solid.
close_reason: Evaluation complete; hosted curl installer not chosen as first-class front door

title: Install UX: evaluate a hosted curl-pipe bootstrap installer
description:
Evaluate whether Yazelix should ship a hosted bootstrap command in the style of modern tools: a short curl-pipe installer that fetches or installs the real runtime cleanly without teaching users the repo layout first.

Design:
Compare trust, auditability, update behavior, rollback story, macOS/Linux portability, and how the installer would hand off to Nix, devenv, runtime assets, yzx wrapper creation, and desktop integration. Be explicit about what must stay transparent to avoid a sketchy installer story.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether a hosted installer is a viable first-class install surface, the exact command shape if yes, and the minimum infrastructure or release discipline Yazelix would need before shipping it.
close_reason: Duplicate of yazelix-aa24.3

title: Install UX: evaluate a first-party nix run bootstrap surface
description:
Evaluate whether the cleanest one-command install UX for Yazelix is a first-party `nix run` surface that bootstraps the real runtime without requiring users to understand the repo layout.

Design:
Assess how thin the required flake interface can stay, what it would install or initialize, whether it can produce `yzx`, desktop entry assets, and runtime placement cleanly, and how it would behave on Linux vs macOS. Be explicit about whether `nix run` is just a bootstrap helper or the long-term user-facing install command.

Acceptance Criteria:
The bead records whether `nix run` is a viable first-class install entrypoint, the exact user-facing command shape if yes, and the concrete implementation prerequisites if Yazelix chooses this path.
notes:
Outcome: viable and preferred.

The cleanest one-command install surface for Yazelix is a thin first-party installer app exposed through a top-level flake:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Why it wins:
- matches Yazelix's real Nix/devenv architecture
- gives users a copy-paste command without inventing a second bootstrap ecosystem
- keeps trust and auditability better than a hosted shell installer
- can stay thin if it only bootstraps a persistent installed runtime and then hands off to the stable `yzx` entrypoint

Critical constraint: the flake source path itself must not become the runtime root. The installer must install or materialize a persistent runtime tree and point `yzx` and desktop assets at that installed runtime.

Implementation follow-on belongs to `yazelix-2ex.1.4.2`.
close_reason: Evaluation complete; first-party nix run installer app chosen as viable

title: Define a first-class one-command install UX for Yazelix
description:
Users should have a first impression closer to modern tools like uv: one obvious copy-paste install path, not a repo-bootstrap explanation. Evaluate concrete install surfaces such as `curl | sh`, `nix run`, or another thin first-party bootstrap that installs the real runtime cleanly without reviving the old clone-under-~/.config/yazelix assumption.

Design:
Treat this as install-product design, not as a docs polish task. Compare the trust, portability, maintenance cost, update story, rollback story, and failure modes of each candidate. Be explicit about what the command installs, where runtime assets live, how user config is initialized, and how the installed `yzx` command and desktop entry are produced. Prefer a surface that feels copy-paste simple for users while still preserving Yazelix's real runtime model and Nix/devenv constraints.

Acceptance Criteria:
A single recommended install UX is chosen and justified against the main alternatives. The bead records the exact user-facing command shape, ownership of runtime/config/state paths, and the minimum supporting implementation work needed to ship it. The result should make the install guide shorter and more copy-paste friendly, not just more carefully worded.
notes:
Decision recorded in docs/specs/one_command_install_ux.md.

Recommendation: Yazelix should ship a first-class one-command install UX built around a thin first-party Nix installer app, not a hosted shell installer.

Canonical front door once implemented:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Important boundary: the GitHub flake source path used during `nix run` is bootstrap-only. The installer must materialize a persistent runtime tree, initialize user config if missing, install the stable `yzx` executable, and optionally install the desktop entry. User config remains under `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/`; generated state remains under `~/.local/share/yazelix`.
close_reason: Install UX decision recorded and follow-on implementation identified

title: yzx update devenv should also sync the devenv lock input
description:
## Problem

`yzx update devenv` only runs `nix profile install` to update the devenv CLI binary, but does not update the implicit `devenv` input in `devenv.lock`. This leaves the lock pinned to an older module revision, causing devenv to print a version-skew warning on every `devenv shell` / `yzx env`:

    ✨ devenv 2.0.7 is newer than devenv input (2.0.6) in devenv.lock. Run 'devenv update' to sync.

The devenv input (`github:cachix/devenv?dir=src/modules`) is mandatory — devenv injects it unconditionally in its generated flake. It cannot be removed. The maintainer-facing `yzx dev update devenv` already calls `devenv update devenv` to sync the lock, but the user-facing command does not.

## Proposed Fix

After `yzx update devenv` successfully installs/updates the CLI, also run `devenv update devenv` in the yazelix directory to re-lock the devenv modules input to match the new CLI version.

### Location

`nushell/scripts/core/yazelix.nu`, `yzx update devenv` command (lines ~399-476). After the `nix profile install` succeeds, add:

```nushell
# Sync the devenv lock input to match the new CLI
let yazelix_dir = (get_yazelix_runtime_dir)
cd $yazelix_dir
^(resolve_preferred_devenv_path) update devenv
```

### Alternatives considered

- `devenv.warnOnNewVersion = false` — suppresses the warning but hides legitimate drift. Not a real fix.
- Telling users to run `devenv update devenv` manually — bad UX, the whole point of `yzx update devenv` is a single command.
- Dropping the devenv input — impossible, devenv requires it for module evaluation.

## Acceptance Criteria

- After `yzx update devenv`, the devenv lock input matches the CLI version (no skew warning on next `yzx env`).
- If the lock sync fails, print a warning but don't fail the overall command (the CLI update already succeeded).
close_reason: Obsolete after the runtime-owned devenv cleanup removed yzx update devenv; the remaining version-skew architecture work is tracked in yazelix-15bx.

title: Update installation and user-facing docs for non-clone Yazelix runtime installs
description: Revise docs and help text so normal usage no longer assumes users cloned the repo into ~/.config/yazelix. Keep maintainer/source-checkout instructions explicit where they still apply.

title: Remove bundled Yazi runtime path assumptions about ~/.config/yazelix
description: Fix shipped Yazi configs, plugins, and related command launchers so they invoke Yazelix integration scripts through the runtime root rather than assuming a source checkout under ~/.config/yazelix.

title: Make shell, terminal, and desktop entrypoints resolve runtime assets without a repo clone
description: Remove launcher assumptions that Yazelix scripts live under ~/.config/yazelix. Terminal wrappers, desktop entry launchers, and startup entrypoints should resolve shipped runtime assets from the installed runtime root while keeping user config under the config root.
close_reason: Completed: POSIX launchers, devenv wrappers, and yzx launch now resolve shipped runtime assets through the runtime root instead of assuming ~/.config/yazelix is a repo checkout.

title: Define the package-ready Yazelix runtime/config root contract
description: Make the config root versus runtime asset root split explicit for non-clone installs. Clarify which environment variables and helpers own user config under ~/.config/yazelix versus shipped/runtime assets under the installed runtime location.
close_reason: Completed: added the runtime root contract spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: Define the package-ready Yazelix runtime/config root contract
description: Make the config root versus runtime asset root split explicit for non-clone installs. Clarify which environment variables and helpers own user config under ~/.config/yazelix versus shipped/runtime assets under the installed runtime location.
close_reason: Superseded by completed child yazelix-hac.2

title: Reduce high-signal nu-lint findings across the Yazelix Nushell codebase
description: Reduce the remaining high-signal nu-lint findings that still fire under Yazelix's curated .nu-lint.toml, without regressing intentional patterns or creating broad style churn. This bead is about paying down real correctness and maintainability warnings after the tool has already been adopted as an explicit maintainer command.
design: Work from the curated nu-lint surface, not upstream defaults. The starting point is the narrowed finding set that remained useful after disabling noisy groups and incompatible rules: unsafe_dynamic_record_access, do_not_compare_booleans, unused_parameter, check_typed_flag_before_use, unchecked_get_index, non_final_failure_check, unused_helper_functions, unused_variable, assign_then_return, fragile_last_exit_code, and unchecked_cell_path_index. Treat this as defect-reduction work, not style cleanup. Preserve intentional process-isolation patterns and repo conventions already defended by the nu-lint policy bead. Prefer fixing classes of warning where the resulting code is actually clearer or safer; if a rule still produces low-value churn during cleanup, capture that evidence and narrow the policy instead of forcing busywork.
acceptance_criteria: The curated nu-lint warning count is materially reduced on the Yazelix Nushell tree, with fixes concentrated on the highest-signal rule families. Every retained warning class is either intentionally deferred with a reason or judged not worth the churn. The cleanup does not re-enable noisy rules that were already rejected, and the resulting code remains consistent with Yazelix conventions and behavior. The bead records the before/after warning counts plus any rule-policy adjustments discovered during cleanup.
notes: Use yzx dev lint_nu as the maintained entrypoint. Initial useful finding mix from the policy bead was roughly: unsafe_dynamic_record_access 49, do_not_compare_booleans 23, unused_parameter 19, check_typed_flag_before_use 18, unchecked_get_index 11, non_final_failure_check 11, unused_helper_functions 10, unused_variable 9, assign_then_return 6, fragile_last_exit_code 4, unchecked_cell_path_index 2. Cleanup should probably proceed in small batches by warning family rather than repo-wide churn.

title: Add yzx dev lint_nu maintainer command
description: Add a lint_nu subcommand to yzx dev that runs nu-lint with the repo .nu-lint.toml config against the nushell/ directory. Should use yzx run for tool invocation.
close_reason: Added yzx dev lint_nu command to dev.nu. Supports --format (pretty/compact) and optional path arguments. Checks for nu-lint availability and .nu-lint.toml presence with clear errors.

title: Create repo-specific .nu-lint.toml config
description: Ship a .nu-lint.toml at repo root that disables 10 noisy groups and ~10 individual rules, keeping ~15 high-signal rules for dead code, runtime safety, and correctness.
close_reason: Shipped .nu-lint.toml with curated config: 11 groups disabled, 13 noisy rules suppressed, ~15 high-signal rules retained. Verified: 205 findings, all genuine signal.

title: Make config_schema.nu and config diagnostics consume the canonical config contract
description: Refactor schema comparison, enum validation, and startup config diagnostics so they consume the canonical main-config contract instead of maintaining another partial copy of allowed values and field knowledge.
design: Keep this focused on validation and diagnostics. The goal is not to auto-generate every error message, but to stop maintaining separate enum lists, known-field sets, and type expectations by hand. Preserve the distinction between structural schema checks, migration-aware diagnostics, and human-facing remediation text.
acceptance_criteria: config_schema.nu and the config diagnostics layer derive their maintained field knowledge and enum constraints from the canonical contract where appropriate. Validation drift between parser, schema checks, and diagnostics is materially reduced rather than redistributed.
close_reason: Completed: config_schema.nu and config diagnostics now derive maintained field knowledge and enum constraints from the canonical main-config contract.

title: Move rebuild-sensitivity metadata into the canonical config contract
description: Stop maintaining rebuild-required keys as a separate manual list by moving rebuild sensitivity into the canonical main-config contract and making config-state logic consume it directly.
design: This bead is specifically about the rebuild boundary, not all parser behavior. The contract should be the place that states whether a field change forces a new devenv profile build. config_state.nu should then consume that metadata instead of reading a parallel handwritten list. Keep non-config inputs such as devenv.lock and devenv.nix outside the field table unless there is a clear reason to unify them.
acceptance_criteria: The rebuild-sensitive config subset is derived from the canonical contract rather than a separate handwritten key list. config_state.nu still computes the correct rebuild hash boundary, but the maintained source of truth for field-level rebuild sensitivity lives in one place.
close_reason: Completed: config_state.nu now derives rebuild-sensitive field paths from the canonical contract artifact instead of a separate handwritten list.

title: Centralize pack catalog metadata separately from main config metadata
description: Create a separate canonical artifact for pack declarations and pack-surface metadata so pack defaults and declarations stop being repeated independently across the pack template, Home Manager, and future validation work.
design: Do not fold the pack catalog into the same flat field metadata table used for scalar config options. Packs are structured content with different evolution pressure. Reuse the same architectural idea as the main config contract, but keep a separate artifact and contract boundary so pack declarations, enabled defaults, and user package handling can evolve without distorting the main option model.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete standalone centralization path for pack metadata that does not overload the main config contract. The bead identifies the canonical artifact boundary for pack declarations, enabled defaults, and related pack-surface semantics.
close_reason: Completed: added separate canonical pack catalog contract artifact and loader helpers

title: Fix config metadata drift revealed by the centralization audit
description: Fix concrete config drift bugs uncovered during the metadata audit, starting with mismatches where the default TOML, Home Manager defaults, and Nushell parser fallbacks do not currently agree.
design: Treat this as a targeted cleanup lane for already-observed mismatches, not a substitute for the broader contract refactor. Start with the highest-signal drift cases, such as yazi.plugins default mismatch, and fix them in a way that improves current behavior even before the canonical contract work is finished.
acceptance_criteria: Known live drift cases uncovered by the metadata audit are fixed or explicitly triaged. Current defaults and parser fallbacks are no longer silently inconsistent for the tracked cases, with yazi.plugins being the first defended example.
close_reason: Completed: fixed the tracked yazi.plugins default drift and added regression coverage for the missing-field fallback path.

title: Make config_parser.nu consume the canonical config contract
description: Refactor the Nushell config parser so defaults, enum handling, numeric-range validation, and field mapping read from the canonical main-config contract instead of re-encoding those rules by hand.
design: Treat this as a consumer-migration bead, not as the place where the contract is invented. Preserve the current parser output shape where it is intentionally consumed elsewhere, but move machine-meaningful defaults and validation data behind the contract artifact. Prefer deleting hand-maintained fallback duplication over wrapping it in another helper layer.
acceptance_criteria: config_parser.nu reads the maintained defaults and validation metadata for supported fields from the canonical contract instead of hardcoding a parallel rule set. Existing parser behavior stays coherent, and any intentional output-key renames are represented explicitly in the contract.
close_reason: Completed: config_parser.nu now consumes the canonical main-config contract for defaults, parser mapping, and existing validation behavior.

title: Introduce canonical machine-readable contract for the main Yazelix config surface
description: Introduce one checked-in, language-neutral metadata artifact for the main Yazelix config surface so defaults, ownership, validation rules, and rebuild sensitivity stop being redefined independently across the TOML template, Home Manager, parser, schema logic, and validator work.
design: The first target is the main config surface only, not the pack catalog. The artifact should carry machine-meaningful semantics such as TOML path, owning surface, default value, type or shape, validation metadata, rebuild sensitivity, and mapping hints where parser or Home Manager names differ. Keep prose comments and integration-specific descriptions out of the first contract so the centralization stays about semantics, not documentation generation.
acceptance_criteria: There is a canonical checked-in metadata contract for the main Yazelix config surface. The contract is rich enough to drive downstream parser, schema, rebuild-sensitivity, and parity-validation work without forcing those consumers to rediscover defaults and validation rules from hand-written TOML or Nix code.
close_reason: Completed: added the canonical main-config metadata artifact and a loader module for downstream consumers.

title: Evaluate nu-lint and define Yazelix Nushell lint policy
description: Evaluate nu-lint against the Yazelix codebase, identify incompatible or noisy rules, and decide what role it deserves: pack-only availability, editor/LSP assistance, explicit maintainer command, pre-commit lane, CI lane, or rejection. This bead owns repo-fit evaluation and lint policy, not just checking that the tool exists.
design: Judge nu-lint by repo fit, but optimize for extracting real value rather than defaulting to a timid pack-only posture. Current evidence says the upstream default rule set is too noisy for blind enforcement: on real Yazelix files it reports parse errors for normal relative `use` imports, and several prominent rules conflict with repo conventions or priorities, especially `kebab_case_commands`, `add_doc_comment_exported_fn`, `missing_output_type`, and broad style rules like `string_may_be_bare`. Pack inclusion is already solved; this bead should now determine the highest-value maintained role nu-lint can play in Yazelix. That may be editor/LSP use, an explicit maintainer command, a tuned pre-commit lane, or a narrower CI lane, but only with a repo-specific config that disables or scopes noisy rules. Do not assume upstream defaults are acceptable, but do aggressively explore whether a constrained rule set yields strong signal.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision on nu-lints role in Yazelix that aims for maximum useful signal, not minimum ambition. The bead records the concrete repo-fit blockers already found: relative-import parse noise, rule conflicts with underscore-oriented naming, and high-churn style/documentation warnings. If adoption is chosen beyond pack availability, the bead defines the exact lane, the repo-specific config or disabled-rule policy, and why that scoped rule set is worth the maintenance cost. If broader adoption is rejected, the bead explains why no high-value narrowed configuration was good enough.
notes: Evaluation complete. Raw nu-lint produces 8565 violations (dominated by noise: string_may_be_bare 2466, kebab_case_commands 845, missing_output_type 784, nu_parse_error 433 from relative imports). With a curated .nu-lint.toml disabling 10 groups and ~10 noisy individual rules, output drops to ~170 findings of genuine signal: unsafe_dynamic_record_access (49), do_not_compare_booleans (23), unused_parameter (19), check_typed_flag_before_use (18), unchecked_get_index (11), non_final_failure_check (11), unused_helper_functions (10), unused_variable (9), assign_then_return (6), fragile_last_exit_code (4), unchecked_cell_path_index (2). Decision: adopt nu-lint as an explicit maintainer command (yzx dev lint_nu) with a repo-specific .nu-lint.toml. Not CI-gated because relative import parse errors are a structural upstream false-positive blocker. Not pre-commit because maintainer-triggered is the right ergonomic fit. redundant_nu_subprocess (39) disabled because all hits are intentional process-isolation patterns in test harnesses, menu dispatch, and script runners. Deliverables: .nu-lint.toml at repo root, yzx dev lint_nu command.
close_reason: Completed: nu-lint adopted as maintainer command (yzx dev lint_nu) with repo-specific .nu-lint.toml. Policy decision documented.

title: Add a dedicated Nushell tooling pack
description: Create a dedicated Nushell pack instead of overloading the generic maintainer pack. Initial scope should include nufmt and should consider nu-lint based on the outcome of the repo-fit evaluation. The pack bead is about package availability and ergonomics, not about forcing formatter or linter enforcement by itself.
design: Keep pack composition separate from policy enforcement. A first-party opt-in Nushell pack is valuable even if nu-lint remains experimental or nufmt adoption is staged. The pack should stay small and purposeful: prefer clear Nushell authoring and hygiene tools over a grab-bag of loosely related utilities. Any change here must preserve the normal Yazelix config model by keeping the pack available through both the pack sidecar/default config path and the Home Manager module, without creating a second-class configuration path.
acceptance_criteria: A dedicated Nushell pack exists in both Yazelix config surfaces, with justified initial contents and no ambiguity about what the pack is for. The pack can be enabled through direct config and Home Manager. Its contents do not silently imply CI enforcement or canonical formatter/linter status beyond what the separate policy beads decide.
close_reason: Completed

title: Evaluate nufmt and define Yazelix Nushell formatting policy
description: Decide whether nufmt should be the canonical formatter for Yazelix Nushell code, define the formatting policy and command surface, and determine how adoption should happen: repo-wide reformat, touched-files-only rollout, or rejection. This bead owns formatter policy, migration story, and enforcement decisions, not just tool installation.
design: The important question is not merely whether nufmt exists, but whether Yazelix wants one authoritative Nushell formatting policy. Resolve formatter ambiguity explicitly: if nufmt is adopted, decide whether it becomes the canonical formatter, where it is invoked from, and whether maintainers are expected to reformat the whole repo or only touched files. The rollout should preserve review quality and avoid accidental churn. If nufmt is rejected or deferred, capture the reasons so the repo does not drift into an undocumented quasi-policy.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit maintainers-facing decision on nufmt: adopt, defer, or reject. If adopted, the bead records the canonical invocation path, the enforcement lane if any, and the migration strategy for existing files. If deferred or rejected, the reasons and blocking concerns are captured clearly enough to prevent accidental partial adoption.
notes: Decision: do not adopt nufmt for Yazelix right now because the current formatter output was observed to rewrite at least one real Yazelix Nushell file into invalid syntax.
close_reason: Closed: current nufmt repo-fit evaluation found unsafe formatter output on real Yazelix Nushell files, so Yazelix is not adopting a Nushell formatting policy around nufmt right now

title: Decide mission-control tab vs tab-local agent sidebar
description: Decide whether Yazelix agent management should first ship as a dedicated mission-control tab or a tab-local sidebar surface, with current bias toward mission control as the higher-value and lower-hanging first slice.
design: Reason from scope first. A mission-control tab is probably the better first slice because it has more room for agent state, actions, and future orchestration, while avoiding premature sidebar overloading. Treat a tab-local agent sidebar as a possible later adjunct, not the default first target. This decision work should define which concerns are tab-local versus session-global, whether agents belong in a narrow side surface, and how a future sidebar would relate to the broader command-center story. Keep session-management sidebar ideas out of the first decision unless they materially change the surface choice.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented decision on whether the first real agent-management surface should be a mission-control tab, a tab-local agent sidebar, or both in staged order. The decision explicitly classifies which agent operations are tab-local versus global, explains why the chosen first surface is the better product slice, and leaves a clear follow-up path for any deferred sidebar idea without hardcoding the current sidebar system around Yazi forever.

title: Sidebar: isolate the reusable motion and visibility contract
description: Preserve and sharpen the reusable Rust-side sidebar contract so closed-sidebar-aware motions and visibility toggles can stand on their own apart from Yazelix launcher assumptions.
design: The left/right motion and sidebar visibility logic is already partly isolated in pure Rust contracts. Strengthen that boundary and keep it generic. The reusable contract should not depend on Yazi-specific launcher details, and it should minimize editor-specific assumptions where possible.
acceptance_criteria: The reusable sidebar and motion contract is clearly isolated and defended. Horizontal motions ignore the sidebar when closed and treat it as a valid focus target when open. The contract is not entangled with Yazi-only launcher details.

title: Sidebar: separate plugin commands from keybinding policy
description: Treat sidebar commands such as toggle and focus as the stable command surface and keep keybindings as docs/examples rather than plugin semantics.
design: The plugin command surface already wants to be the stable contract, while keybindings belong to generated config and user policy. Make that separation explicit before any extraction attempt so the later standalone story is about commands, not about copying Yazelix default keymaps.
acceptance_criteria: Sidebar command surfaces such as toggle and focus are documented or treated as the stable contract. Keybindings remain configurable policy in Zellij config, with examples rather than hard requirements.

title: Popup: make managed popup identity explicit and configurable inside Yazelix
description: Replace the remaining hardcoded managed popup identity assumptions with an explicit internal contract so popup selection is not implicitly coupled only to `yzx_popup` and `yzx_popup_program.nu`.
design: Today the popup identity contract is split across `yzx popup`, `yzx_popup_program.nu`, and the popup-runner selector. Make that identity explicit: pane title, wrapper marker, and any downstream selector inputs should be treated as a contract rather than incidental matching. Keep this internal to Yazelix first.
acceptance_criteria: Managed popup identity is represented explicitly in code rather than only as scattered hardcoded string assumptions. The popup-runner selector and wrapper flow agree on one clear identity contract. Existing popup behavior still works.

title: Popup: formalize the argv/cwd/geometry adapter contract
description: Turn the existing popup command, cwd, and geometry behavior into an explicit internal adapter contract that downstream extraction could reuse later.
design: The code already resolves popup argv, workspace-root cwd, and popup width/height through config parsing and Home Manager parity. Make that shape explicit as the internal adapter contract before any extraction discussion. Prefer documenting and defending the existing contract over inventing broader popup abstractions.
acceptance_criteria: The popup argv command, cwd policy, and geometry are treated as an explicit internal contract. The current config path and Home Manager path remain in sync. The behavior is documented or defended clearly enough that later extraction would not need to rediscover the contract.

title: Popup: harden managed single-instance regression coverage
description: Add or strengthen focused tests around the managed popup contract so later refactors or extraction attempts do not regress single-instance behavior.
design: Keep this focused on the contracts that matter: one managed popup instance, configured argv command, workspace-root cwd preference, and configured geometry. Avoid generic command-discovery tests.
acceptance_criteria: Focused regression coverage defends the managed popup identity and single-instance behavior, the popup argv command contract, the cwd policy, and popup geometry behavior.
close_reason: Covered by popup command/wrapper tests and transient-pane contract coverage

title: Sidebar: make the sidebar launcher configurable inside Yazelix
description: Remove the assumption that the managed sidebar is always launched through `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` so the sidebar surface can become generic before any extraction attempt.
design: The current layouts and fragments still hardwire a Yazi launcher script for the sidebar. Generalize this inside Yazelix first: the launcher command should become an explicit config/generation concern rather than a fixed Yazi path baked into every layout.
acceptance_criteria: Generated layouts no longer inherently depend on `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` as the only sidebar launcher path. The sidebar launch surface is configurable inside Yazelix while preserving current behavior by default.

title: Extract a standalone sidebar plugin with sidebar-aware motions
description: Internal-first roadmap for eventually extracting a generic sidebar plugin with sidebar-aware motions. The reusable motion logic is already partially isolated, but the sidebar launcher and generated layouts are still tightly coupled to Yazelix-managed Yazi behavior.
design: Grounded in current code: sidebar width is already config-driven and the closed-sidebar-aware motion logic already lives in reusable Rust contracts. The main blocker is launcher and layout coupling: generated layouts still hardwire `launch_sidebar_yazi.nu` and a managed `sidebar` pane running Yazi. Do not jump straight to a standalone plugin. First make the sidebar launcher configurable inside Yazelix, isolate the reusable sidebar and motion contract from Yazelix-specific Yazi/editor assumptions, and keep plugin commands as the stable contract while treating keybindings as docs/examples only. Extraction only makes sense if those steps leave a generic sidebar contract behind.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix first has an explicit internal sidebar contract covering configurable sidebar program, sidebar width, toggle/focus commands, and horizontal motions that ignore a closed sidebar. The launcher and layouts are no longer inherently tied to Yazi-only behavior. After those steps, there is enough evidence to decide whether standalone extraction is justified.
notes: Terminology guardrail: keep `sidebar` as the generic side-surface term in architecture and plugin-contract work. Use `file tree` or `Yazi file tree` only when referring specifically to the current Yazi-backed surface in user-facing wording. This matters because future side surfaces may include agents or other tab-local tools, and the sidebar abstraction should stay broader than the current file-tree implementation.

title: Extract `zellij_popup_runner` into a standalone managed popup plugin
description: Internal-first roadmap for eventually extracting the managed popup flow into a standalone Zellij plugin. Today the popup command and geometry are already config-driven inside Yazelix, but popup identity and selector assumptions are still hardwired to Yazelix names and wrapper flow.
design: Grounded in current code: `yzx popup` already owns argv command resolution, workspace-root cwd selection, and popup geometry through config parsing and Home Manager parity. The remaining coupling lives in the popup identity contract: `yzx_popup`, `yzx_popup_program.nu`, and the popup-runner selector logic. Do not jump straight to a standalone plugin. First make popup identity explicit and configurable inside Yazelix, formalize the adapter contract around argv/cwd/geometry, and harden the managed single-instance tests. Extraction only makes sense if those steps leave behind a clean generic contract with little or no Yazelix-specific shell glue.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix first has an explicit internal popup contract covering managed popup identity, argv command execution, cwd policy, and geometry. Popup identity is no longer implicitly tied to hardcoded wrapper names alone. Regression coverage defends the single-instance managed popup behavior. After those steps, there is enough evidence to decide whether standalone extraction is justified.

title: Evaluate whether Television belongs in Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether the Television fuzzy finder (tv) adds enough value to Yazelix to justify packaging, integration, or documentation. The question is not whether Television is interesting in general, but whether it improves Yazelix workflows enough to deserve a maintained place in the product.
design: Television is a fast terminal fuzzy finder with channels, shell integration, and editor integrations. Judge it against Yazelix's actual workflow surfaces: Yazi, Helix, popup flows, shell search, and any future picker surfaces. Determine whether Television would be redundant with existing tools, complementary as a generic fuzzy-search surface, or valuable only as an optional pack tool. Prefer a concrete go, no-go, or optional-integration decision over vague enthusiasm for another terminal tool.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision on whether Television should be ignored, documented, added to a pack, or integrated more deeply into Yazelix. The bead records which user workflows it would improve, what it duplicates, and what the lightest worthwhile adoption shape would be if it is kept.

title: Define Yazelix Nushell tooling strategy
description: Umbrella for deciding how Yazelix should package and govern Nushell-specific maintainer tooling. Scope includes a dedicated Nushell pack, nufmt formatting policy, and nu-lint lint policy. Keep tool availability separate from enforcement: shipping tools in an opt-in pack must not by itself decide canonical formatter status, maintainer workflows, or CI gates.
design: Reason from first principles: Yazelix needs a coherent Nushell-tooling story, not an ad hoc accumulation of commands. There are three separate questions that should not be collapsed into one: 1. what Nushell-specific tools belong in a first-party opt-in pack; 2. whether nufmt becomes the canonical formatter and what rollout it should have; and 3. whether nu-lint is repo-compatible enough to justify any maintained command surface. The parent bead should stay an umbrella for those planning decisions rather than directly implementing a single tool addition.
acceptance_criteria: The subtree under this bead cleanly separates pack composition, formatting policy, and lint policy; each child bead has an explicit boundary and success criteria; and maintainers can make progress on one area without accidentally forcing the others. The parent can close once the dedicated pack direction and the nufmt and nu-lint policy decisions are each resolved.
close_reason: Completed: all three strategy decisions resolved — Nushell pack shipped, nufmt rejected, nu-lint adopted as maintainer command.

title: Evaluate whether Yazelix should drop Foot support because it is not on macOS
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix should stop treating Foot as a meaningful supported terminal option because it is not available on macOS, unlike the other first-class terminal candidates under consideration.
design: Reason from the actual support matrix and product goals rather than from terminal popularity alone. Compare Foot against the terminals Yazelix most plausibly wants to support across platforms, especially macOS-relevant options like WezTerm and Alacritty. The key decision is whether Foot still earns documentation, config generation, testing effort, and maintainer attention despite being Linux-only. If Foot remains supported, the bead should justify that cost explicitly. If not, it should define the de-support path cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision to keep, demote, or drop Foot support. The decision records the platform and maintenance tradeoffs, how Foot compares to WezTerm and Alacritty for Yazelix's goals, and what documentation, config, or support-matrix changes would follow from the chosen direction.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Evaluate whether sesh adds meaningful value to Yazelix session workflows
description: Evaluate what sesh would actually add to Yazelix compared with the current Zellij and workspace flow, and decide whether it is worth integrating, documenting, or ignoring.
design: Compare sesh against the problems Yazelix already has or may soon have around session discovery, startup, switching, and saved workflows. Do not evaluate it as an abstract cool tool. The question is whether sesh solves a real user problem better than current Yazelix or Zellij surfaces, and if so whether integration should be first-party, optional, or purely documented.
acceptance_criteria: There is a go, no-go, or optional-integration decision on sesh, grounded in concrete session-workflow use cases. The bead records what sesh does better, what it duplicates, and what integration shape would make sense if it is worth adopting.

title: evaluate splitting some yazelix builting zellij plugins to standalone plugins
description: Imported GitHub issue #485.
close_reason: Superseded by child issues yazelix-l968.1 / #503 and yazelix-l968.2 / #504

title: clone yazi zellij and helix near yazelix so it's easier for AI to read their code
description: Imported GitHub issue #486.
close_reason: Imported closed GitHub issue #486.

title: Use a floating Yazi picker from Helix when the sidebar is disabled
description: Narrower slice of the broader floating Yazi picker feature: provide a picker flow that remains useful when the normal sidebar is disabled, likely using a Zellij floating pane instead of depending on the persistent side surface.
design: Keep this aligned with the broader floating-picker direction instead of creating a second unrelated Helix-Yazi picker implementation. The key question is how the picker should behave when the file-tree sidebar is unavailable: invocation path, floating-pane UX, selected-path return, and cancellation behavior. This should remain compatible with a future general floating picker from Helix.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear design or implementation path for using a floating Yazi picker from Helix when the sidebar is disabled, and it remains aligned with the broader picker feature rather than diverging into a duplicate flow.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Clarify sidebar vs file tree terminology
description: Clarify Yazelix terminology so `sidebar` remains the generic side-surface term while `file tree` is used for the current Yazi-specific surface where that wording is clearer.
design: Do not perform a literal repo-wide rename from `sidebar` to `filetree`. The current codebase uses `sidebar` as a pane role and workspace contract term, and that should remain the generic abstraction because future side surfaces may not be file trees. The right change is terminology cleanup: preserve `sidebar` for the architectural slot and command surface, while using `file tree` or `Yazi file tree` in user-facing docs or copy when specifically referring to the current Yazi-backed side surface.
acceptance_criteria: The issue is reframed as terminology clarification rather than a mass rename. User-facing wording is clearer about when Yazelix means the generic sidebar surface versus the current Yazi file tree. Internal architectural and plugin-contract language is not renamed in a way that would block future non-Yazi sidebar surfaces.

title: Evaluate whether yzx cwd should be removed, narrowed, or kept
description: Evaluate whether the yzx cwd command still deserves to exist as a first-class command surface, and if so what narrower contract it should have.
design: Reason from user-facing intent rather than implementation habit. Determine what real job yzx cwd is doing today: tab naming, workspace retargeting, cwd synchronization, or a compatibility shim for older flows. Then compare that against overlapping surfaces and recent bugs. If the command is confusing or too easy to misuse, prefer narrowing or deleting it with a clear migration story rather than preserving it out of inertia.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit decision to keep, narrow, rename, or remove yzx cwd. The bead records the user-facing contract if it survives, or the replacement and migration story if it does not. Any decision accounts for overlaps with newer workspace and tab-cwd flows.
close_reason: Completed: keep yzx cwd as the explicit current-tab workspace-retarget command. The narrowed contract is not "generic navigation" or "open a new project tab"; it is tab-local workspace-root mutation plus managed editor/sidebar sync. Docs and command descriptions were tightened around that contract, while a future warp-style new-tab flow remains a separate feature idea rather than a replacement.

title: Keep Zellij pane cwd aligned with restart directory
description: Imported GitHub issue #500. Restart currently boots the sidebar from the requested directory but was rewriting Zellij's own default cwd to HOME, so native new panes ignored the restart directory.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub

title: Revisit override-layout via owned explicit-run pane creation
description: Capture the future path for returning to Zellij override-layout transitions after the current rollback to layout-family swapping. The experiment showed that override_layout only becomes viable if Yazelix owns the relevant pane-creation surfaces and opens panes with stable explicit run instructions instead of default-shell panes that Zellij normalizes to invoked_with = None. Scope this as design + implementation exploration, not an immediate bugfix. Evaluate which pane creation surfaces Yazelix can realistically own (Alt+m, pane-mode creation, split flows), whether built-in Zellij Run/NewTiledPane/NewStackedPane actions are enough, and what exact contract would let Alt y / Alt [ ] preserve live panes under override_layout without duplicating panes or sidebar state.
design: Treat this as the current explicit-run path back toward override-layout after the earlier rollback. The core question is whether Yazelix can own enough pane-creation surfaces to preserve live panes under override_layout by using stable explicit run instructions instead of anonymous shell panes. This should remain a design and contract bead first, not a promise that override-layout is immediately ready to ship again.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete contract for what Yazelix would need to own in order to make override-layout viable again, including pane creation surfaces, run-instruction identity, and layout-transition invariants. If the path is still too costly or constrained, that is captured explicitly instead of leaving override-layout in limbo.

title: Reduce config-surface duplication between Nushell and Nix
description: Reduce unnecessary duplication between the Nushell and Nix config surfaces after the canonical config and launch-profile contract is defined. The goal is not total collapse into one file, but fewer parallel sources of truth and fewer places where the same runtime choice must be expressed twice.
design: This work should follow the canonical config-surface contract rather than guess its own target. First identify which values genuinely need dual representation across Nushell and Nix and which ones are accidental duplication. Then delete redundant surfaces before optimizing the survivors. Prefer one clear source-of-truth plus explicit derivation or validation over two independently maintained copies of the same intent.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete duplication-reduction plan grounded in the canonical config and launch-profile contract. The bead records which duplicated fields should be unified, which must remain mirrored with validation, and which existing duplication is acceptable. Follow-up implementation work is narrower than this planning bead.
close_reason: Completed: documented the config metadata centralization plan and classified which duplication should be unified, validated, or kept.

title: Add validator for config surface and launch-profile parity
description: Add a validator that enforces the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract once that contract is defined, so drift is caught mechanically instead of by maintainer memory.
design: This bead should not invent the contract; it should defend the canonical config-surface and launch-profile ownership model from yazelix-b16x together with the machine-readable main-config contract introduced by yazelix-0oj1.1. Keep the validator high-signal: check the maintained parity and ownership rules that actually matter, and avoid noisy pseudo-invariants that mainly create churn. The right validator may be structural, config-based, or generation-based depending on what the canonical contract ultimately exposes.
acceptance_criteria: There is a validator for the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract, and it checks the maintained source-of-truth invariants that the contract explicitly requires. The validator is justified by the contract rather than by general anxiety about drift.
close_reason: Completed: added config surface and launch-profile contract validator with CI and pre-commit integration

title: Specify canonical config surface and launch-profile contract
description: Define the canonical config surfaces and launch-profile contract for Yazelix so downstream work can stop guessing which configuration path owns what behavior and how launch entrypoints are supposed to compose.
design: This is a high-leverage contract bead. Clarify the relationship between direct config, Home Manager, generated runtime state, launch profiles, and any environment-specific entrypoints. The goal is not just documentation; it is a stable contract that downstream beads can depend on when reducing duplication or adding validators. Prefer sharp ownership rules and explicit derivation paths over fuzzy precedence.
acceptance_criteria: There is a documented canonical contract for config surfaces and launch profiles that downstream work can reference. The contract states what each surface owns, how launch profiles compose with configuration, and where parity or validation is required. Dependent beads such as duplication reduction and parity validation can proceed without redefining the model.
close_reason: Completed: added the canonical config-surface and launch-profile contract spec and linked it from the architecture map.

title: add yzx import zellij/yazi to import native configs into yazelix-managed overrides
description: Add a short import flow that brings native Zellij and Yazi config files under Yazelix management instead of making users manually copy files into the managed override paths.
design:
Keep the command surface short and product-shaped: use `yzx import zellij` and `yzx import yazi` instead of a generic `yzx cp` command or a 4-word `yzx config import ...` flow. Treat this as an adoption/import path, not a reset path: the source is the native config location and the destination is Yazelix's managed override location.

For Zellij, import only `~/.config/zellij/config.kdl` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl`. For Yazi, import only the override files Yazelix already has a clear contract for: `yazi.toml`, `keymap.toml`, and `init.lua` from `~/.config/yazi/` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/`. Do not try to import plugin directories, flavors, caches, or other broader state in v1.

Fail fast when the managed destination already exists so users do not silently clobber their Yazelix-managed files. Allow an explicit `--force` path, but back up any overwritten managed file first and keep the messaging clear about what was imported, skipped, backed up, or overwritten.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx import zellij` imports `~/.config/zellij/config.kdl` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/zellij/config.kdl`. `yzx import yazi` imports `yazi.toml`, `keymap.toml`, and `init.lua` from `~/.config/yazi/` into `~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/yazi/`. Missing native source files fail clearly instead of silently succeeding. Existing managed destination files are not overwritten unless `--force` is passed, and the `--force` path writes backups before replacing managed files. The docs point users to the import commands as the supported way to adopt native Zellij or Yazi config into Yazelix-managed overrides. Add focused command-level regression coverage for the happy path and overwrite protection.
close_reason: Completed

Clarify the value to users and contributors, the hosting/update burden, and whether the public project graph should be live, periodically generated, or linked out to an existing viewer. Prefer the simplest surface that still gives meaningful roadmap visibility.
