Follow through on the broader packaging/path-model work so command entrypoints, helper scripts, and validation paths resolve from the installed runtime or repo checkout explicitly instead of relying on HOME-relative repo paths.
close_reason: Completed: removed CI symlink bootstrap, resolved runtime helpers/tests via YAZELIX_DIR, and verified nu nushell/scripts/dev/test_yzx_commands.nu passes against a temp fixture without ~/.config bootstrap

title: Make adding custom Zellij layouts straightforward
description: Imported GitHub issue #438 after verifying that custom Zellij layouts are only easy in a narrow no-sidebar case and are otherwise blocked by hardcoded layout generation, hardcoded sidebar-family knowledge in the pane orchestrator, and stale docs.
design: Treat this as a structural customization problem rather than a docs-only paper cut. The supported path for adding layouts should be explicit: layout files must be discoverable or registered through one clear mechanism, sidebar layout families must expose enough metadata for the pane orchestrator to switch and toggle them without hardcoded family names, and unsupported layouts must fail fast with clear errors instead of silently disappearing. The documentation should reflect the actual current filenames and the real extension workflow.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can add a supported custom layout without touching multiple unrelated code paths by hand; generated Zellij config picks up the new layout through the documented mechanism; sidebar-aware layouts either work through a documented manifest or naming scheme or fail fast with a clear error; docs describe the current layout files and the actual extension workflow; automated tests cover the supported custom-layout path.
close_reason: Made top-level Zellij layout discovery automatic, documented the supported customization boundary, and added regression coverage for custom layout generation.

title: Add yzx dev bump for version and tag automation
description: Add a maintainer-facing yzx dev bump command that automates the release bump flow instead of doing version edits, commits, and tags by hand.
design: Implement a maintainer-only yzx dev bump command that computes or accepts the next version, updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu so YAZELIX_VERSION matches the intended tag, creates a dedicated version bump commit, and creates the matching git tag. The command should fail fast on a dirty worktree, an existing conflicting tag, or a version mismatch between the file and the requested tag. The flow should be explicit and deterministic so maintainers do not need to remember the manual release steps.
acceptance_criteria: A maintainer can run yzx dev bump with an explicit version and the command updates nushell/scripts/utils/constants.nu to that version; the command creates a dedicated git commit for the version bump with a predictable message; the command creates the matching git tag after the commit succeeds; the command refuses to run when the git worktree is dirty; the command refuses to run when the requested tag already exists or conflicts with repository state; the command verifies that YAZELIX_VERSION matches the tag it creates; the command has automated tests covering the happy path and failure cases.
close_reason: Completed: added yzx dev bump as a clean-worktree maintainer release helper that rotates Unreleased release metadata into a real version, resets the Unreleased placeholder, updates YAZELIX_VERSION, updates the README version marker, creates a dedicated commit, and creates the matching git tag. Automated tests now cover the happy path plus dirty-worktree and existing-tag failures.

title: Document Alt+m cwd regression spiral after refactor
description: Record the regression sequence where Alt+m pane creation was refactored away from the known-good orchestrator path and reintroduced the Zellij cwd bug, leading to panes opening in HOME, stale sidebar-state confusion, and crashing helper-pane experiments.
design: Keep this as a postmortem-style regression bead linked conceptually to yazelix-2ex.1.7.5. The key invariant is that Yazelix must resolve the live sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate pane creation to the pane orchestrator via open_terminal_in_cwd. Refactors must not replace that path with zellij new-pane/run --cwd helpers, explicit shell launches, or command panes. Preserve the later good fixes from this incident: immediate sidebar-state cache refresh after open-from-Yazi sync, pruning stale sidebar session files, and avoiding launcher cwd poisoning from ~/.config/yazelix.
acceptance_criteria: The bead notes capture the failure chain, the known-good contract, and the forbidden fallback paths. Future work touching Alt+m or pane cwd should be able to use this bead to avoid repeating the regression.
notes: Regression chain on 2026-03-15: a refactor replaced the orchestrator-owned Alt+m pane-open flow from a7b5716/yazelix-2ex.1.7.5 with helper-driven zellij new-pane --cwd and later zellij run --cwd shell launches. Symptoms included panes opening in /home/lucca or ~/.config/yazelix despite correct target resolution, stale sidebar-state files masking the real issue, helper crashes, and a wider session cwd spiral after layout churn. Confirmed root cause remained the upstream Zellij cwd bug already documented in zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final recovery: restore open_workspace_terminal.nu to call run_pane_orchestrator_command_raw "open_terminal_in_cwd" with the resolved sidebar/workspace cwd, keep sidebar-state freshness fixes, and treat the orchestrator path as the only supported Alt+m implementation.
close_reason: Documented the Alt+m regression spiral, the repeated Zellij cwd bug trigger, and the required orchestrator-owned recovery path.

title: Improve Helix support for Ghostty cursor effects
description: Track work needed to improve Ghostty cursor-effect support when using Helix inside Yazelix. Cover which trail effects currently fail or degrade in Helix, what editor/runtime limitations are involved, and what Yazelix-side adaptations or fallbacks could close the gap with Neovim support.
design: Treat this as a capability-gap analysis between Neovim-style cursor effects and the Helix environment Yazelix actually supports. Identify which Ghostty effects fail in Helix, whether the limitation is in Ghostty triggers, Helix state visibility, or Yazelix-side integration, and whether the right answer is parity, graceful degradation, or explicit unsupported status. Avoid promising full parity if the editor/runtime stack cannot expose the right events reliably.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear support decision for Ghostty cursor effects in Helix: which effects work, which do not, why, and what Yazelix-side adaptation or fallback is justified. If there is a viable improvement path, the bead leaves behind narrower implementation work instead of keeping the problem generic.

title: Add Ghostty cursor effects for Zellij pane changes
description: Explore extending the Ghostty cursor-effect system beyond editor mode changes so Yazelix can trigger visual feedback on Zellij pane focus changes, such as ripple/boom effects when switching panes. Cover feasibility, event plumbing, and whether Ghostty shader triggers can be driven cleanly from Zellij/Yazelix without breaking existing cursor behavior.
design: Treat this as an event-plumbing and UX-value question, not just shader enthusiasm. Determine which pane-focus events Yazelix can observe reliably, how those events could trigger Ghostty effects without colliding with editor-mode effects, and whether the result would actually improve navigation clarity rather than becoming distracting visual noise. Prefer a small number of meaningful pane-change effects over an open-ended effect buffet.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear feasibility and UX-value decision for Ghostty cursor effects on Zellij pane changes, including which events can be observed, what effect shapes are worth trying, and whether the feature should move forward, stay experimental, or be dropped.

title: Fix skip_welcome_screen=false being ignored during interactive launch
description: The core.skip_welcome_screen setting is currently being ignored for interactive Yazelix launch flows. Users with skip_welcome_screen = false still do not see the welcome UI.
design: Trace so far: the interactive launch path still calls show_welcome from nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix_inner.nu, so the setting is not being ignored because the UI code was removed. The likely regression is environment leakage: launch wrappers pass --skip-welcome into the devenv/bootstrap layer, which sets YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME=true, and show_welcome treats that as test_mode. The most suspicious paths are nushell/scripts/core/start_yazelix.nu, nushell/scripts/utils/environment_bootstrap.nu, and nushell/scripts/yzx/launch.nu. The likely fix is to keep bootstrap welcome suppression for environment setup while clearing or scoping YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME before the real interactive inner session starts.
acceptance_criteria: With core.skip_welcome_screen = false, interactive Yazelix launch shows the welcome screen again. With core.skip_welcome_screen = true, the welcome screen is skipped and the info is logged as before. The fix must not regress env-only, one-shot command, or sweep/test flows that intentionally use YAZELIX_SKIP_WELCOME.
close_reason: Cannot reproduce

title: Replace local pi_agent_rust packaging with upstream flake
description:
Track the follow-on work for removing Yazelix's local `pi_agent_rust` derivation once upstream ships a real flake surface. Codex does not need a matching bead because `openai/codex` already exposes `flake.nix` and `flake.lock`.

Scope:
- monitor upstream `pi_agent_rust` flake support
- when a flake exists, replace the local `buildRustPackage` path in `devenv.nix`
- prefer consuming upstream flake outputs instead of carrying custom packaging in Yazelix
- keep the current `pi_rust` user-facing alias stable while changing only the packaging source under the hood

Success means Yazelix no longer needs to maintain a local Nix package definition for `pi_agent_rust`
close_reason: Obsolete after removing pi_rust support from Yazelix

title: support for https://github.com/just-every/code and pi
close_reason: Completed

title: Add yzx keys command for Yazelix bindings and tool discoverability
description: Add a small `yzx keys` surface that shows Yazelix-owned keybindings by default and offers tool-specific discoverability helpers like `yzx keys yazi` and `yzx keys hx`. Keep it intentionally narrow: the default output should focus on the Yazelix layer, while per-tool subcommands explain how to view upstream keybindings inside Yazi or Helix instead of duplicating their full keymaps.
close_reason: Implemented yzx keys with Yazelix defaults plus yazi and hx discoverability helpers

title: Review docs on IDE integration and document both default-terminal modes
description: Review the editor and IDE integration docs and document both recommended terminal-profile setups: one where yzx env is the default integrated terminal and one where it remains an explicit non-default profile.
design: Treat this as a docs-clarity pass grounded in real user confusion. The docs should show both modes cleanly for VS Code or Cursor: a vanilla terminal as the default with an optional Yazelix profile, and a setup where the integrated terminal defaults directly to yzx env. Keep the examples consistent with the current command flow and explain the tradeoff between preserving a raw shell and dropping straight into the Yazelix environment.
acceptance_criteria: The IDE integration docs clearly present both supported VS Code/Cursor terminal configurations and when to choose each one. The JSON examples are current, minimal, and internally consistent. Validate the documented commands or config snippets and leave logs or notes showing which terminal profile becomes default in each example.
close_reason: Completed

title: Investigate Ctrl+y conflict between Yazelix and Yazi picker recommendations
description: Investigate the possible conflict between Yazelix's current keybinding model and Yazi's documented Tips and Tricks recommendation for using Ctrl+y to drive Yazi as a picker from Zellij/Helix.
design: Treat this as an input-model and docs-coherence investigation, not just a keybinding bikeshed. Verify what Yazi's website currently recommends for picker usage from Zellij or Helix, how that overlaps with Yazelix's existing Ctrl+y or related bindings, what escape sequences and ownership layers are involved, and whether Yazelix should preserve the upstream recommendation, intentionally diverge from it, or document a different integrated workflow. The goal is to avoid a confusing mismatch between Yazelix and the mental model users will pick up from Yazi's own docs.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear documented decision about the Ctrl+y flow for Yazi-as-picker usage in Yazelix. If the current mapping is wrong or confusing, update the keybinding/docs accordingly. Add validation or end-to-end coverage for the affected key path and log the emitted sequence, target action, and final documented behavior.
close_reason: Aligned Yazelix keybindings with Ctrl+y => Yazi mental model and removed stale Space-y Helix default

title: Fix pane drift by adding pane pinning mechanics
description: Add pane pinning mechanics so the managed editor pane keeps a stable position instead of drifting around the stack during normal Yazelix workspace activity. The main user-facing goal is to keep the editor anchored as the top pane in the relevant stack so the workspace remains predictable.
design: Treat this as workspace-structure behavior, not just a one-off layout patch. Define what it means for a pane to be pinned, which pane types deserve pinning first, when pinning should be re-applied after layout changes or pane creation, and how this interacts with user-initiated pane moves. The first target is the managed editor pane: it should remain the top pane in its stack unless the user explicitly breaks that contract. Avoid hidden fights against intentional user actions, but prevent routine Yazelix actions from causing gradual pane drift.
acceptance_criteria: The managed editor pane no longer drifts out of its intended top position during normal workspace operations. Add unit tests for the pinning decision logic and pane-order helpers, integration tests for pane-target selection, and end-to-end scripts that exercise pane creation, focus changes, workspace retargeting, and layout transitions with detailed logs of pane ids, stack order, pin state, and any re-anchoring actions.

title: add option of silent refresh on a specific time (like 4am)
description: Imported GitHub issue #389.
close_reason: Too hard and out of scope for Yazelix right now

title: Add eye-watering welcome screen
description: Redesign the default Yazelix welcome screen so it has a visually striking hero treatment without making startup feel heavy or slow. The default experience should feel beautiful, surprising, and recognizably Yazelix instead of just showing plain welcome text.
design: Preferred direction: use an abstract animated visual, gif, or lightweight animation that frames the welcome content rather than obscuring it. The motion should either stay out of the text area or gracefully darken or fade once the reveal is done so the welcome information remains readable. The Yazelix logo should play a central role if possible, ideally through an animation that resolves into the final Yazelix mark. A strong direction would be a logo-based animation that nods to the Yazi, Helix, and Zellij influences already present in the current logo while landing on the final Yazelix icon form.
acceptance_criteria: The default welcome screen has a visually distinctive animation or image path that feels fast, branded, and readable, with a static fallback users can choose explicitly. The final design should work in narrow and split layouts, preserve welcome-text readability, and feel like Yazelix rather than a random screensaver. Add unit tests for animation-selection or layout-decision helpers, end-to-end startup/render validation for normal and narrow layouts, performance measurements for startup impact, and detailed capture logs for whichever animation or asset pipeline is chosen.
notes: Candidate visual directions: a magician casting colorful ASCII magic; a colored liquid animation, ASCII or non-ASCII depending quality/perf tradeoffs; a Game of Life style simulation; particle-life style motion similar to OfficialCodeNoodles/Particle-Life-Simulation; and continuous-life variants like Lenia or SmoothLife for a more fluid organic feel. Longer-term direction: ship a small curated set of beautiful default welcome animations and choose one at random on each start. Existing context: Yazelix already has a star animation that looks nice, but it degrades badly in vertical splits or lower-width layouts, so any new direction should adapt better to constrained dimensions. Imported overlap from GitHub issue #263: replacing the current welcome art with the Yazelix logo and making the result work well on half screens and narrow layouts is part of this same feature, not a separate track.

title: yzx tutor
description: Add a tutor surface under `yzx tutor` that helps users learn both the native tools Yazelix ships and the Yazelix-specific workflow layered on top of them.
design: Split the feature in two parts. Part one: thin wrappers for native tutors that already exist, specifically `yzx tutor hx` -> `hx --tutor` and `yzx tutor nu` -> Nushell `tutor`. Part two: Yazelix-specific markdown tutors for `yzx`, `yazi`, and `zellij`. `yzx tutor yzx` should teach the Yazelix mental model and core commands. `yzx tutor yazi` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Yazi experience compared with vanilla Yazi. `yzx tutor zellij` should explain what changes in the Yazelix-managed Zellij experience compared with vanilla Zellij. Prefer opening concise curated markdown guides over building a live demo or interactive checklist system first.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx tutor hx` and `yzx tutor nu` launch the existing upstream tutors. `yzx tutor yzx`, `yzx tutor yazi`, and `yzx tutor zellij` open maintained Yazelix-specific guides. The tutor surface feels coherent: every subcommand either teaches a real upstream tutor or opens curated Yazelix-specific onboarding content. Avoid shallow website-link wrappers with no Yazelix adaptation. Add unit tests for command routing and target resolution, docs-existence checks for the markdown guides, and end-to-end smoke tests that log the resolved tutor path or launched command.
close_reason: Completed: shipped the yzx tutor MVP with a Yazelix overview plus hx/nu upstream tutor wrappers; richer structured tutor flow moved to yazelix-3jw

title: detect first time running yazelix and give a helpful message explaining the zjstatus permission thingy
description: Imported GitHub issue #327.
close_reason: First-run permission guidance is now covered in the welcome flow and docs

title: Allow and document Starship customization
description: Allow users to customize Starship in a supported way and document where the boundary lies between Yazelix-managed prompt behavior and user-owned prompt configuration.
design: This should preserve Yazelix's managed defaults while giving users a real override path. Decide whether the right model is merge-based, file-inclusion-based, or a simpler user hook, then document exactly what is stable and what can drift. Avoid a design where Starship becomes a second uncontrolled config island with no compatibility story.
acceptance_criteria: Users can apply Starship customization without forking core Yazelix files, and the docs explain the supported customization path clearly. Add unit tests for merge or override helpers, integration tests for the chosen generation path, and end-to-end checks that verify the final prompt config is generated as expected, with detailed logs of the merge source and output path.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Expand user config control where the merge cost is worth it
description: This is about giving users more control over generated tool configs without turning Yazelix into a fully generic config engine. The clearest remaining gap is terminal config merging: users should be able to layer more of their own terminal-emulator config on top of Yazelix-managed config when the merge model is safe and maintainable.
design: Treat this as an evaluation-first feature, not a promise to toml-ize every config surface. Start by identifying the low-hanging configuration areas where merging is practical and the maintenance cost is acceptable. The current likely target is terminal config merging. Keep the broader idea of a more fully controlled profile as direction, but do not expand this bead to cover `devenv` config control; that should stay in a separate bead. The bar is whether the extra user control is worth the implementation and support complexity.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision and scope for which config surfaces should support richer user merging. If terminal config merging is worth it, Yazelix should support it in a maintainable way. If not, the bead should still produce a documented rationale and narrower control model rather than vague ambition about controlling everything. Any implementation path should include unit tests for merge semantics, end-to-end generation checks for the affected config surfaces, and detailed logs showing the user input, managed defaults, and final merged output.
close_reason: Not planned: first-class Ghostty/WezTerm support should expand through explicit Yazelix-owned options and the existing terminal.config_mode ownership boundary, not through a generic terminal-config merge engine.

title: add links to the overview
description: Imported open GitHub issue #276.
close_reason: README overview now links out to the main docs entrypoints

title: replace welcome art with logo. make it work on half screens
description: Imported open GitHub issue #263.
close_reason: Covered by the broader welcome-screen redesign bead yazelix-2k9

notes:
Implemented on 2026-04-04:
- Added structured startup profiling records under ~/.local/share/yazelix/profiles/startup/ via nushell/scripts/utils/startup_profile.nu
- Upgraded yzx dev profile to drive the real startup path through nushell/scripts/utils/profile.nu instead of shallow helper-only timings
- Instrumented real startup boundaries in start_yazelix.nu, environment_bootstrap.nu, environment.nu, and start_yazelix_inner.nu
- Added a maintainer schema/helper regression plus an end-to-end harness regression in test_yzx_maintainer.nu
- Updated docs in docs/yzx_cli.md and README.md to describe the real report artifact and clear-cache semantics

Measured findings from local runs:
- Warm current-terminal startup was dominated by inner.generate_zellij_config (~2.9-3.3s on this machine)
- Cold/rebuild-heavy startup was dominated by bootstrap.devenv.shell_entry (~16s on this machine)
- Cold path also showed two distinct shellhook process invocations during shell entry; the profiler now surfaces their separate process contexts in the report instead of collapsing them into one ambiguous duplicate block
close_reason: Implemented structured end-to-end startup profiling and documentation

title: Yazi: open the current file or directory in the system file manager
description: Add a Yazelix-managed Yazi action that opens the current file or directory in the host system file manager, on Linux and ideally macOS too.
design: This should start with an investigation because Yazi may already support part of this flow natively. Verify what Yazi already provides, then decide whether Yazelix only needs to surface it through a keybinding or whether Yazelix needs a small wrapper for Linux and macOS. The user-facing goal is simple: from Yazi, hit a keybinding and open the current location in the platform file manager. This is likely a low-hanging fruit and a good candidate to tackle soon once planning cleanup is done.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear supported way from Yazi to open the current file or directory in the system file manager. If Yazi already supports it cleanly, Yazelix documents or binds that flow. If not, Yazelix adds the minimal integration needed for Linux and macOS. Add unit tests for opener selection and platform dispatch helpers, integration tests for keybinding routing, and end-to-end validation that logs the selected path, platform opener invoked, and fallback behavior when the opener is unavailable.
close_reason: Not planned

title: zjstatus: allow a small user-configurable text slot
description: Give users a small piece of personal space in the Yazelix zjstatus bar where they can define a short custom string.
design: Keep this intentionally narrow. The likely placement is to the left of the `YAZELIX` segment. This should not become a full templating or arbitrary string-programming system for the entire status bar. A simple configurable text field in `yazelix.toml` is enough if the result feels stable and readable.
acceptance_criteria: Users can set a short custom string for zjstatus, and it appears in a defined place near the YAZELIX branding without destabilizing the rest of the status bar design. Add unit tests for config parsing and rendering helpers, width-budget or rendering checks, and end-to-end validation that logs the configured value, rendered placement, and truncation or hiding behavior on narrow layouts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Tailwind CSS LSP to JS/TS pack
description: Add the Tailwind CSS language server to the JavaScript and TypeScript language-pack story so modern frontend projects have the expected completion and diagnostics out of the box.
design: Scope this to the curated pack model rather than ad-hoc per-user tweaks. Decide whether Tailwind LSP belongs directly in the JS or TS pack or in a narrower frontend or web pack, then wire the package, editor integration, and docs accordingly. Be explicit about how this behaves for projects that do not use Tailwind so the default does not feel like noisy overreach.
acceptance_criteria: Projects that use Tailwind get a working LSP path from the relevant Yazelix pack, and users can understand why it is included. Add unit tests for pack membership and resolver logic, config-generation checks for the chosen editor integration, and an end-to-end smoke test in a minimal frontend fixture with detailed LSP startup logs.
notes: Implemented by adding tailwindcss-language-server to the ts pack in both yazelix_default.toml and home_manager/module.nix, documenting the pack change, and adding a ts-pack parity test. Broader frontend-pack/toolchain questions were split into yazelix-7zs.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add emoji support (status/UI)
description: Add an emoji insertion path inside Yazelix instead of treating emoji support as a vague cosmetic request. The main user value is being able to insert emoji quickly from the terminal workspace, either through a lightweight emoji picker keybinding or by exposing a good emoji CLI in the environment if that gives a cleaner result.
design: Prefer the smallest UX that feels native in Yazelix. First evaluate whether a dependable emoji CLI is enough or whether Yazelix should wire a dedicated keybinding that opens an emoji menu or picker. Keep the feature opt-in or low-friction, avoid disturbing existing keymaps, and make sure the experience works in the managed shell/editor workflow instead of only in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Users can insert emoji from a Yazelix session without leaving the workspace. The chosen flow is documented and ergonomic enough to use regularly. If a keybinding or menu approach is used, it avoids obvious key conflicts and fails clearly when the underlying emoji tool is unavailable. Add unit tests for picker or command-selection logic, integration tests for the picker or CLI wiring, and an end-to-end script that records the invoked path and resulting insertion behavior with detailed logs.
close_reason: Not planned: low-value compared with other work; any useful residue is better handled as optional docs rather than a first-class Yazelix feature.

title: Add a Helix note-taking section
description: Add a focused documentation section on note taking in Helix for Yazelix users, with special attention to Markdown-heavy workflows, Obsidian-style vaults, and markdown-oxide support.
design: This should not become a generic Helix note-taking essay. Focus on the practical Yazelix angle: how to work on Markdown notes inside the managed editor, what language tooling matters, how markdown-oxide fits in, and what an Obsidian user needs to know when living in a terminal-first workspace instead of a GUI vault app.
acceptance_criteria: The docs give a clear path for a Yazelix user who wants a serious Markdown or Obsidian-adjacent workflow in Helix. The section explains the role of markdown-oxide, shows a realistic note-taking setup, and avoids hand-wavy editor advice. Verify the referenced tooling and commands in the current environment and keep examples and logs concrete enough that regressions in docs examples would be obvious.
close_reason: Not planned

title: Fix Alt-( and Alt-) keybindings in Helix under Zellij
description: Fix the Alt-( and Alt-) Helix keybindings when running under Zellij so these editor shortcuts behave reliably instead of getting lost or mangled by the terminal and Zellij layer.
design: Treat this as an input-stack bug spanning terminal, Zellij, and Helix. First confirm what escape sequences are emitted in the supported terminal set, then decide whether the fix belongs in Zellij keybindings, terminal keyboard protocol settings, Helix mappings, or some narrower compatibility layer. Avoid a fix that only works in one terminal if Yazelix claims broader support.
acceptance_criteria: The affected Alt bindings work in the supported Yazelix terminal stack or the docs state the exact caveat when they cannot. Add unit tests for escape-sequence normalization or mapping helpers where they exist, regression tests or scripted input capture, and end-to-end validation with detailed logs of the emitted sequences and the final behavior inside Helix.
close_reason: Closed after fresh manual verification confirmed Alt-( and Alt-) currently work in the supported Yazelix stack.

title: UX: floating pane option for lazygit/AI TUI
description: Offer an optional floating-pane workflow for transient TUIs such as lazygit or AI terminals when that better fits the workspace than dedicating a persistent split.
design: Keep this focused on the tools that benefit from temporary, attention-stealing use rather than making every pane type floatable by default. Evaluate how floating panes should interact with focus, sizing, closing, cwd inheritance, and the existing managed-pane model so the feature feels intentional instead of bolted on.
acceptance_criteria: Users can launch the supported transient TUIs in a predictable floating-pane flow without breaking normal pane management. Add unit tests for the command and config routing and end-to-end scripts that open, focus, and close the floating TUI path while logging pane ids, cwd, and focus transitions.
close_reason: Shipped configurable managed floating popup workflow for transient TUIs

title: Keep the Git Yazi plugins state from getting stale
description: Keep the Git-related Yazi plugin state and the related Starship Git status from going stale when repo state changes inside the same directory. Prefer narrow event-driven refreshes so users do not need to leave and re-enter a directory just to get current repo information, and keep any added Git styling subtle enough to preserve normal sidebar readability.
design: Treat this as an event-driven freshness contract, not a generic polling problem. First verify which Git-related Yazi surfaces go stale while staying in the same directory, then prefer narrow refresh triggers when Yazelix returns attention to Yazi: sidebar focus regain and popup close back into Yazi are the first-class candidates. Do not add background polling or a global heartbeat unless a clean event-driven refresh primitive proves insufficient. If Yazi exposes a real refresh or file-list invalidation action, use that directly; otherwise stop before adding a fragile keystroke-injection hack. If Git state is tinted in the sidebar, prefer foreground text styling over row-background fills so hover and selection states remain legible.
acceptance_criteria: Git-related Yazi indicators no longer require a directory change to become fresh again after repo state changes inside the same directory. The initial fix is limited to high-signal events: entering the sidebar pane and closing the popup pane back into Yazi. Defend the trigger contract with focused tests where practical, and only proceed if the Yazi-side refresh mechanism stays simple and maintainable. Reject background polling, broad layout or menu triggers, or input-injection hacks unless later evidence proves they are necessary. Any Git tinting should remain a foreground text treatment that coexists cleanly with existing hover and selection visuals.
close_reason: Completed: added event-driven Yazi sidebar refresh on popup close, sidebar focus, initial open, and Yazi navigation, plus distinct Git styling for staged versus unstaged changes and Starship refresh on sidebar open.

title: Fix status bar overflow on narrow layouts
description: Fix status-bar overflow and fit problems so the bar remains usable on narrower screens instead of clipping or breaking layout.
design: Solve this as a responsive-layout problem, not by blindly truncating everything. Audit which widgets or segments dominate width, define a graceful compaction or hiding strategy, and make sure the status surface still communicates the important state at common narrow widths and split sizes.
acceptance_criteria: The status bar fits and remains readable on supported narrow layouts. Add unit tests for width-budget and widget-selection helpers, rendering or layout regression checks, and end-to-end validation in multiple terminal widths with detailed logs of the computed widget set, widths, and any truncation or hiding decisions.
close_reason: Cancelled: this is no longer a product direction Yazelix intends to pursue.

title: Define the behavior for editors opened from the shell instead of from Yazi
description: Decide how Yazelix should treat editors opened directly from the shell instead of through the managed Yazi-to-editor flow, and implement the chosen behavior coherently.
design: This is a workspace-model decision, not just a bug fix. Figure out what state Yazelix can and cannot assume when a user starts Helix or another editor manually from a shell pane: cwd ownership, pane identity, managed editor tracking, sidebar interactions, and open-file routing. The result may be explicit support, explicit non-support, or a narrower managed-handoff model, but it must be intentional.
acceptance_criteria: The behavior of shell-opened editors is explicitly defined and documented: only Yazelix-managed `editor` panes are reused by managed editor flows, while shell-opened editors remain ordinary panes. Add pane-policy unit tests for managed pane detection and focus context, routing regression checks for Yazi open behavior, and user-facing doctor/docs wording that matches the contract.
close_reason: Defined the shell-opened-editor contract, documented it, added Yazi routing regressions, and added plugin pane-policy unit tests for managed editor detection and focus context.

title: Make new panes open on the active Yazi project path
description: Make new Yazelix-managed panes inherit the project path implied by the current Yazi context when that is what the user is clearly working on.
design: The feature should strengthen the workspace model instead of creating hidden cwd magic. Define when a clicked or entered Yazi directory should become the effective workspace root for new panes, how that interacts with the stable tab workspace cwd, and when the user's current shell/editor state should override the file-browser context. Keep the behavior predictable and document the mental model clearly.
acceptance_criteria: New managed panes open on the expected project path when the user is working through Yazi. Add unit tests for workspace-root selection helpers, integration tests for workspace-root resolution, and end-to-end coverage that clicks into directories, opens new panes, and logs the Yazi path, tab workspace root, and resulting pane cwd.
notes: Root cause: Zellij with default_shell=nu did not reliably preserve the requested pane cwd for this path, which matches upstream bug zellij-org/zellij#3068. Final fix: resolve the live sidebar/Yazi cwd in Nushell, then ask the pane orchestrator to open a normal pane directly in that cwd. Rejected approaches: command panes caused the exit prompt UI, and injected cd commands worked but were too ugly.
close_reason: Fixed Alt+m pane opening to resolve the live Yazi/sidebar cwd in Nushell and delegate normal pane creation to the pane orchestrator, avoiding both wrong cwd fallback and command-pane exit UI

title: replace bottom bar with hideable one
description: Replace the current bottom bar with a hideable alternative that keeps the useful workspace status without permanently stealing screen space.
design: This is a layout and information-density problem, not just a cosmetics tweak. Evaluate approaches such as a second zjstatus instance, a hideable bar, or a more context-sensitive status surface, and optimize for narrow layouts, clarity, and discoverability. The result should preserve the important information users rely on while making it easier to reclaim vertical space.
acceptance_criteria: Users can hide or collapse the bottom status surface without losing the important workspace cues permanently. Add unit tests for layout-selection or visibility-state helpers, layout or rendering regression checks for narrow widths, and end-to-end validation of show and hide behavior with detailed logs of the active layout state and visible widgets.

title: [Bug]: The desktop launcher doesn't work with fish
description: Make the desktop launcher work reliably when the user's default shell is fish instead of depending on shell-specific assumptions that only happen to work for Bash-like setups.
design: Treat the desktop entry as a real supported entrypoint, not a maintainer-only convenience. Verify what environment and shell state the desktop launch path actually has, remove assumptions that leak from interactive shell startup, and make the launcher robust regardless of whether the user prefers fish or another shell. Keep the fix aligned with the broader push away from stale env dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: Launching Yazelix from the desktop entry works for fish users without requiring a manual desktop-file patch. Add unit tests for launcher command-construction helpers where they exist, a launcher smoke test or reproducible script that simulates the desktop-entry environment, and logs that make shell-specific regressions easy to diagnose.
close_reason: Replaced copied desktop asset flow with generated desktop install/uninstall commands, aligned Home Manager on the POSIX launcher, and verified the supported launcher contracts.

title: [Bug]: Starting Yazelix with yzx launch --here --path doesn't work
description: Fix the yzx launch --here --path flow so it opens the intended directory and does not break keybindings or leave the resulting session unresponsive.
design: This is a real multi-symptom bug: wrong startup path, bad session responsiveness, and confusing interactions with user remaps and non-default shells. The fix should trace how the here-path flow sets cwd, initializes the session, and wires the managed plugins so that the path override does not corrupt the normal control layer.
acceptance_criteria: Launching with --here --path opens the requested location and keeps the session responsive. Add unit tests for the command builder and path-handling helpers, integration coverage for the launch argument plumbing, and end-to-end tests that launch into a custom path under at least one non-default shell and capture detailed logs for cwd, plugin readiness, and keybinding responsiveness.
close_reason: Closed after fresh verification showed non-persistent --here --path launches already pass the requested cwd through to Zellij, while existing persistent sessions intentionally reattach and ignore --path with a clear warning.

title: [Bug]: Yazi user configuration not taken into account
description: Honor user-supplied Yazi configuration when generating the merged runtime config instead of silently dropping the user customization path.
design: Decide what the supported merge boundary is for Yazi user config, then implement it consistently. The generated config path should combine Yazelix-managed defaults with the supported user-owned overrides in a way that is predictable and documented, without pretending to merge unsupported pieces silently.
acceptance_criteria: Supported user Yazi configuration is present in the final generated runtime config. Add unit tests for the merge logic and end-to-end validation that inspects the final Yazi config output, with logs showing the user source path, merge inputs, and resulting generated file.
close_reason: Completed

title: Packaging: rework Home Manager integration for the package-ready runtime model
description: Rework the Home Manager module so it matches the package-ready runtime model instead of assuming a repo checkout and repo-owned runtime assets.
design: Define exactly what the Home Manager module should own versus what Yazelix should generate at runtime. The module should set up the install path, stable config hooks, and any package/runtime references cleanly, while avoiding assumptions that only make sense for a live clone under ~/.config/yazelix. Keep option parity with the default config model where that still makes sense, but do not drag repo-first behavior into the packaged world.
acceptance_criteria: The Home Manager path remains first-class under the package-ready runtime model. Add unit tests for option translation and path derivation, evaluation tests for module options and defaults, and end-to-end Home Manager install or activation smoke tests with detailed logs showing where configs, assets, and runtime state land.
notes: The emerging testing model is: runtime behavior stays in the normal Yazelix/devenv lane, while Home Manager module correctness is exercised through Nix/Home Manager evaluation. A flake-native smoke check under home_manager/flake.nix provides a clean bridge: yzx dev test can call nix eval on a focused attr for the default regression path, while broader nix flake check usage can remain a CI or dedicated package-readiness tool. After measuring the cached smoke check, there is no current need for a separate fast-path toggle in yzx dev test.
close_reason: Completed

title: Packaging: add a first-party flake interface around Yazelix
description: Add a thin first-party flake interface around the existing Yazelix runtime so users can consume it through a standard Nix surface without creating a second environment definition.
design: Keep devenv.nix and the runtime model as the source of truth. The flake should expose useful outputs such as a dev shell, launcher or app entrypoints, and module exports where appropriate, but it should stay thin and avoid reimplementing the runtime separately. The point is discoverability and integration, not a forked architecture.
acceptance_criteria: Users can consume Yazelix through a first-party flake surface that wraps the real runtime instead of duplicating it. Add unit tests for any flake-output helper logic, flake evaluation tests, and smoke tests for the exported outputs, with logs that make it obvious which runtime assets and entrypoints are being exercised.
notes:
This bead is now the implementation track for the install decision captured in `yazelix-aa24` and `docs/specs/one_command_install_ux.md`.

Target front door:
  nix run github:luccahuguet/yazelix#install

Keep the flake thin: it should expose installer/launcher surfaces around the existing runtime model, not create a second environment definition.
close_reason: Top-level flake interface and install front door implemented

title: Yazi: remove extra step after zoxide jump
description: Remove the awkward extra step after a Yazi zoxide jump so users can jump to a directory and act on it immediately instead of backing out one level first.
design: Preserve the speed of the current zoxide integration but fix the UX mismatch. After a jump, the user should be able to open the selected target naturally instead of manually navigating back to a parent just to re-select the folder. The design should stay consistent with Yazi semantics and avoid surprising behavior for file versus directory targets.
acceptance_criteria: After a zoxide jump, the next action on the selected target feels direct and natural. Add unit tests for jump-resolution or target-selection helpers, integration tests for the jump-resolution path, and end-to-end Yazi flow coverage with logs showing the resolved target, resulting cursor location, and open action.
close_reason: Completed via direct-open Yazi zoxide flow on Alt+z with managed-editor reuse/new-pane fallback.

title: Zellij keybindings: replace awkward Ctrl+Alt combos with easier workspace bindings
description: Replace the ugliest Ctrl+Alt workspace bindings with easier combinations where possible without wrecking the editor-first feel of Yazelix.
design: Audit the current Zellij-facing bindings, identify the high-frequency awkward ones, and remap only the places where the ergonomic win is real. Favor easier workspace-oriented bindings such as Alt combinations or other low-conflict keys, but keep Helix, Yazi, shells, and terminal-emulator behavior as hard constraints. This should be a surgical ergonomics pass, not a full keybinding philosophy rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: The highest-friction workspace bindings become easier to use without introducing major conflicts. Add unit tests for keymap generation or binding-selection helpers, keymap validation checks where possible, and end-to-end tests in the supported terminal stack that exercise the changed bindings and log the emitted sequences, Zellij actions, and any conflict notes.

title: Remove stale TODO noise from shipped Yazi flavor snapshots
description: Several shipped Yazi flavor snapshot files still contain low-value '# TODO: remove' markers that do not describe a real maintenance action anymore. Clean those comments up so the shipped asset surface reads like an intentional snapshot rather than a half-finished scratch area.
design: This is a tiny cleanup bead, not a style rewrite. Delete obviously stale TODO markers or replace them with a precise upstream-sync note only if the comment still carries real meaning. Do not change flavor behavior just to satisfy the cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: The stale TODO markers in shipped Yazi flavor snapshots are removed or replaced with precise durable comments, and the asset content is otherwise unchanged.
notes: Removed stale '# TODO: remove' marker comments from six shipped Yazi flavor snapshots while leaving all TOML rules and values unchanged. Verification: rg found no remaining TODO markers under configs/yazi/flavors; taplo check --no-auto-config --no-schema passed on the six changed flavor.toml files; git diff --check passed. Full taplo check with repo schema still fails on existing unrelated Yazi schema drift in ashen.yazi.
close_reason: Completed: stale TODO marker comments removed from shipped Yazi flavor snapshots without changing asset behavior.

title: Make yzx status a structured Nushell table instead of hand-printed lines
description: yzx status is worth keeping, but its current line-by-line print output hides structure and makes later trimming harder to see. Rework it to build a clear record and table-oriented output so the surviving v15 status surface is easier to read, cheaper to extend, and more obviously scoped.
design: Prefer building a structured record first and letting Nushell render the main view, with optional richer sections like versions or verbose shell-hook details layered on top. The point is not flashy formatting; it is to make yzx status honest, smaller, and easier to keep aligned with the real surviving runtime contract.
acceptance_criteria: yzx status renders from structured data rather than a pile of manual prints, and the resulting output is clearer without expanding the command scope. Tests cover the surviving structured contract instead of brittle line-order trivia.
close_reason: Reworked yzx status to render from structured rows instead of manual print lines and aligned tests to the surviving table-oriented contract.

title: Document the stale-live-session contract after runtime updates and owner-managed relaunches
description:
Users can update the on-disk Yazelix runtime correctly and still keep an older live Ghostty/Zellij session open. That is a reasonable live-session contract, but it is easy to misread as a broken update when the product does not say it plainly.

Add one concise owner-aware note explaining that already-open Yazelix windows continue running the old runtime until they are explicitly relaunched or restarted. Keep the wording tight and factual in the relevant update and troubleshooting surfaces.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- Updating the runtime on disk and transitioning already-open live sessions are different operations.
- That distinction is normal, but users will read it as a failed update unless the product says so plainly.
- Keep this narrow and factual. Do not add noisy UI; just document the contract in the places where users already think about updates and recovery.
- The wording should stay owner-aware: fresh launch paths use the owner-provided wrapper or desktop surface, while already-open sessions stay on their current live runtime until explicit relaunch or restart.
acceptance_criteria: The relevant update and troubleshooting docs state clearly that already-open Yazelix windows keep running their current live runtime until they are relaunched or restarted. The wording matches the surviving manual-install and Home Manager owner contracts, including the owner-resolved restart behavior from yazelix-4buc.12 and the Home Manager smoke lane from yazelix-4buc.13. The docs must not promise invisible hot-swapping of already-open sessions.
notes: Completed: README, installation docs, troubleshooting docs, yzx CLI docs, and Home Manager README now state that already-open windows keep the current live runtime until relaunch or yzx restart.
close_reason: Completed: stale-live-session update ownership contract is documented across the relevant user-facing surfaces.

title: Delay devenver until v15 Yazelix proves a clean optional runtime and runner boundary
description: Evaluate `devenver` only after the trimmed v15 Yazelix line proves a real need for an optional dynamic runtime plus runner layer. Do not start a second new project at the same time as the v15 trim-first reset and do not genericize the current backend story prematurely.
design:
Reason from first principles:
- If `devenver` is created too early, it will likely just relocate current Yazelix entanglement and call it generic.
- The extraction should happen only after the trimmed v15 line proves the clean boundary between product semantics and optional runtime/runner semantics.
- `devenver` should be thought of as a runtime + runner system, not as a package manager.
- Nix/Home Manager still own install/update. A future `devenver` would own things like dynamic runtime preparation, entry/launch/exec handoff, refresh-like runtime rebuilding if it exists, and related cache/state.
- The output should define extraction criteria, what `devenver` would and would not own, and what evidence would justify extracting it later from the main Yazelix repo rather than leaving it as an internal boundary.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit delayed-extraction plan for `devenver`. The result defines when `devenver` should stay an idea, when it becomes an internal boundary, and what conditions would justify extracting it later from the main Yazelix repo after the trimmed v15 line is real.
notes: Completed: README/release direction records that the broader devenv runtime terminal layer may continue only as a separate future project and should not block the v15 trim-first line.
close_reason: Completed: optional devenv/devenver extraction is explicitly delayed until after v15 proves a clean boundary.

title: Classify Yazelix config surfaces by backend and devenv dependence
description: Audit `yazelix.toml` and `yazelix_packs.toml` by ownership and runtime dependence. Classify which settings remain meaningful in a package-runtime-only model, which require backend materialization or `devenv`, which are rebuild-relevant, and which would need to narrow, move, or disappear in a backend-free workspace-only slice. The first output should be a clear matrix and guidance for future simplification work, not an immediate config-file split unless the audit proves that split is actually justified.
notes: This is still useful, but it is no longer the immediate next move now that upstream nixpkgs submission is off the table. Do it after the flake-package-first surface and installer demotion work is concrete enough that the config audit is answering the right product boundary.
close_reason: Completed

title: Re-scope uninstall and desktop integration after runtime_current identity removal
description: Re-scope `yzx uninstall`, manual desktop-install logic, and related ownership assumptions after installer-owned and Home Manager-owned runtime-current identity is removed from the main product model. Keep only the user-local integration surfaces that still make sense for a flake-package-first and Home Manager-first product. Ground this in the real command code: `desktop.nu`, `uninstall.nu`, and `home_manager.nu` currently preserve or clean up paths whose meaning depends on the older installer-managed runtime model.
close_reason: Completed

title: Document distribution-vs-backend ownership trim matrix
close_reason: Documented the distinction between distribution trimming and backend trimming

title: Document Yazelix subsystem code inventory and LOC snapshot
close_reason: Documented subsystem LOC inventory and linked it from architecture map

title: Add Alt+<number> keybindings to yzx keys documentation
description: The yzx keys command should document the Alt+1 through Alt+9 keybindings that Yazelix provides for direct tab navigation. These keybindings already exist in the Zellij config (yazelix_overrides.kdl) but are not shown in the yzx keys output.
notes: Already satisfied by the current keybinding docs: yzx keys reports Alt+1..9 as direct tab navigation, README.md documents Alt+1..9, and docs/keybindings.md documents Alt+1 through Alt+9. No code change needed for this bead.
close_reason: Already documented in yzx keys, README, and keybindings docs.

title: Add local startup profile comparison and baseline tooling
description:
The new structured startup profiler makes local evidence possible, but there is still no first-class way to compare two runs, summarize phase deltas, or keep stable local baselines without relying on noisy hosted CI timing. That limits the practical value of the new reports for ongoing performance work.

Design:
Build on the existing structured JSONL report format rather than adding a separate metrics store. Provide a local-only surface that can diff two startup profile reports or compare a fresh run against a named baseline, highlighting phase deltas and major regressions. Keep this explicitly local and deterministic; do not turn it into a flaky GitHub Actions wall-clock gate.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. There is a user-facing local command or helper to compare two startup profile reports. 2. The comparison output highlights total wall-time delta plus per-step or per-phase deltas. 3. The tooling can work from saved reports under the Yazelix state dir without rerunning startup. 4. Docs explain the intended local workflow and explicitly avoid promising a noisy hosted-CI timing gate.

title: Evaluate a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake backend for Yazelix Core
description: Evaluate whether a future Yazelix Core edition could use a Nix-only, no-devenv backend centered on an opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack as one fixed bundle. The question is not whether this should replace full Yazelix. The question is whether keeping Nix while dropping devenv yields a simpler, more honest Core backend by shipping the core default tool/runtime set directly: Zellij, Yazi, Helix, Nushell, Starship, Lazygit, relevant default terminal support, runtime assets, and launch/update entrypoints.
design: Treat this as a post-v14 evaluation bead and as a specific Core-backend candidate, not as a new commitment for main. Reuse the backend contract, runtime dependency contract, command-surface audit, and Core-boundary work first. Then evaluate whether a fixed flake-defined default-stack bundle plus app/shell outputs can support the Core surface cleanly without carrying devenv profile semantics. This is intentionally not a full replacement for the current flexible pack-driven model. The evaluation should explicitly compare this shape against the Pixi experiment lane and explain where a Nix-only Core bundle is simpler, more honest, or more constrained.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether a Nix-only, no-devenv, opinionated flake that ships the default Yazelix stack is a good fit for Yazelix Core. The result identifies which current Yazelix guarantees it could preserve, which ones it would intentionally drop, what install/update/run surface it would expose, how it would treat the current default tool set, and whether it is a better Core candidate than a non-Nix backend experiment. The output is specific enough to guide either a later prototype or an explicit no-go decision.
notes: Completed: the v15 branch already answers this evaluation in code and docs. The candidate is not a separate Core experiment anymore; it is the active trimmed branch direction: fixed packaged runtime bundle, no runtime-local devenv, no packs sidecar, built-in Ghostty, and explicit upstream/Home Manager update ownership.
close_reason: Completed: the current v15 branch itself is the evaluated Nix-only, no-devenv, fixed-runtime direction, with explicit update owners and a narrower packaged workspace contract.

title: Define the Rust/Nushell bridge contract and incremental crate layout
description: Define how Rust code will be integrated incrementally into the current Nushell-driven repo so small slices can land after v15.0 during v15.x without forcing a big-bang rewrite or pretending v15.0 itself is the Rust release. Cover the helper-binary versus library boundary, JSON/stdout-stderr contracts, error-classification handoff, repo/workspace crate layout, build/distribution implications, and how Nushell command surfaces should call into Rust while preserving current UX.
design: This is the missing bridge between Rust planning and actually shipping incremental Rust slices in the live v15 repo. The repo already contains Rust Zellij plugins, but those wasm crates do not answer how a future Rust core should coexist with Nushell commands, Nix packaging, and generated-config flows. Prefer a thin typed Rust helper/binary model first, with stable machine-readable output consumed by Nushell shims. Do not jump straight to replacing the whole CLI with clap, and keep the bridge compatible with a longer v15.x insertion path that feeds into v16.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation for the incremental Rust integration strategy: where Rust code lives, how Nushell invokes it, what machine-readable contract is used, how errors/logging are surfaced, and how the build/distribution story stays coherent with the current repo. The result is specific enough that later Rust slices can share one integration pattern across v15.x and into the broader v16 push instead of inventing their own ad hoc shims.

title: Define the Rust migration matrix and first-slice order after boundaries are proven
description: Produce an explicit Rust migration matrix for the current v15 codebase in a form that supports gradual v15.x insertion and a later Rust-forward v16 release. Classify the major surfaces by Rust fitness, parity risk, expected payoff, and whether they should stay in Nushell, move behind a Rust shim, or move fully into a Rust core. Ground the matrix in the actual hot modules carrying product complexity today: config parsing and validation, runtime contract checking, doctor and preflight logic, environment bootstrap, launch/bootstrap state, config generation, and the mixed yzx command surface.
design: This is the gate between 'Rust is safer/faster' sentiment and an implementation sequence that actually improves the live v15 product without confusing release identity. Build directly on the current architecture work: runtime/helper trims, pane-orchestrator cleanup, migration-layer deletion, runtime dependency/preflight contracts, and shared checker work. The output should prefer delete-first simplification before translation. Explicitly identify what should remain Nushell or Nix glue even in a Rust-heavier future, and separate slices that can land quietly during v15.x from slices that are more naturally part of the later v16 push.
acceptance_criteria: There is a written Rust migration matrix covering the main surviving Nushell surfaces with a recommended keep/bridge/rewrite decision for each. The matrix identifies which slices can land during v15.x, which should likely wait for the broader v16 Rust-forward push, and which should likely remain Nushell or Nix. The recommendation is grounded in current hot spots and the live v15 product contract rather than in generic preference for Rust.
notes: Current sequencing note after the 2026-04-13 product decision: treat this as planning for the live v15 line after the current trim and boundary-hardening work, not as a placeholder for a parallel future branch. Do not use the Rust matrix to bypass the remaining cleanup lanes; it should start only after the key ownership and product-boundary work is stable enough that Rust effort will clarify the system instead of freezing transitional clutter into a new implementation.

title: Port the surviving config contract, parsing, and validation core behind a Rust shim after migration deletion
description: Plan and later implement a Rust-backed core for the deterministic config logic that survives after the v15 migration layer is deleted. The target is the current config contract, parsing, schema validation, and state logic, not the old migration machinery. The goal is not to rewrite every config-facing command immediately, but to move the most structured, failure-prone surviving logic into a safer typed core while keeping a thin Nushell compatibility layer during migration.
design: Treat this as a good early Rust slice once the migration matrix and Rust/Nushell bridge strategy are ready and once the config-migration deletion bead has landed. This logic is highly structured, heavily reused, and benefits from strong types, deterministic serialization, and richer tests. Keep the UX and command surface stable by exposing the Rust logic through a narrow helper binary or shim first. Do not mix this with launch orchestration or package-backend work in the same slice, and do not preserve deleted migration behavior just because the old bead used to mention it.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear migration plan for moving the surviving config contract/parsing/validation/state core behind a Rust shim without breaking the current command surface. The slice defines parity fixtures for the live config contract, identifies which Nushell modules become thin wrappers versus being deleted, and is concrete enough to implement incrementally during v15.x or as part of the broader v16 push.
notes: Primary target modules after migration deletion: config_parser.nu, config_contract.nu, config_schema.nu, config_state.nu, config_diagnostics.nu, and any tightly coupled surviving helpers they require. Explicit non-goal: porting config_migrations.nu or migration transaction machinery, because that layer is being removed from the live product.

title: Port runtime dependency checking and doctor reasoning into a Rust core
description: Once the runtime dependency contract and shared checker are defined in Nushell, evaluate and stage a Rust-backed implementation for the dependency/preflight reasoning that launch and doctor share. Focus on the classification engine and structured results, not on rendering every doctor message in Rust on day one. This is a candidate for gradual v15.x insertion if the slice becomes mature enough, otherwise it can remain part of the larger v16 Rust-forward push.
design: This should follow the contract work, not replace it. First prove the dependency model and user-visible semantics in the current v15 codebase. Then move the checker core into Rust if it materially simplifies correctness, structured diagnostics, and future performance or maintainability. Keep doctor and launch presentation in Nushell initially if that reduces churn.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for moving the shared runtime contract checker into Rust, with a clear boundary between structured result generation and user-facing rendering. The plan identifies parity cases, launch-time performance constraints, expected machine-readable output, and how doctor will consume the same results without reintroducing duplicate logic. It also states honestly whether this slice belongs in a v15.x point release or should wait for the broader v16 push.
notes: Primary target logic lives around doctor.nu and the future shared runtime checker introduced by yazelix-hv2m. This slice should not start until j4qv and hv2m have clarified the contract in the current codebase.

title: Make welcome launch prompt use any key instead of Enter
description: The welcome screen currently says 'Press Enter to launch Zellij and start your session...' and waits for a full line input. Change it to a true single-key continue prompt so the text and behavior match and startup feels snappier.
close_reason: Changed welcome prompt to a true any-key continue

title: Clarify and verify the zsh/fish managed-shell availability boundary
description: Record and evaluate the current shell boundary: Yazelix-managed zsh/fish hook surfaces exist and work in managed shells, but zsh and fish are not currently shipped in the installed runtime bin/ and are instead provided through the managed devenv environment. Decide whether that is the intended product contract or whether extra shells should eventually become runtime-owned too.
design: Keep this as a low-priority clarification bead, not a pressure to runtime-own zsh or fish. The goal is to make the boundary explicit: Yazelix-managed hook surfaces exist for zsh and fish, but the shells themselves currently come from the managed devenv environment rather than the installed runtime bin. Verify the real behavior, record the intended support boundary, and only add tests or docs that defend that boundary clearly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The repo has a clear documented statement of where zsh and fish are expected to come from in managed Yazelix sessions today. 2. The support boundary between managed hook surfaces and runtime-owned shell binaries is verified instead of guessed. 3. Any added validation or docs defend that actual boundary without turning this into pressure to runtime-ship more shells immediately.

title: Scaffold visible stubs for Yazelix-managed user config surfaces
description: Create lightweight empty/commented stubs for relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces so users can discover where customizations belong without reading docs first.
design: Scope this to lightweight, self-explanatory scaffolding for Yazelix-owned user config surfaces under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/. Prefer creating stubs automatically when a surface becomes relevant or is first managed, not a giant first-run dump of empty files. For shells, likely create ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/shells/ plus small commented stubs. For other managed surfaces, favor one-line comment stubs or empty directories only when they materially improve discoverability. These are user-owned runtime files under ~/.config/yazelix, not tracked repo files and not personal dotfiles outside Yazelix ownership.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Relevant Yazelix-managed user config surfaces are auto-created under ~/.config/yazelix/user_configs/ when they become relevant. 2. Scaffolding stays lightweight and does not create a noisy pile of unused files by default. 3. Stub files clearly say they are Yazelix-managed extension points. 4. No personal config files outside ~/.config/yazelix/ are created or modified by this feature. 5. The implementation does not add tracked repo-state pollution for these user-owned stubs.

title: Audit update devenv and related version-sync flows
description: Verify yzx update devenv and the surrounding version-sync behavior against the current runtime contract. Confirm the command updates to the Yazelix-pinned devenv surface and does not leave version reporting, doctor output, or runtime entrypoints in a contradictory state.
close_reason: Audit completed: yzx update devenv and yzx dev update canaries were verified; canary syntax and temp config writability bugs were fixed.

title: Surface desktop-entry rebuild progress after terminal switches
description: If the user edits yazelix.toml to switch to a terminal that is not present in the currently active Yazelix environment, launching from the desktop entry can spend noticeable time rebuilding the environment before the terminal becomes available. Today that happens silently, so it looks like the desktop entry is broken while Yazelix is actually doing work.
design: Treat this as a launch UX problem, not a terminal-packaging bug. Reproduce a terminal switch that requires a fresh environment (for example Ghostty to WezTerm or Kitty), then decide how the desktop-entry path should surface that work. The user should get immediate visible feedback that Yazelix is rebuilding or preparing the environment, rather than waiting with no signal and assuming launch failed.
acceptance_criteria: When a desktop-entry launch needs to rebuild or re-enter the environment because the configured terminal is not available in the current activated profile, the user gets immediate, clear feedback that Yazelix is preparing the environment. The launch still completes normally, and the UX no longer feels like a dead desktop entry.

title: Evaluate Ghostty package source policy
description: Evaluate which Ghostty package source Yazelix should prefer for its managed/default terminal path once the current integration bugs are fixed.
design: Compare the three realistic Ghostty package sources separately instead of treating 'Ghostty' as one blob: nixpkgs Ghostty on Linux, the upstream Ghostty flake, and system Ghostty / Homebrew on macOS. Judge them on stability, platform coverage, integration burden, update cadence, and whether they improve or complicate Yazelix's managed launch path. Do not let this policy work blur into the current Ghostty runtime/shader bug fixes.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear Ghostty package-source policy for Yazelix covering nixpkgs Ghostty, the upstream Ghostty flake, and system/Homebrew Ghostty on macOS. The chosen default(s) and any experimental/override path are explicit, with reasoning tied to platform support and maintenance cost.
notes: Decision on 2026-04-01: Yazelix should not switch its default managed Ghostty source from nixpkgs to the upstream Ghostty flake. The upstream flake is explicitly the tip/dev path, not the regular-user package path, and it carries the same non-NixOS OpenGL caveat. Recommended policy shape: macOS should continue to use system/Homebrew Ghostty; NixOS can continue to treat nixpkgs Ghostty as the normal managed package path; non-NixOS Linux should stop assuming nixpkgs Ghostty is a friction-free default and should likely prefer another default terminal or a system Ghostty path unless the user explicitly opts into the Nix-managed Ghostty path and its GPU/driver caveats.
close_reason: Completed

title: Clarify pinned bootstrap-tool onboarding responsibilities
description:
Problem:
Yazelix now pins and manages some bootstrap-adjacent tools, but the onboarding story is still fuzzy about what the installer should install directly versus what it should merely assume or validate.

Design:
Define the onboarding contract for pinned bootstrap tools such as devenv, and clarify what is in scope for the installer versus what must already exist on the host.

Acceptance Criteria:
- The onboarding contract clearly states which bootstrap tools Yazelix installs or refreshes itself.
- The docs/specs distinguish installer-managed bootstrap tools from host prerequisites.
- The result does not blur into unrelated release/update automation work.
close_reason: Completed

title: Reconcile docs around the current flake install story
description:
Problem:
Some docs still describe flake as removed or otherwise contradict the new `nix run ...#install` front door.

Design:
Audit and fix the remaining contradictory docs so the current install story is internally consistent.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Primary docs no longer contradict the existence of the flake install surface.
- User-facing installation guidance is internally consistent.
close_reason: Reconciled README and troubleshooting docs with the current flake install story

title: Decide desktop entry installation responsibility for the flake installer
description:
Problem:
The flake installer materializes runtime state, but desktop-entry installation is not yet an explicit part of the contract.

Design:
Decide whether the installer should install/update desktop-entry assets directly or whether that remains a separate setup responsibility.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Desktop-entry responsibility is explicitly decided.
- The decision is reflected in docs or implementation follow-up scope.
close_reason: Decided desktop-entry installation stays explicit via yzx desktop install and updated specs/docs accordingly

title: Improve yzx run argument pass-through for external commands
description: Improve yzx run so argument pass-through to external commands is predictable and does not silently reshape argv in surprising ways.
design: Treat this as a command-surface contract issue. Clarify exactly what yzx run should pass through, where Yazelix-specific parsing should stop, and how quoting, separators, and variadic arguments should behave for external commands. Avoid compatibility hacks that preserve ambiguous behavior; the end state should be a clear argv contract that maintainers can test directly.
acceptance_criteria: There is a defined argv pass-through contract for yzx run, including representative examples of external command invocation. The bead leaves behind either an implementation path or a documented decision on what ambiguous legacy behavior will not be preserved.
close_reason: Completed with wrapped argv passthrough contract and focused regression coverage

title: Remove tracked .vscode repo state
description: Remove the tracked .vscode/settings.json Kiro-local preference, ignore .vscode/, and keep editor-local repo settings out of git by default.
close_reason: Resolved earlier: .vscode is ignored and no .vscode files are tracked.

title: Evaluate Lenia welcome style later
design: Defer Lenia until the welcome-style system exists and the cost/readability tradeoffs are proven. Prefer a pre-rendered or precomputed frame-sequence evaluation first; only consider live Lenia later if it proves clearly worth the complexity. This bead is for evaluating whether Lenia should join the animated pool later, not for forcing a live simulation into startup prematurely.
acceptance_criteria: The evaluation documents whether Lenia should be live, precomputed, or rejected for startup use, and only proceeds after the shared welcome_style selector exists.

title: Add optional yazelix_packs.toml sidecar config
description: Add an optional ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_packs.toml sidecar so dense pack configuration can move out of yazelix.toml without removing any existing pack capability or turning packs into a folder forest.
design: Treat this as a configuration-clarity feature, not a pack redesign. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door and add yazelix_packs.toml as an optional structured sidecar for denser pack enablement and pack-specific settings. Preserve all current pack features; the sidecar must not remove any capability currently expressible in yazelix.toml. Define one sharp ownership rule so users are never left wondering which file wins: either the sidecar owns dense pack config while yazelix.toml keeps only simple top-level references, or Yazelix fails fast when the same pack keys are declared in both places. Prefer explicit duplicate-source errors over silent precedence surprises. Keep this limited to packs; do not turn it into a generic many-file config explosion. Because packs are supported through both direct config and Home Manager today, the sidecar design must preserve a coherent story for both paths.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix supports an optional yazelix_packs.toml without regressing existing pack behavior from yazelix.toml. The final design clearly documents what pack data belongs in each file, how duplicate or conflicting definitions are handled, and how the feature works for both direct config users and Home Manager users. Add focused unit tests for sidecar discovery, parse or merge behavior, duplicate-source errors, and pack-resolution behavior. Add end-to-end tests with detailed logs for main-file-only pack config, sidecar-only pack config, a conflicting dual-definition case that fails clearly, a direct-config path, a Home Manager path, and a representative generated-config run showing the resolved pack set and emitted tooling or config output.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add data-driven Ghostty cursor builder
description: Add a constrained, data-driven Ghostty cursor builder that lets users choose a supported cursor family and supply 2-3 colors in ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml while preserving all existing named presets. Generate runtime shader output from structured Yazelix-owned config rather than exposing raw GLSL or terminal-native shader editing.
design: Keep this builder deliberately narrow and schema-driven. The first slice should cover the families that already map cleanly to shared shader helpers, specifically simple_dual, orbit_dual, and axis_gradient, with exact color-count validation per family. Keep existing preset names working unchanged and add one custom or builder selection path that reads from yazelix_cursors.toml. Fail fast on invalid family names, wrong color counts, invalid hex colors, or incomplete config; do not silently fall back to a preset. Generate the custom shader into the normal runtime Ghostty shader location and keep the tracked source variants as the curated preset library. Avoid adding freeform GLSL, timing knobs, or a general shader DSL in this bead.
