Because this becomes a supported user configuration surface, define the direct-config and Home Manager story explicitly rather than leaving one path ambiguous.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select a custom Ghostty cursor family plus 2-3 colors through ~/.config/yazelix/yazelix_cursors.toml, and Yazelix generates a working runtime shader without regressing existing named presets. The feature works coherently for both direct config users and Home Manager users, with no silent divergence between the two paths. Add focused unit tests for sidecar parsing, schema validation, family-to-renderer routing, exact color-count enforcement, and invalid-config failures. Add end-to-end config-generation tests with detailed logs that show the input sidecar config, resolved family, normalized colors, selected output shader path, and generated shader identifiers/constants. Include at least one e2e case per supported family, one direct-config case, one Home Manager case, and one regression case proving named presets still work when no sidecar builder config is present.

title: Replace Bash-heavy terminal wrappers in devenv.nix with Nushell-first helpers
description: Reduce remaining Bash-heavy terminal wrapper logic in devenv.nix only where moving it into shared Nushell helpers meaningfully shrinks duplication without obscuring terminal-specific launch behavior.
design: Treat this as a cleanup follow-up, not an abstraction contest. The goal is to centralize shared resolution logic, error messaging, and config-path handling in Nushell while leaving terminal-specific CLI flags explicit where they differ materially. Do not hide per-terminal launch contracts behind generic wrappers that become harder to reason about than the current code. Any refactor must preserve fail-fast startup, deterministic terminal config resolution, and the current Yazelix versus user mode semantics. Measure the change by whether the wrappers get simpler and the ownership boundary becomes easier to understand, not by whether every line of shell disappears.
acceptance_criteria: Shared wrapper logic that is truly common moves into Nushell, while terminal-specific launch behavior remains readable and correct. Add focused unit tests for the Nushell helpers that replace wrapper logic, especially config resolution, mode validation, and error paths. Add end-to-end wrapper smoke tests with detailed logs for at least Ghostty, WezTerm, Kitty, Alacritty, and Foot, showing the resolved config path, effective mode, and final terminal invocation shape. Verification must demonstrate that cleanup reduced duplication without changing supported behavior.
notes: Dropped as obsolete on v15: devenv.nix and the Bash-heavy terminal wrapper surface targeted by this bead no longer exist. Remaining devenv.nix mentions are historical/spec text, not an active wrapper implementation to refactor.
close_reason: Dropped as obsolete: v15 removed devenv.nix and the targeted Bash-heavy terminal wrapper implementation.

title: yzx onboard
description: Guide first-time users through creating the supported Yazelix config set instead of making them hand-edit configuration from scratch. Keep yazelix.toml as the front door, but allow onboarding to emit optional sidecars such as yazelix_packs.toml when that is the supported architecture.
design: Treat yzx onboard as an interactive first-run config generator, not as a generic tutorial surface. The command should ask the user a focused set of high-value questions and then write a sensible configuration outcome based on the answers. The interaction model should feel like a lightweight terminal questionnaire: arrow-key navigation for single-choice prompts, and explicit multi-select prompts where space toggles choices and enter confirms. Keep the scope tight around the settings that matter most for getting productive quickly rather than exposing every config knob up front. Question areas should likely include terminal preference, shell choice, editor choice, whether the sidebar is enabled, whether sessions are persistent, and a small set of useful packs or defaults. The output should stay aligned with the supported config architecture rather than hardcoding everything into yazelix.toml forever; if pack config lives in yazelix_packs.toml, onboarding should generate that file instead of collapsing pack answers back into the main file.
acceptance_criteria: `yzx onboard` can guide a first-time user through creating a valid supported Yazelix configuration with a small number of high-signal prompts. Single-choice prompts support arrow-key selection, and multi-select prompts support toggling choices before confirmation. The generated output is valid, readable, and aligned with the same supported options as the normal config path, including optional sidecars where the architecture calls for them. Add behavior tests for prompt flow and generated-config output, including at least one multi-select case, one interrupted-or-aborted flow, one case that writes the main yazelix.toml only, and one case that writes a pack sidecar when pack answers require it.

title: Adopt Nushell-style tutor flow for yzx tutor
description:
Follow up to the shipped `yzx tutor` MVP.

Keep this bead strictly about a richer tutor flow, not config onboarding. Config-first setup belongs under `yzx onboard`, not here.
design:
Adopt the shape of Nushell's built-in `tutor`: explicit subcommands like `begin`, `list`, and short guided steps instead of a single static overview. Keep the scope Yazelix-specific rather than copying Nushell literally. The goal is a small guided learning flow with mini quests that help users practice the managed workspace model.

Potential shape:
- `yzx tutor begin` starts the first Yazelix lesson
- `yzx tutor list` shows available lessons
- lessons are short, structured, and action-oriented
- mini quests ask the user to try concrete actions like `yzx keys`, `yzx menu`, `yzx cwd`, and Yazi-driven editor opens

Non-goal:
- guided `yazelix.toml` creation or interactive setup questionnaires; that belongs to `yzx onboard`
acceptance_criteria: The tutor surface can present multiple short lessons with explicit entrypoints instead of only a flat overview. At least one lesson includes a mini quest with a concrete Yazelix workflow to try. The flow stays distinct from `yzx help` \(command reference\), `yzx keys` \(keybinding discovery\), and `yzx onboard` \(config creation\).

title: Evaluate replacing yzx gc's du store-size probe with a modern Rust tool
description:
Investigate whether Yazelix should replace the current `du -sb /nix/store` size probe used by `yzx gc` with a modern Rust-based alternative such as `dust`, `dua`, or `gdu`.

Scope:
- identify which candidate tools are realistically available in the Yazelix environment or Nixpkgs
- verify whether any candidate provides a stable script-friendly single-path total size mode suitable for automation
- compare startup/runtime cost and output stability against `du -sb`
- avoid switching just for novelty; the replacement must be at least as robust for scripted GC summaries

Success means there is a clear decision: either adopt one concrete Rust tool for the probe with a clean contract, or document why `du -sb` should remain the implementation.
design: Judge replacement candidates by scriptability and robustness first, not by novelty or a Rust implementation. The current du probe may be ugly but acceptable if it is stable, available, and cheap enough. Any replacement must provide a clean single-path total-size contract, acceptable runtime, and predictable output that Yazelix can safely automate.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete keep or replace decision for the store-size probe. If replaced, one tool and invocation contract are chosen. If not, the bead records why the current du-based probe remains the better maintained option despite the appeal of modern tooling.
close_reason: Obsolete after deleting yzx gc from the v15 public surface.

title: Evaluate FlakeHub adoption for Yazelix
description: Evaluate whether FlakeHub would bring enough value to Yazelix to justify the additional platform coupling and paid features. Current evidence suggests the immediate upside is limited because Yazelix is still primarily a repo plus devenv plus yazelix.toml workflow, while the strongest future benefits would be binary cache ergonomics and distribution once Yazelix has a more package-first flake interface. Include current pricing, free/open-source eligibility, CI/cache use cases, and decision criteria for when adoption would become worthwhile.
design: Treat FlakeHub as a distribution and maintenance decision, not a generic ecosystem checkbox. Compare it against Yazelix's actual packaging maturity, expected install paths, binary-cache needs, CI ergonomics, open-source eligibility, and future flake-first direction. The decision should be time-aware: a no-for-now answer is valid if the product shape is not ready yet.
acceptance_criteria: There is an explicit adopt, defer, or reject decision on FlakeHub, with current value, costs, and future trigger conditions documented. If adoption is deferred, the bead records what would need to change in Yazelix's packaging model for FlakeHub to become worthwhile later.

title: Plan gradual Rust insertion across v15.x toward a Rust-forward v16 release
description: Create a long-term plan for introducing Rust into Yazelix only after v15.0 ships, and then only gradually during v15.x where it buys real safety, maintainability, or performance, while keeping the release identity honest: v15.0 remains the trimmed rebooted non-Rust release, and v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact. The goal is not rewriting for aesthetics; it is getting a more explicit, testable, and maintainable system with clear behavior contracts and a cleaner boundary between deterministic core logic and the surviving shell/Nix glue.
design: Treat this as a current-v15 architecture-and-process initiative with explicit release framing. Do not make Rust a prerequisite for shipping v15.0 and do not market v15.0 as the Rust release. Rust can land incrementally behind later v15.x point releases when a slice is mature enough, but the roadmap should keep that work honest and narrow. The rewrite plan should define what belongs in a Rust core, what remains Nushell or Nix glue, and how feature behavior becomes spec-driven by default. Every user-visible feature should have a Markdown spec and tests that map to the spec's acceptance cases. The sequence should start with deterministic, reusable logic, not interactive shell glue.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete plan for post-v15.0 gradual Rust insertion during v15.x and for a more explicitly Rust-forward v16 release. The plan identifies the first migration slices, the surfaces that should remain Nushell or Nix, the parity strategy for each slice, and the support boundary between Rust and the remaining glue so work can proceed incrementally without confusing release identity or turning Rust into a prerequisite for the initial v15 release.
notes: Updated 2026-04-13: release framing is now explicit. v15.x may gain Rust incrementally, but v16 is the release where Rust becomes a major visible architectural fact.

title: Evaluate integrating sahaj-b/ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix-managed cursor effects
description: Evaluate adding curated support for the MIT-licensed ghostty-cursor-shaders repo to Yazelix so Ghostty cursor effects remain fully Yazelix-managed instead of requiring users to clone and wire shader files manually. The goal is to extend the current Ghostty cursor-trail system with upstream trail and pulse/boom patterns while preserving Yazelix generation, docs, defaults, and random selection.
design: Current state: Yazelix already generates Ghostty shader config, vendors local shader files under configs/terminal_emulators/ghostty/shaders, and defaults terminal.cursor_trail to random. The upstream repo is a good fit because Ghostty supports multiple custom-shader lines and the repo is simple standalone GLSL under MIT. Important caveat: these upstream effects are not only color variants. They include trail shaders like cursor_warp, cursor_sweep, and cursor_tail, plus mode-change shaders like ripple_cursor, sonic_boom_cursor, rectangle_boom_cursor, and ripple_rectangle_cursor. That means this should be treated as a broader cursor-effects surface, not just more colors. Preferred direction: keep Yazelix as the builder and orchestrator, vendor or generate a curated subset of upstream shaders locally, and expose them through a structured selector that supports both existing color presets and new pattern effects. A robust design is to support an ordered list for Ghostty shader selection and composition, with backward-compatible string aliases for existing cursor_trail values. If random remains the default, define a curated random pool explicitly instead of sampling every shader, because pulse and boom effects are behaviorally different from trails and may need separate inclusion rules. Also account for Ghostty-specific caveats such as custom-shader-animation = always for some line-cursor effects, and avoid regressing Kitty fallback behavior that currently maps random to snow.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear implementation decision and scope for integrating ghostty-cursor-shaders into Yazelix. The chosen design keeps shader management inside Yazelix, documents the upstream source and license, and specifies whether the config surface stays terminal.cursor_trail, becomes truly list-capable, or is renamed to a broader cursor-effects concept. The plan identifies which upstream shaders are included, how random selection works, what Ghostty-only caveats must be emitted in generated config, and how existing cursor_trail users remain backward-compatible. Verification includes generating Ghostty config with a fixed preset, a composed or list-based preset if adopted, and the default random path.
close_reason: Implemented Ghostty cursor effect integration with vendored shaders, new Ghostty effect config, generated shader build, and collection/docs updates

title: Experiment: evaluate a Pixi-backed Yazelix branch
description: Evaluate whether Yazelix could support a separate Pixi-backed experiment branch without turning main into a multi-backend runtime. Focus on a narrow prototype: Helix, Yazi, Zellij, Nushell, and core CLI flows such as yzx env, yzx run, and launch/bootstrap. The goal is to determine whether Pixi is credible for a lower-support branch, what major gaps remain compared with the current devenv/Nix model, and whether the branch should exist at all.
design: Treat this as an exploration task, not a commitment to multi-backend support in main. Keep the scope narrow: prototype a separate Pixi-backed branch that covers the core Yazelix stack plus the command slice that still matters after the backend-contract and command-surface work lands. The task should identify what maps cleanly from the current runtime model to Pixi, what becomes unsupported or degraded, and whether the resulting branch would be honest enough to maintain as a lower-support option. Avoid solving generic backend abstraction unless the prototype proves the branch is worth pursuing first.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation on whether Yazelix should maintain a Pixi-backed experiment branch. The result includes: a validated package-availability check for the core toolchain; a clear list of current devenv/Nix assumptions that would need replacement; a small prototype or spike proving the core launch/env/run flow is either viable or not; and a documented capability matrix showing which features would work, degrade, or remain unsupported. Leave behind enough notes and logs that the branch decision is reproducible instead of intuition-driven.

title: Proper agent command center IDE for teams of running agents
description: Create a real agent command-center surface for coordinating multiple running agents across one or more monitors, with fast visibility into agent state and the ability to pop open related tools.
design: Design this as a first-class operations surface rather than a pile of widgets. The core use cases are seeing which agents exist, whether they are idle or active, showing or hiding their panes, opening related terminals or tools, and exposing lightweight usage or health stats. Prefer a coherent workspace dashboard that can scale across monitors and degrade gracefully when some agent integrations are unavailable.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a credible agent command-center direction with a concrete first slice that helps manage multiple running agents. The chosen surface can reveal agent state, toggle or focus the relevant workspace surfaces, and open related tools without feeling bolted on. Add unit tests for state and action routing plus end-to-end validation that logs the visible agents, derived status, and invoked workspace actions.

title: Prepare Steel-mode support for Helix
description: Prepare Yazelix to support Helix's emerging Steel-based plugin system once it is merged upstream or otherwise stable enough to justify first-class support.
design: Keep this future-facing but concrete. The bead covers three possible paths that should be evaluated explicitly: supporting the eventual upstream plugin system in stock Helix, allowing advanced users to point Yazelix at a Helix fork that already ships the Steel plugin runtime, or maintaining a Yazelix-managed fork if that becomes justified. Do not assume the right answer yet. First define the support model, packaging implications, runtime path implications, and how plugin installation and config would fit Yazelix without turning editor support into chaos. Options like pointing a derivation at the plugin author's fork or shipping a Yazelix-branded fork such as neohelix should be treated as tradeoffs to evaluate, not default answers.
acceptance_criteria: The result is a clear adoption plan for Steel-based Helix plugins, not a vague placeholder. It should spell out the supported path, unsupported path, and migration story if upstream Helix merges the system. Any implementation work must include unit tests for config parsing and runtime-path decisions and end-to-end smoke tests that launch the chosen Helix runtime with plugins enabled, with detailed logs showing runtime resolution and plugin load results.

title: Define curated LSP/formatter/linter defaults for every Yazelix-supported language pack
description: Define and document the default LSP, formatter, and linter for every Yazelix-supported language pack so each pack feels like a curated development environment rather than a loose bundle of packages.
design: Keep this opinionated and pack-first. For each supported language pack, decide the default LSP, formatter, and linter Yazelix stands behind, document the reasoning, and make sure the pack actually installs the chosen tools. Avoid turning this into an explosion of alternatives; the point is a clear supported default for each pack.
acceptance_criteria: Every supported language pack has a documented default LSP, formatter, and linter, and the pack implementation matches that documentation. Add unit tests for the pack-to-tool mapping, integration tests for config generation, and end-to-end fixture checks for a representative set of languages, with detailed logs of the resolved tools and launched language-service commands.

title: Experiment: use Nushell job freeze/unfreeze to reuse pane (no-sidebar)
description: Investigate whether Nushell job freeze/unfreeze can be used under the hood to let Helix and Yazi share the same pane in the no_sidebar layout instead of paying the full reopen cost every time.
design: This is an experiment, not a guaranteed feature. The target flow is the no_sidebar layout only: freeze one program, swap in the other, and unfreeze it later so the pane feels reused rather than recreated. Evaluate correctness first: terminal state, shell/editor cleanup, focus behavior, log noise, and what happens when a frozen process crashes or the pane is closed. If the technique is brittle, capture that clearly and reject it rather than forcing it into production.
acceptance_criteria: The bead ends with either a working prototype for no_sidebar or a written rejection based on concrete evidence. If implemented, add unit tests around the state machine that tracks frozen vs active processes, plus e2e coverage for repeated Helix/Yazi swaps, crash/recovery paths, and pane-close behavior with detailed timing and state-transition logs.

title: Yazi: open multiple selected files
description: Let Yazi open multiple selected files into the managed editor workflow instead of forcing one-at-a-time opens when a multi-file edit session is the natural next step.
design: The feature should respect the existing managed editor model rather than bypassing it. Decide how selections map into Helix or other supported editors, how pane reuse and focus behave, and what should happen when the current editor already has an active workspace context. Keep the first version predictable even if that means limiting which editors or selection counts are supported initially.
acceptance_criteria: Users can select multiple files in Yazi and open them through Yazelix in one action. Add unit tests for selection aggregation and editor-command construction helpers, integration tests around selection collection and editor-command construction, and end-to-end coverage for multi-file open behavior with detailed logs showing the selected paths, target pane, and resulting editor invocation.

title: Workspace: keep a stable workspace cwd for Yazelix-managed tabs and pane creation
description: Workspace/Yazi ergonomics sub-item that is already complete.
close_reason: Already shipped

title: yazi opening full white
description: Always-on maintenance lane item that is already closed on GitHub.
close_reason: Already resolved

title: Agent usage widgets in the Zellij widget tray
description: Expose agent-related information in the Zellij widget tray so the AI and agent tooling Yazelix already ships feels like a first-class workspace surface instead of just installed packages.
design: Keep the first version lightweight and read-only. Good candidates are agent availability, default tool family, or compact usage information when the relevant trackers are installed. The widget should degrade cleanly when packs are disabled and must not turn the tray into a polling-heavy telemetry panel.
acceptance_criteria: The widget tray can show a concise agent-related surface when the relevant tooling is present, and it stays quiet when it is not. Add unit tests for widget selection and fallback behavior plus end-to-end validation that logs the detected tool state and final rendered widget set.

title: Docs experience pass
description: Improve the docs experience so users can discover the right guide quickly and the written surface matches the current product instead of feeling like repo archaeology.
design: This is broader than fixing one page. Review navigation, entrypoints, installation flow, customization docs, troubleshooting, and how examples map to the current runtime model. The result should keep docs maintainable while making the first path through Yazelix much easier to follow.
acceptance_criteria: The docs have clearer entrypoints, more coherent structure, and fewer stale or confusing paths. Add unit tests for any generated docs-index or navigation helpers, end-to-end link and command-reference validation scripts for the main docs routes, and a logged checklist covering the examples and entrypoints that were verified.

title: Website launch at yazelix.com
description: Create a thin but credible public website for Yazelix so the project has a clear front door beyond the repository README.
design: Keep the site focused on what Yazelix is, who it is for, how it differs from a plain editor or shell setup, and where new users should go next. This should complement the repo docs instead of competing with them. Favor maintainability and strong messaging over building a complex web app.
acceptance_criteria: yazelix.com becomes a credible first stop for new users, with clear installation and docs entrypoints and visuals that match the current product. Add unit tests for any generated route or content-selection helpers, end-to-end route and outbound-link validation, and detailed logs for the final validated site structure, screenshots, and key flows.

title: Support a separate user-managed devenv file
description: Support a separate user-managed devenv file as a power-user override path without replacing yazelix.toml or the managed runtime model as the default story.
design: This should be a deliberate extension point, not a hole punched through the configuration model. Define what a user-managed devenv overlay can change, how it composes with Yazelix-managed files, and how support boundaries are documented so power users get flexibility without making the default runtime impossible to reason about.
acceptance_criteria: Power users can provide a separate devenv file through a documented, supported path, and the default yazelix.toml flow remains the primary story. Add unit tests for path-resolution and overlay-selection helpers, config and path-resolution integration tests, and end-to-end validation that the override file is discovered and composed correctly, with detailed logs of the final devenv inputs used.

title: Notification system
description: Design and add a notification system that surfaces important Yazelix events without turning the workspace into a noisy status carnival.
design: Do not start with implementation detail. First define the event model: which events deserve a notification, what channel they should use, when they should be silent, and how users can dismiss or tune them. The system should reinforce existing flows such as rebuilds, permission issues, and long-running actions instead of compensating for unclear product behavior with spam.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented notification model and an implementation that surfaces the right events with restrained UX. Add unit tests for event routing and suppression rules plus end-to-end scripts that trigger representative events and log delivery channel, payload, and user-visible outcome.

title: Completed roadmap items
description: Migrated from docs_explore/roadmap.md. Historical roadmap items that are already shipped or otherwise complete.
close_reason: Historical grouping only

title: investigate FlakeHub Cache and magic-nix-cache
description: Investigate whether FlakeHub Cache or magic-nix-cache would materially benefit Yazelix CI or installs.
close_reason: Not planned after investigation: Yazelix already uses magic-nix-cache in CI, and FlakeHub Cache is not justified right now.

title: Define the current Yazelix roadmap around the live v15 line, delete-first cleanup, gradual v15.x Rust insertion, and the v16 Rust release
description: Define the current roadmap for Yazelix now that v15 is the only supported line, config-migration support is being removed, and the release framing is clearer: v15.0 is the trimmed rebooted non-Rust release, later v15.x point releases may gain selective Rust slices where they pay for themselves, and v16 is the Rust-forward release. The roadmap should capture the main pillars that matter from here: tighter subsystem boundaries, deleting stale compatibility layers, top-to-bottom profiling, a clearer front door, selective post-v15.0 Rust insertion, and the longer path toward a much more Rust-heavy v16.
design: Treat this as the roadmap for the current live v15 line, not as a placeholder for a second product and not as a disguised all-at-once rewrite plan. Reuse the real existing lanes instead of creating duplicate implementation work: the runtime/helper trim work, the popup/menu and pane-orchestrator cleanup, the migration-layer deletion, the profiling bead, the website issues, and the Rust planning beads that now describe Rust as a post-v15.0 incremental path rather than something required to ship v15.0 itself. The useful output is a coherent framing for what Yazelix is now, what v15.0 must finish without depending on Rust, what v15.x may still improve incrementally, and what kind of architectural shift is intentionally reserved for v16.
acceptance_criteria: There is a durable roadmap skeleton for the live v15 Yazelix line and its path toward v16. It identifies the main pillars for the current product era, names the delete-first cleanup that still matters before and after v15.0, points at the active Rust/architecture/profiling/website lanes that feed the next stages, and keeps the release framing explicit: v15.0 ships as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, selective Rust slices may land later in v15.x, and v16 is the later Rust-forward release.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Port launch/bootstrap state and backend adapter logic into Rust after the seam is extracted
description: After the backend adapter seam is clearer, and only after v15.0 has shipped, evaluate moving the launch/bootstrap state machine and backend-calling logic out of environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, and related yzx env/run/launch/refresh helpers into a Rust core. The target is safer orchestration and clearer state transitions, not a generic backend framework. This is a candidate only for a later v15.x point release if the seam is truly stable; otherwise it belongs in the broader v16 Rust-forward push.
design: Do not start here and do not treat this as part of shipping v15.0. This is a later slice because it mixes process spawning, environment shaping, state reuse, and user-facing behavior. Only move it once the backend contract and command-surface audit have made the boundary explicit and after the earlier Rust slices have proven the integration strategy. A likely good outcome is a Rust core that owns the state machine and structured actions while Nushell stays as a thin CLI and host-integration layer. If the boundary still feels porous after the earlier work, defer this slice to v16 instead of forcing it into v15.x.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete migration plan for the launch/bootstrap/backend adapter path that respects the extracted backend seam and does not re-entangle product semantics. The plan identifies which Nushell modules become shims or are removed, how parity will be verified for env/run/refresh/launch behavior, why this slice is worth doing after the earlier ones, and whether it is realistically safe for a later v15.x point release or should wait for the broader v16 push.
notes:
Primary target modules: environment_bootstrap.nu, launch_state.nu, yzx/env.nu, yzx/run.nu, yzx/launch.nu, and any helper code that still encodes backend-specific state transitions.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- Treat `yazelix-m5rk.1` as the concrete precondition that makes the runtime state machine explicit before this slice moves into Rust.

title: Decide which config generators should stay Nushell versus move to Rust
description: Evaluate the major generated-config surfaces such as yazi_config_merger.nu, zellij_config_merger.nu, terminal_configs.nu, and related template helpers to decide whether they are good Rust targets or should stay in Nushell. The goal is to avoid rewriting text-heavy, low-risk template glue just because v16 will be Rust-forward, while still identifying any generator slice that would genuinely benefit from stronger modeling. v15.0 should ship without Rust becoming the owner of these surfaces; any generator move would be a later v15.x or v16 decision.
design: Use the migration matrix to separate structured generator logic from simple template stitching. Favor keeping straightforward text/template assembly in Nushell unless there is repeated correctness pain, complex state reconciliation, or a clear typed model that would become substantially safer in Rust. Do not let this bead become a license to port all generators indiscriminately just because Rust is on the roadmap or because v16 is intended to be Rust-forward. The default should be to keep v15.0 and probably much of v15.x generator ownership in Nushell unless a narrower structured subcore proves worth extracting.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear keep-versus-port recommendation for the main config generators, with concrete reasons tied to defect history, complexity, and expected payoff. The output identifies any generator slice worth porting, explicitly names the ones that should remain Nushell, and states honestly whether any surviving Rust candidate belongs in a later v15.x point release or should wait for v16.
notes:
Likely keep-in-Nushell candidates today: zellij_config_merger.nu, yazi_config_merger.nu, and much of terminal_configs.nu unless a narrower structured core emerges that is worth extracting.

Sequencing note after the 2026-04-05 architecture review:
- `yazelix-jheg` is the likely non-Rust improvement path for `zellij_config_merger.nu`; if that typed render-pipeline work lands first, reevaluate whether any narrower structured subcore is still worth porting instead of assuming the whole generator should move.

title: Narrow backend comparison issue into a Pixi-first exploration lane
description: GitHub issue #518 asked for a broad backend comparison across devenv, mise, brioche, pixi, and prefix. That framing mixed tools from different layers. The outcome is to close the broad issue and keep the real exploration scoped to a Pixi-first experiment branch instead of a generic multi-backend bakeoff.
close_reason: Closed in favor of the narrower Pixi-first evaluation lane tracked separately in yazelix-qow.

title: Postmortem: session-global Yazi sidebar state caused cross-tab cwd leaks
description: Symptom: yzx cwd appeared to change the cwd of other tabs, and the same class of issue was suspected around Yazi-driven path changes. Initial suspicion was a pane-orchestrator tab-isolation leak. Actual cause: read_active_sidebar_state in nushell/scripts/integrations/yazi.nu selected the most recently modified Yazi state file for the whole session, so sidebar sync could target another tab Yazi pane. Because every tab starts with Yazi, this produced a real cross-tab leak in normal usage. Fix: resolve the current tab sidebar_pane_id via debug_editor_state, then read that exact sidebar state file. Verified in a disposable two-tab Yazelix session.
design: Keep this as a regression postmortem rather than implementation work. The value is preserving the causal chain: session-global sidebar state looked convenient, but it violated tab locality and produced real cross-tab cwd leaks. Capture the invariant that any sidebar or path-sync state must be keyed to the current tab or pane identity rather than global recency.
acceptance_criteria: The bead clearly documents the regression cause, the violated tab-locality invariant, the final fix, and the guardrail future work should preserve. A maintainer touching sidebar state later can use this bead to avoid reintroducing session-global leaks.

title: Auto-assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer
description: Automatically assign new GitHub issues to the maintainer instead of leaving assignment as a manual follow-up step.
design: Treat this as repository workflow automation, not product runtime work. Decide whether GitHub Actions, issue templates, or another automation path is the right place to assign newly opened issues to the maintainer, and verify the permission model before committing to an approach. The solution should be reliable without creating noisy side effects on transferred, reopened, or bot-created issues unless that behavior is explicitly desired.
acceptance_criteria: New issues are assigned to the intended maintainer automatically, or the repo has a documented explanation of why that automation is not supportable. Include validation for the chosen workflow path, with logs or dry-run evidence showing the assignment behavior for representative issue-opening scenarios.

title: yzx wallpaper / screensaver
description: Explore whether Yazelix should ship a branded wallpaper and/or screensaver path instead of leaving the idea as a vague aesthetic request.
design: Keep this explicitly low-priority and opt-in. First decide what problem this would solve: branding, delight, showcase visuals, or an idle-state experience tied to Yazelix sessions. Distinguish between static wallpapers, lightweight animated assets, and an actual screensaver workflow, and avoid introducing platform-specific complexity unless the user value is real.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear decision on whether Yazelix should ship wallpapers, a screensaver path, both, or neither. If something is implemented, it is lightweight, optional, and documented with the intended use case and platform caveats. Leave behind validated assets or a documented rejection, plus any generation or preview commands needed to reproduce the result.

title: Support Emacs as a low-priority compatibility target
description: Keep Emacs support on the backlog mainly so Yazelix continues to cover the three big model editors, not because this is a current priority area.
design: This should stay aspirational for now. When revisited, verify the modes people actually use, especially Doom Emacs and similar real-world setups, and evaluate whether tools like chemacs2 help keep integration manageable. Do not let this expand into a broad editor-support project before higher-value workspace and packaging work is done.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has a documented path or verified compatibility story for mainstream Emacs setups when this eventually gets picked up. Until then, this should remain clearly marked as low-priority backlog work rather than active roadmap pressure.

title: Document and validate Zellij session sharing with Yazelix
description: Investigate how Zellij session sharing works in practice for Yazelix users, including whether Yazelix makes the process easier, harder, or just different compared with vanilla Zellij. The main sharing paths to verify are Zellij's built-in web client and ordinary attach/share flows for existing sessions.
design: Start as a docs-and-validation task grounded in the official Zellij sharing model. Zellij now has a built-in web server and web client that can be started with `zellij web` or through the share plugin, and authenticated with one-time login tokens. The default local URL scheme and token flow should be verified in a Yazelix session. Also verify ordinary multi-client session attach flows and session resurrection/share behavior. Then check how Yazelix interacts with these flows: shared Yazelix environments, shared devenv profiles, plugin/runtime expectations, persistent session naming, and whether the current keybinding model makes the share plugin discoverable enough. If Yazelix creates friction, consider small helper behavior or targeted docs from the Yazelix side to smooth it out.
acceptance_criteria: There is accurate documentation for sharing Zellij sessions with Yazelix, grounded in verified behavior rather than guesswork. Any Yazelix-specific helper or caveat is documented clearly. Leave behind a repeatable validation script or checklist with detailed logs covering the tested sharing path, authentication or web-client steps, and any plugin or session caveats that users need to know.
notes: Official Zellij sources to anchor this work: Web client tutorial, web client user guide, and the 0.43.0 release notes about browser sharing. The docs note that the web client is useful for remote access, demos, pair programming, and collaborative debugging. They also recommend HTTPS for anything beyond local-only use.

title: Update static preview assets (screenshots/gifs)
description: Refresh Yazelix's static preview assets so screenshots and GIFs reflect the current product instead of stale layouts, widgets, and onboarding states.
design: Treat this as a content-quality pass tied to the current runtime, not just regenerating everything blindly. Inventory where preview assets are used across README, docs, and the website, replace the stale ones, and prefer smaller, more focused captures that survive UI evolution better.
acceptance_criteria: The main docs and marketing surfaces show current Yazelix visuals, stale captures are removed, and the asset set is small enough to maintain. Include a logged capture checklist so the asset generation process is reproducible instead of ad hoc.
close_reason: Reduced repo preview assets to one maintained README screenshot, removed stale head demos, and documented the lightweight capture policy

title: Figure out how to install Yazelix in an existing app on Android
description: Figure out whether Yazelix can be installed inside an existing Android app environment and what the credible support story would look like if that path is worth pursuing.
design: This is an exploration bead, not a promise of a mobile app. Focus on realistic hosts such as Termux or other existing app shells that can run the needed Nix or terminal stack, and identify what would break first: rendering, keyboard input, terminal multiplexing, install size, or package assumptions. The result should distinguish technically possible from supportable.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concrete recommendation about whether Android-in-an-existing-app is viable, what the supported host would be, and what major caveats remain. Leave behind a documented validation path and detailed logs of the tested environment so the conclusion is reproducible.

title: Filter large GIFs from git history
description: Remove oversized GIF history from the repository in a deliberate way so the repo is lighter to clone without breaking the current docs and preview experience.
design: This is history hygiene, not just deleting files from HEAD. Identify which demo GIF blobs are disproportionately large, decide what should stay in git versus move to releases or external hosting, and plan the rewrite carefully so active docs, screenshots, and README references still work afterward. Document the migration and force-push implications before rewriting history.
acceptance_criteria: Large legacy GIF blobs are removed from git history, the current repo still has working preview assets, and contributors have a clear migration note if history rewriting is required. Include a verification script or logged before-and-after measurements for repo size, clone size, and asset reference integrity.

title: Split demo GIFs into smaller pieces
description: Split the large demo GIF story into several smaller focused demos so users can understand specific Yazelix workflows without one heavyweight clip doing all the work.
design: The goal is not just smaller files. Each demo should communicate one idea clearly: onboarding, Yazi plus Helix flow, workspace switching, and so on. Coordinate with the preview-asset and git-history cleanup beads so the final asset set is coherent and lightweight.
acceptance_criteria: The docs and website use smaller focused demo assets instead of a single oversized catch-all GIF. Asset file sizes, placement, and narrative purpose are documented in a capture checklist or logged plan so the set remains maintainable.

title: Windows support: verify native and WSL paths and document caveats
description: Re-evaluate what level of Windows support Yazelix can credibly claim now that Zellij supports Windows, and document the caveats honestly instead of leaving the answer implicit.
design: Treat this as a support-boundary verification task, not a promise that native Windows is automatically first-class. Validate both realistic paths: native Windows, now that Zellij supports it, and WSL as an alternative/fallback path. Check the core flows that matter: install, launch, terminal integration, shell/path handling, clipboard and browser/file openers, fonts, file-manager interop, plugin/runtime caveats, and any assumptions in Yazelix that still depend on Unix-only behavior. The output should separate supported, works-with-caveats, and unsupported cases for each path.
acceptance_criteria: Users can tell whether Yazelix is a credible native-Windows option, a WSL-only option, or a mixed-support story with specific caveats. The docs include concrete caveats and recovery tips for both native Windows and WSL where relevant, and the validation process leaves behind a logged checklist or script so support can be re-verified later as Zellij and Yazelix evolve.

title: Benchmark: clean install using Nix (cold/warm)
description: Benchmark clean Yazelix installation and first-run behavior under Nix so the project can talk concretely about cold and warm setup costs instead of guessing.
design: Measure the path users actually care about: cold install, warm install, first launch, and any major cache effects. Capture the environment, cache state, hardware notes, and the exact commands used so the numbers remain meaningful instead of becoming one-off anecdotes. The README or docs update should present the results honestly rather than as marketing theater.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix has reproducible cold and warm install benchmarks that can be referenced in docs. Add unit tests for any benchmark parsing or summarization helpers, end-to-end benchmark scripts or documented procedures with detailed logs for timing, cache state, and machine context, and update the relevant docs or README with the measured results.

title: Sidebar toggle/orchestration shipped in v13
description: Completed roadmap item. Deterministic sidebar toggle, editor/sidebar focus, and managed editor targeting are shipped in v13.
close_reason: Shipped in v13

title: Packaging: prepare and submit a nixpkgs package for Yazelix
description: Prepare Yazelix for nixpkgs submission once the runtime and packaging boundaries are clean enough that the remaining work is primarily packaging and review.
design: This is a distribution milestone, not a hidden architecture refactor. Define the package shape, wrapper behavior, runtime assets, and installation story expected by nixpkgs reviewers after the package-ready path model exists. Be explicit about Linux and macOS implications, update strategy, and what remains outside scope for a first nixpkgs submission.
acceptance_criteria: Yazelix reaches a state where a nixpkgs submission is mostly packaging work. Add unit tests for package metadata or wrapper-generation helpers where applicable, packaging smoke tests that build and run the wrapped entrypoints, and logged validation of install paths, runtime asset availability, and user-facing launch behavior.
notes: Upstream nixpkgs submission is no longer a planned project goal. Keep the completed contract and local package work as historical reference, but do not treat upstream submission as a downstream milestone for the current package-runtime-first simplification lane.

title: Evaluate a broader Rust/clap rewrite as a v16-or-later path after boundaries are proven
description: Keep the possibility of a broader Rust or clap rewrite on the table as a v16-or-later path, but only after the current v15.0 product, runtime model, and UX seams are proven well enough that a rewrite would clarify rather than scramble the system.
design: This remains intentionally late and skeptical. A broader Rust or clap rewrite should not be a reflexive response to complexity that really comes from fuzzy product semantics. Revisit it only after v15.0 has shipped as the trimmed non-Rust reboot, the runtime boundaries are proven, the Rust migration matrix exists, and the Rust/Nushell bridge strategy is explicit. The default assumption for v15.x should be incremental Rust helpers, not an immediate whole-CLI rewrite; v16 is the earliest release framing where a much broader Rust/clap move should become a visible product direction.
acceptance_criteria: There is a clear justification document for any future broader Rust/clap rewrite path, including what problems it would solve, what parity must be preserved, whether it belongs in v16 or later, and what benchmarks or maintainability wins would justify the cost. Any eventual implementation must come with unit tests for rewritten helper logic, end-to-end parity comparison scripts against the current behavior, and detailed logs showing what changed and what remained equivalent.

title: Packaging and distribution umbrella
description: Umbrella for the remaining packaging and distribution coordination work. Keep this small and planning-focused while concrete child issues track the real implementation work such as nixpkgs packaging and any packaging-surface consolidation follow-ons.
design: Keep this as the packaging and distribution coordination layer, not as another implementation issue. The children should capture the concrete work: Home Manager alignment, flake interface, and nixpkgs packaging. Use real blockers only where one packaging path truly depends on another.
acceptance_criteria: The packaging follow-on work is represented by concrete child beads with honest dependencies and clear deliverables. This umbrella stays small and planning-focused instead of accumulating implementation detail.
close_reason: Umbrella no longer needed; remaining public packaging work continues in #402 and follow-on tasks stay separate

title: Workspace UX and Yazi ergonomics
description: Umbrella for the workspace-model and Yazi ergonomics work that makes Yazelix feel more coherent and less fiddly in day-to-day use.
design: This cluster is about the lived workspace experience: keybindings, Yazi-to-editor transitions, pane cwd behavior, status surfaces, and other small seams that shape whether the environment feels magical or awkward. Keep the child beads concrete and user-facing, and avoid using this umbrella as a dumping ground for unrelated UI tweaks.
acceptance_criteria: The workspace-UX child beads cover the main friction points without duplicating each other, and their dependencies reflect real interaction-model prerequisites where those exist. The umbrella itself remains a coordination bead, not an execution target.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Main roadmap sequence
description: Top-level roadmap track for the major product and packaging work that should shape Yazelix after the current stabilization pass.
design: This bead is a planning container, not a feature to implement directly. Its job is to keep the major streams visible without forcing fake blocker edges between unrelated work. The child beads under this epic should use real technical dependencies where they exist and otherwise remain parallelizable so Beads triage stays honest.
acceptance_criteria: The main roadmap children are well-scoped, their blockers reflect actual prerequisites rather than aesthetic ordering, and the graph remains usable for bv triage. Revisit this epic whenever major roadmap themes change rather than stuffing execution detail into the container itself.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies

title: Always-on maintenance lane
description: Always-on maintenance lane for cross-cutting bug fixes, cleanup, and reliability improvements that should continue even while the larger roadmap moves forward.
design: This is the place for real ongoing maintenance work, not an excuse to bury urgent bugs under a generic label. Use it for issues that improve the product continuously without pretending they are the next big milestone. Keep the children concrete and user-impactful so the lane stays actionable.
acceptance_criteria: Maintenance-lane children are clearly described, independently actionable, and not hidden behind fake sequence blockers. The lane remains useful for triage and does not become a miscellaneous junk drawer.
close_reason: Obsolete grouping container; replaced by labels and direct dependencies
